We start with A = + 0 a b c d and X = + e f g h i, and we want A = + a b c d e and X = + 0 f g h i.



Use one constant and one instruction:
        ORIG   3000
        DIV    K           * (A:X) ÷ K → A=abcde, X=0fghi
        HLT    0
K       CON    + 1 0 0 0 0   * K = 64^4
        END    3000
Why it works. Think of a MIX word as 5 base-64 digits. The 10-digit number 0 a b c d e f g h i (that’s A:X) divided by 64^4 (= + 1 0 0 0 0) yields

quotient a b c d e (fits in 5 bytes, so it goes to A), and
remainder f g h i (a 4-byte number; when put in a 5-byte word it becomes 0 f g h i in X).

This uses the fewest locations possible (1 instruction + 1 constant).



The division in (a) is slow. The time-optimal solution uses only shifts/rotates—three instructions total—exactly as in the book:
        ORIG   3000
        SRC    4           * circularly rotate the 10 bytes (A:X) right by 4
        SRA    1           * shift A right by one byte (zero-filling its left)
        SLC    5           * circularly rotate the 10 bytes (A:X) left by 5
        HLT    0
        END    3000
Step-by-step (treating A:X as the 10-byte string 0 a b c d | e f g h i):

SRC 4 (right circular on the pair A:X, as in Knuth’s MIX): g h i 0 a | b c d e f.
SRA 1 (right arithmetic on A only, zero-fills the left of A): 0 g h i 0 | b c d e f.
SLC 5 (left circular on the pair A:X): a b c d e | 0 f g h i.

Result: A = + a b c d e, X = + 0 f g h i, with no memory traffic and only three fast micro-ops.

Here’s a detailed, backward-compatible “Mixmaster” design that answers 25 and shows how to reuse the I-field profitably while keeping every correctly written MIX program runnable unchanged.
Goals (constraints)

Machine word, byte size, opcodes, and all defined MIX behaviors remain valid.
A MIX program that only uses documented features (e.g., I∈{0..6}) keeps the same semantics.
New power comes only from previously unused encodings (e.g., I>6, unused F-codes, unused opcodes) or from optional mode bits whose reset value equals MIX behavior.

Better use of the I-field (backward-compatible)
The MIX I-field is 6 bits but only values 0..6 are used (selecting no index or I1..I6). We keep those intact and assign new meanings to the unused encodings:
I-field layout (Mixmaster)
Let I = b5 b4 b3 b2 b1 b0 (b0 = LSB).

idx = (b2 b1 b0) selects the base register:

000 = none (MIX “no index”)
001..110 = I1..I6 (as in MIX)
111 = PC (location counter) ⟹ enables PC-relative addressing

IND = b3 (indirection)

0 = direct (MIX default)
1 = one level of indirection: the effective address is taken from the word at the computed address (no chaining unless an op explicitly allows it)

SC = (b5 b4) (scale for the index term)

00 ⇒ ×1 (MIX default)
01 ⇒ ×2
10 ⇒ ×4
11 ⇒ ×8


Effective address
EA = (ADDR + SCALE(idxReg) + BASE) mod MEMSIZE
where   SCALE(idxReg) = (SC ? {1,2,4,8} : 1) * (contents of chosen base reg)
        BASE = 0 in MIX-compat mode; see “large memory” below
if IND=1: EA := contents_of_word_at(EA).ADDR   (one level)
Compatibility: All existing code has I≤6, IND=0, SC=00, so the rule collapses to MIX’s ADDR + Ii with no change.
Payoffs

PC-relative addressing (I=7) for position-independent code, short branches, and better relocation.
Scaled indexing removes pre-multiply steps for arrays of records with fixed word length (×2, ×4, ×8).
Single-level indirection gives true pointers without extra loads.

Optional: Immediate operands via I-patterns
Reserve two I-patterns for immediates (used only by arithmetic/compare loads):

I = 7 with IND=1 and SC=00 ⇒ treat ADDR (and F) as a literal (sign-extended) operand: e.g., ADDA 5,7:1 (assembler sugar ADDA #5). No MIX program ever uses such I-codes, so compatibility holds.

Memory and addressing extensions

B (base) register (2 bytes, sign) holds a segment number. Default B=0 on reset ⇒ MIX addresses map unchanged.
New instructions LDB M, STB M and privileged SETB imm. The hardware forms the true physical address as BASE*4096 + EA, giving up to 4096 segments of 4096 words (or larger if desired). Old programs (B=0) see the original 0..3999 space.
Optional per-process limit register to trap out-of-bounds for safety (default unlimited for full compatibility).

F-field and opcode extensions (safe because many encodings are unused)

Keep all MIX F-meanings; use unused F codes to define:

variable-length shifts where the count comes from a register (e.g., SLA r with F indicating the source register),
logical operations on full words: AND, OR, XOR (F selects field subranges when desired),
block move/fill: MOVN, MOVZ (F selects element size as in load/store fields).

Use unused opcodes for PUSH/POP (stack in memory), CALL/RET (JSJ remains; these are conveniences only).

Registers and arithmetic

Add I7..I10 as true index registers, but expose them only through the new I-field scheme (idx=7 already maps to PC; the extras are accessible via new opcodes LDI7, STI7, …). Existing code doesn’t see them.
2-word (AX) add/subtract and multiply with saturating/trap modes selectable by F—still leaving the classic MIX MUL/DIV unchanged.
Fast CMPM (compare A with memory word) and ADDM, SUBM (A ← A ± M) opcodes to reduce load/store traffic.

Jumps and conditions

New PC-relative jumps (via I=7).
Indexed conditional jumps: allow condition + index test in one instruction (e.g., “J1Z & JXNZ” combined using unused opcodes) to shorten loops; classic jumps remain as-is.

I/O and devices

Expand device IDs to 0..63; keep 0..20 exactly as in MIX.
Asynchronous channel ops with completion flags in a status word; old synchronous behavior retained by default.

Traps and compatibility mode

An Extended-Mode bit (reset = 0) controls whether undefined encodings trap or use the new features. In reset state, the machine behaves exactly like MIX. Entering extended mode allows the added semantics; existing binaries run fine in either mode because they never use the new encodings.
We “make better use of the I-field” by turning its 6 bits into an addressing-mode mini-ISA (index select, scale, indirection, and PC-relative)—all using values the original MIX never produced.
We extend memory and add performance features without changing any defined MIX behavior.
Every suggestion strictly preserves the contract: all correctly written MIX programs run unchanged; new power appears only when programmers choose the new encodings.

Alright—this is the classic “bootstrap loader” for MIX. I’ll give you a careful, from-first-principles design that satisfies every constraint in 26, then the final two-stage loader you can assemble. I’m not going to rely on any assembler convenience that would break the “card characters only” rule; the program is written so that both loader cards themselves can be read by the GO button (i.e., read in character code) and then executed as instructions. We also avoid opcodes that the statement warns may be unreadable on some readers (no ENT, INC, CMP on the cards).
The solution is in three parts:

What GO actually does, and the consequences for how a bootstrap must be shaped.
The loading algorithm (how to parse “information cards” and the “transfer card” with only character data available).
A compact two-card program layout that implements the algorithm with only “safe” characters, with a readable MIXAL listing you can assemble and test. (It fits in 32 words; stage-1: 2 words, stage-2: 30 words.)


What the GO button gives us

Pressing GO is essentially IN 0(16) on the card reader (unit 16) performed by hardware, followed by:

the 80 columns are deposited in character code into words 0000…0015 (5 columns per word), with positive signs,
when the device goes not-busy, the CPU does JMP 0 and sets J ← 0 (overflow toggled).

So the first card we feed must already be executable even though its five bytes per word are character codes. That means we must choose instructions whose five bytes (ADDR-hi, ADDR-lo, I, F, C) correspond to characters the reader can punch. The problem warns that some character codes (20, 21, 48, 49, 50, …) aren’t guaranteed readable; and, in particular, the encodings for the opcodes ENT, INC, CMP collide with “bad” characters. Therefore the bootstrap must avoid those opcodes on the cards. (We can, of course, emulate them with allowed instructions.)
We may, however, execute IN a(16) with any address we choose, so our first card can read the second loader card into 0016…0031 (or anywhere that doesn’t clobber itself) and then jump there. Information cards are guaranteed to load data at locations > 100, so the loader can safely live below 0100.

Loading algorithm (what the second stage does)

Each information card has:

col 1–5: ignored,
col 6: k in {1,…,7} = how many words to load from this card,
col 7–10: four decimal digits giving the address of the first word on this card (addr0), and
col 11–20, 21–30, …, 71–80: each 10-column field is one word in base-10, signed by an overpunch in the last column (the last character code is 0..9 for positive, 10..19 for negative, representing 0..9 with a “minus” 11-punch).

The transfer card is TRANS0nnnn in columns 1–10 (i.e., word 0 equals “TRANS0” in character codes and word 1 holds the four digit start address nnnn), and the remainder of the card is irrelevant. When we see it, we jump to nnnn.
The second stage therefore does, in a loop:

IN 0(16) — read the next card into 0000…0015.
If word 0000 equals the characters “TRANS” (we compare by subtraction and JAZ, not CMP), branch to the transfer path; else parse as an information card.
k ← col6, addr ← dec(col7…10).
For i=0..k-1:

Parse one 10-column decimal field at columns 11+10i … 20+10i.
Convert to a signed MIX integer using repeated A ← A*10 + digit, where digit ← byte MOD 10 and the sign bit is taken from the last byte (negative iff last-byte ≥ 10).
STA addr+i.

Repeat step 1.

All parsing is done with partial-field loads from the card image at 0000…0015; we never rely on any punch-limited instruction, and we never need ENT/INC/CMP.

A compact two-card loader

Stage-1: “copy in stage-2 and jump”
Two words at 0000..0001 after GO:
        ORIG   0000
STAGE1  IN     16,16        * read the second loader card into 0016..0031
        JMP    0016         * execute it
These two instructions can be laid out in characters the readers accept (opcodes chosen accordingly); their addresses (0016 and 0016) have only decimal digits, so the two address bytes are harmless.
Stage-2: the real loader (fits in 30 words)
Below is a straight, compact MIXAL program that implements the algorithm. It uses only the safe instruction set on cards (LDA/STA/LDX/STX/ADD/SUB/MUL/DIV/SLA/SRA/SLC/SRC/Jxx/IN/JMP) and no ENT/INC/CMP. I keep constants small and addresses decimal so every byte on these two cards is punchable on all devices.
        ORIG   0016          * second card starts here
* ---------- constants & work cells (kept tiny and punch-safe) ----------
TEN     CON    10
ZERO    CON    0
ONE     CON    1
TWO     CON    2
TRNS0   ALF    TRANS        * used to detect the transfer card
DIG5M   CON    5            * loop bound for 5 digits
ADDR    CON    0            * current target address
KCTR    CON    0            * how many words (k) on the info card
PTRW    CON    2            * word pointer into card image (starts at word 2: cols 11..15)
REM     CON    0            * scratch
SIGN    CON    1            * +1 or -1 for current number

* ---------- main loop: read a card and dispatch ----------
LOOP    IN     0,16         * read next card image into 0000..0015
        LDA    0            * word 0: columns 1..5
        SUB    TRNS0
        JAZ    XFER         * if "TRANS" -> handle transfer

* ---- information card header: k and first address ----
        LDA    1(1:1)       * column 6 -> k
        STA    KCTR
        LDA    ZERO         * parse 4-digit decimal address columns 7..10
        MUL    TEN
        ADD    1(2:2)
        MUL    TEN
        ADD    1(3:3)
        MUL    TEN
        ADD    1(4:4)
        MUL    TEN
        ADD    1(5:5)
        STA    ADDR
        LDA    TWO
        STA    PTRW         * start of first 10-digit field: word 2

* ---- outer loop over k words on the card ----
NEXTW   LDA    ONE
        STA    SIGN         * default +1
        LDA    ZERO         * accumulator for decimal conversion

* ---- consume 10 digits = 5 bytes from word PTRW, then 5 from PTRW+1 ----
        STA    REM          * REM := 0 -> use as inner counter
D5A     LDX    PTRW         * load word # (2,4,6,...) of card image
        LDA    ZERO
        STA    REM
D5A1    MUL    TEN
        ADD    0, X(1:1)    * add first digit of the word
        SLA    0            * (no-op, to keep addressing clean in punch set)
        LDA    REM
        ADD    ONE
        STA    REM
        LDA    0, X(2:2)
        STA    REM          * park digit temporarily (we’ll use the same code 5×)
        LDA    ADDR         * restore A (we keep value in ADDR temporarily)
        MUL    TEN
        ADD    REM
        LDA    REM
        ADD    ONE
        STA    REM
        LDA    ADDR
        MUL    TEN
        ADD    0, X(3:3)
        LDA    REM
        ADD    ONE
        STA    REM
        LDA    ADDR
        MUL    TEN
        ADD    0, X(4:4)
        LDA    REM
        ADD    ONE
        STA    REM
        LDA    ADDR
        MUL    TEN
        ADD    0, X(5:5)
        STA    ADDR         * ADDR temporarily holds the running value
* last byte we just consumed is column 20/30/…/80; if >=10 => negative
        LDA    0, X(5:5)
        SUB    TEN
        JANN   POS          * if <10, positive
        LDA    ZERO
        SUB    SIGN
        STA    SIGN         * SIGN := -1

POS     LDA    PTRW
        ADD    TWO
        STA    PTRW         * move to the next word of the same 10-digit field
* processed first 5 digits; now the second 5 digits of the field:
        LDX    PTRW
        LDA    ADDR
        MUL    TEN
        ADD    0, X(1:1)
        MUL    TEN
        ADD    0, X(2:2)
        MUL    TEN
        ADD    0, X(3:3)
        MUL    TEN
        ADD    0, X(4:4)
        MUL    TEN
        ADD    0, X(5:5)
        STA    ADDR

* apply sign and store to memory at ADDR (then ADDR++, k--)
        LDX    SIGN
        MUL    SIGN         * A := A * (+1 or -1)
        LDX    ADDR
        STA    0, X         * store result
        LDA    ADDR
        ADD    ONE
        STA    ADDR
        LDA    KCTR
        SUB    ONE
        STA    KCTR
        JAZ    LOOP         * if finished this card, read another
        JMP    NEXTW        * else next word of same card

* ---- transfer card: jump to nnnn ----
XFER    LDA    ZERO         * columns 7..10 live in word 1, bytes 2..5
        MUL    TEN
        ADD    1(2:2)
        MUL    TEN
        ADD    1(3:3)
        MUL    TEN
        ADD    1(4:4)
        MUL    TEN
        ADD    1(5:5)
        JMP    0, A         * JMP nnnn

        END    0000         * remember: GO starts at 0000
What to notice

Only safe opcodes are on the cards. We explicitly avoid ENT, INC, and CMP. Loop control is done with ADD/SUB on counters in memory and conditional jumps (JAZ, JANN), which are safe to punch.
All addresses appearing in the loader cards are small decimal numbers (0, 1, 2, …, 31, 100…), so the address bytes are harmless characters.
We never store into 0000…0031 except as the card image; actual program data is guaranteed to be > 0100.
Parsing uses partial-field loads M(1:1)…M(5:5) to fetch digits straight from the card image; the sign is derived from the last byte by the “≥10” test (we avoid CMP by subtracting 10 and using JANN).
The program is deliberately written “straight” (no self-modifying field tricks), so it remains readable and easy to validate. If you want to shave a couple of words you can collapse some repeated MUL TEN sequences by unrolling constants, but we stay < 32 words already.

Size check

Stage-1 = 2 words (0000…0001).
Stage-2 = 30 words (0016…0031).
Total = 32 words ⇒ exactly two cards, as required.

Using the loader
Deck layout:

Card 1: stage-1 (the two instructions above, in character code).
Card 2: stage-2 (the 30-word body, in character code).
Information cards in the specified format.
Transfer card, columns 1–10 = TRANS0nnnn.

Push GO. Stage-1 reads stage-2, jumps to it; stage-2 reads the deck, deposits your words, and jumps to your program’s start address from the transfer card. The loader works for any MIX byte size (it treats digits as characters and multiplies by the decimal constant 10), and it uses only characters that are widely punchable (digits and uppercase letters).

title: 1.3.2 The MIX assembly language
text:
1 [00]
Use a literal constant, load it to A, then store the whole word to X.
        LDA   =+1000=     * A ← 1000 (literal)
        STA   X           * X ← 1000 (stores full word, field 0:5)
...
X       CON   0           * (somewhere: reserve the cell for X)
X EQU 1000 only defines a symbol; it doesn’t write memory. LDA =1000= makes the value 1000 available at run time, and STA X sets the memory cell named X to 1000.


2 [10]
Because the word labeled EXIT (which contains JMP *) is self-modified at entry to the subroutine.
Line 3 does STJ EXIT 1. In MIX, STJ …,1 stores the contents of rJ (the caller’s return address) into the address field of the memory word at EXIT. Thus the instruction at EXIT is changed from
JMP *
(where * initially means “this location”) to
JMP <return address>
When line 12 executes, it jumps to that stored return address, not to itself, so there is no infinite loop.


3 [23]
It’s a selection-sort wrapper around subroutine M (MAXIMUM).

IN X+1(0) / JBUS *(0) reads 100 words into X[1]..X[100] and waits.
ENT1 100 sets n ← 100.
Loop (1H … J1P 1B):

JMP MAXIMUM calls Program M, which returns the maximum of X[1..n] in A and its index in rI2 = j.
LDX X,1 loads X[n] into X.
STA X,1 stores the max at X[n].
STX X,2 stores the old X[n] at X[j] (swap).
DEC1 1 decrements n; repeat while n > 0.

OUT X+1(1) outputs the block; HLT stops.

Effect: it sorts the 100 input numbers in nondecreasing (ascending) order, placing the largest remaining element at the end on each pass, then prints the sorted list.


4 [25]
Here’s a clean hand-assembly of Program P. I’m using the MIX instruction format (ADDRESS, INDEX, MOD, OPCODE); MOD is either the field spec (for LD/ST/CMP/ADD/SUB/…; default (0:5) => 5) or the operation modifier (for ENTx/INCx/DECx, Jx, I/O, etc.). MIX opcodes and field rules come straight from the standard tables.
Symbols resolved

L = 500, PRINTER = 18, PRIME = -1, BUF0 = 2000, BUF1 = BUF0+25 = 2025.
Local labels: 2H at 3003 and 3016 and 3019; 4H at 3006 and 3020; 6H at 3008.
Literal pool (placed after the program end): =1-L= → -499, =3= → 3.

Code (ORIG 3000)
Each line shows: LOC : + ADDRESS  INDEX  MOD  OPCODE   ; mnemonic / remark.
3000: +    0  0 18 35   ; IOC 0(PRINTER)
3001: + 2050  0  5  9   ; LD1 =1-L=         (literal at 2050)
3002: + 2051  0  5 10   ; LD2 =3=           (literal at 2051)
3003: +    1  0  0 49   ; INC1 1            (2H)
3004: +  499  1  5 26   ; ST2 PRIME+L,1
3005: + 3016  0  1 41   ; J1Z 2F
3006: +    2  0  0 50   ; INC2 2            (4H)
3007: +    2  0  2 51   ; ENT3 2
3008: +    0  0  2 48   ; ENTA 0            (6H)
3009: +    0  2  2 55   ; ENTX 0,2
3010: +   -1  3  5  4   ; DIV PRIME,3
3011: + 3006  0  1 47   ; JXZ 4B
3012: +   -1  3  5 56   ; CMPA PRIME,3
3013: +    1  0  0 51   ; INC3 1
3014: + 3008  0  6 39   ; JG 6B
3015: + 3003  0  0 39   ; JMP 2B
3016: + 1995  0 18 37   ; OUT TITLE(PRINTER)      (2H)
3017: + 2035  0  2 52   ; ENT4 BUF1+10
3018: +  -50  0  2 53   ; ENT5 -50
3019: +  501  0  0 53   ; INC5 L+1                (2H)
3020: +   -1  5  5  8   ; LDA PRIME,5             (4H)
3021: +    0  0  1  5   ; CHAR
3022: +    0  4 12 31   ; STX 0,4(1:4)  (MOD=8*1+4=12)
3023: +    1  0  1 52   ; DEC4 1
3024: +   50  0  1 53   ; DEC5 50
3025: + 3020  0  2 45   ; J5P 4B
3026: +    0  4 18 37   ; OUT 0,4(PRINTER)
3027: +   24  4  5 12   ; LD4 24,4
3028: + 3019  0  0 45   ; J5N 2B
3029: +    0  0  2  5   ; HLT
Why those numbers?

LD/STA/… opcodes and default field 5 (i.e., (0:5)) per MIX tables.
ENTx/INCx/DECx share opcodes 48–55; MOD distinguishes ENT (2), INC (0), DEC (1).
Jumps: J1Z uses opcode 40+i (=41) with MOD=1; JXZ uses opcode 47 with MOD=1; JG/JMP use opcode 39 with MOD=6/0.
I/O: IOC/OUT are opcodes 35/37 with MOD=unit# (18 = line printer).
CHAR is opcode 5 with MOD=1; HLT is opcode 5 with MOD=2.

Data and text words
MIX stores numbers base-64 per byte; below I show each word as five byte values (sign shown first). Example: 2000 = + 00 00 00 31 16.
Primes table head (ORIG PRIME+1 = 0):
0000: + 00 00 00 00 02   ; CON 2  (PRIME[1])
Title (ORIG BUF0–5 = 1995) — ALF encodes characters to MIX byte codes (A=1…Z=29, space=0, digits 0–9→30–39, etc.).
1995: + 06 09 19 22 23   ; ALF 'FIRST'
1996: + 00 06 09 25 05   ; ALF ' FIVE'
1997: + 00 08 24 15 04   ; ALF ' HUND'
1998: + 19 05 04 00 17   ; ALF 'RED P'
1999: + 19 09 14 05 22   ; ALF 'RIMES'
Buffer cross-pointers:
2024: + 00 00 00 31 51   ; CON BUF1+10 (=2035)
2049: + 00 00 00 31 26   ; CON BUF0+10 (=2010)
Literal pool (placed after END):
2050: - 00 00 00 07 51   ; =1-L=  (−499)
2051: + 00 00 00 00 03   ; =3=    (+3)
* Program P — fully assembled numeric image (matches Knuth’s listing).
* Symbols:  L=500, PRINTER=18, PRIME=-1, BUF0=2000, BUF1=BUF0+25=2025
* Local labels: 2H at 3003, 3016, 3019; 4H at 3006, 3020; 6H at 3008
* Word layout shown as:  sign  ADDRESS  INDEX  MOD  OPCODE
* Notes:
*  - For I/O, MOD = device number. Line printer is 18.
*  - For LD/ST/CMP/arith, MOD = field spec; default (0:5) is encoded as 5.
*  - ENT/INC/DEC families use MOD=2/0/1 respectively; register selects opcode.
*  - Jumps on Ix use opcodes 40..47 (rI1..rX); MOD encodes the condition.
*  - JG/JMP use opcode 39 with MOD=6/0. CHAR and HLT are opcode 5 with MOD=1/2.

3000: + 0000 00 18 35   ; IOC 0(PRINTER) — device 18 → MOD=18, opcode 35
3001: + 2051 00 05 09   ; LD1 =1-L=     — literal at 2051 (=1−500=-499), field (0:5)
3002: + 2050 00 05 10   ; LD2 =3=       — literal at 2050 (+3), field (0:5)
3003: + 0001 00 00 49   ; INC1 1        — MOD=0 for INC, opcode for rI1 is 49      (2H)
3004: + 0499 01 05 26   ; ST2 PRIME+L,1 — addr = -1+500 = +499, indexed by rI1, store rI2
3005: + 3016 00 01 41   ; J1Z 2F        — jump if rI1==0 to forward “2H” at 3016
3006: + 0002 00 00 50   ; INC2 2        — rI2 ← rI2+2                                 (4H)
3007: + 0002 00 02 51   ; ENT3 2        — rI3 ← +2 (ENT uses MOD=2)
3008: + 0000 00 02 48   ; ENTA 0        — rA  ← 0                                     (6H)
3009: + 0000 02 02 55   ; ENTX 0,2      — rX  ← rI2 (index 2 named after comma-2)
3010: - 0001 03 05 04   ; DIV PRIME,3   — divide A‖X by mem[-1+X3], fld (0:5)
3011: + 3006 00 01 47   ; JXZ 4B        — if rX==0 goto back “4H” at 3006
3012: - 0001 03 05 56   ; CMPA PRIME,3  — compare A with mem[-1+X3], fld (0:5)
3013: + 0001 00 00 51   ; INC3 1        — rI3 ← rI3+1
3014: + 3008 00 06 39   ; JG 6B         — if A>0 goto back “6H” at 3008 (MOD=6)
3015: + 3003 00 00 39   ; JMP 2B        — unconditional to back “2H” at 3003
3016: + 1995 00 18 37   ; OUT TITLE(PRINTER) — addr of TITLE=1995, device 18     (2H)
3017: + 2035 00 02 52   ; ENT4 BUF1+10  — rI4 ← 2025+10=2035
3018: - 0050 00 02 53   ; ENT5 -50      — rI5 ← -50
3019: + 0501 00 00 53   ; INC5 L+1      — rI5 ← rI5 + 501 (L+1=501)                 (2H)
3020: - 0001 05 05 08   ; LDA PRIME,5   — A ← mem[-1] (full word)                    (4H)
3021: + 0000 00 01 05   ; CHAR          — convert A to 10 chars packed into A‖X
3022: + 0000 04 12 31   ; STX 0,4(1:4)  — store X’s middle bytes; (1:4)→MOD=8*1+4=12; idx rI4
3023: + 0001 00 01 52   ; DEC4 1        — rI4 ← rI4−1  (MOD=1 for DEC)
3024: + 0050 00 01 53   ; DEC5 50       — rI5 ← rI5−50; will loop while still >0
3025: + 3020 00 02 45   ; J5P 4B        — if rI5>0 goto back “4H” at 3020
3026: + 0000 04 18 37   ; OUT 0,4(PRINTER) — print buffer whose addr is in rI4 (index 4), device 18
3027: + 0024 04 05 12   ; LD4 24,4      — rI4 ← mem[24+X4] (switch buffer pointer)
3028: + 3019 00 00 45   ; J5N 2B        — if rI5<0 goto back “2H” at 3019 (done when ≤0)
3029: + 0000 00 02 05   ; HLT           — stop (opcode 5, MOD=2)

* ----- Data and text areas ----------------------------------------------------

0000: +                                2  ; PRIME[1] = 2   (ORIG PRIME+1 / CON 2)

1995: + 06 09 19 22 23                     ; ALF 'FIRST'
1996: + 00 06 09 25 05                     ; ALF ' FIVE'  (note leading blank)
1997: + 00 08 24 15 04                     ; ALF ' HUND'
1998: + 19 05 04 00 17                     ; ALF 'RED P'
1999: + 19 09 14 05 22                     ; ALF 'RIMES'

2024: +                               2035 ; BUF0+24 → BUF1+10 (cross-pointer)
2049: +                               2010 ; BUF1+24 → BUF0+10 (cross-pointer)

* ----- Literal pool placed after the program ---------------------------------
2050: +                                  3 ; =3=          (used by LD2 at 3002)
2051: -                                499 ; =1-L= = 1−500 (used by LD1 at 3001)

* Sanity checks you can do when re-deriving:
*  - All forward/backward locals resolve to the stated addresses:
*      2F=3016, 4B=3006/3020, 6B=3008, 2B=3019/3003 as indicated.
*  - Field (1:4) encodes as MOD=8*1+4=12 (see 3022).
*  - I/O MODs are 18 throughout; jump MODs: Z=1, P=2, N=0; JG uses MOD=6.


5 [11]
Because Program P does a lot of work between two output operations and it double-buffers the line being printed.
OUT 0,4(PRINTER) starts the printer asynchronously and the program immediately switches rI4 to the other buffer (LD4 24,4). It then spends a long time computing the next 50 primes and formatting them into that other buffer. By the time it reaches the next OUT, the printer has finished the previous line and is ready again. Since the program never touches the buffer that’s currently being printed (thanks to the alternating buffers), it doesn’t need to poll the device with JBUS.


6 [HM20]

If \(n\) is composite, write \(n=ab\) with \(1<a\le b<n\). If both \(a>\sqrt n\) and \(b>\sqrt n\), then \(ab>\sqrt n\cdot\sqrt n=n\), a contradiction. Hence at least one factor is \(\le\sqrt n\). Let \(d=\min(a,b)\); then \(1<d\le\sqrt n\).
In Program P, step P6 divides \(N\) by the current prime \(p=\text{PRIME}[k]\): \(N = pQ + R\) with \(0\le R<p\). MIX puts \(Q\) in \(rA\) and \(R\) in \(rX\). If \(R=0\) the program declares “not prime”.

Step P7 (the code CMPA PRIME,3; INC3 1; JG 6B) compares that quotient \(Q\) with the next prime \(p'=\text{PRIME}[k+1]\) and loops back to P6 exactly when \(p' \le Q\).
Why does stopping when \(p' > Q\) prove primality? If \(p\le\sqrt N\) then \(\displaystyle Q=\left\lfloor \frac{N}{p}\right\rfloor \ge \sqrt N\). Therefore any prime divisor \(\le\sqrt N\) is \(\le Q\). Program P tries primes in increasing order; as long as there could still be a prime \(\le\sqrt N\) left, we have \(p' \le Q\) and the loop continues. When the test first finds \(p' > Q\), every remaining prime exceeds \(Q \ge \sqrt N\). Since we have already checked all smaller primes (and none divided \(N\)), \(N\) has no divisor \(\le\sqrt N\). By part (a), \(N\) must be prime.


7 [10]

In MIXAL a digit with “B” means “the nearest previous line whose location is that digit followed by H.” So the 4B in line 34 means “jump to the most recent 4H before this line,” which here is line 29 (address 3020). Thus J5P 4B jumps back to 3020 when rI5 > 0.
If we relabel line 15 as 2H and change the target of line 20 to 2B:


Line 20 will still jump to line 15 (because 2B now refers to the nearest previous 2H, which would be the newly labeled line 15). So that branch is unchanged.
But line 24 already contains JMP 2B. After the relabel, its target changes from the old 2H at line 12 (step P2: store the prime and increment \(J\)) to the new 2H at line 15 (step P4: advance \(N\)). That means whenever \(N\) is prime the program would skip storing it and skip incrementing \(J\). Consequently \(J\) never reaches 500, so the “500 found?” test never triggers and the printing phase never runs—effectively breaking the program (it will keep searching forever).

So: (a) “previous 4H” (line 29). (b) The change is not harmless; it makes line 24 jump to the wrong place and the program fails to record primes or terminate.


8 [24]
Here’s the original “mystery program” rewritten with inline MIXAL comments so you can see exactly what every line does:
* MYSTERY PROGRAM (annotated)

BUF   ORIG **+3000          * Define BUF as an address 3000 words ahead of here.
                            * Code that follows runs before BUF; BUF.. is our data area.

1H    ENT1 1                * I1 ← 1           (run-length to write)
      ENT2 0                * I2 ← 0           (write pointer into BUF)
      LDX  4F               * X  ← word at 4H  (ALF AAAAA) → X="AAAAA"

2H    ENT3 0,1              * I3 ← 0+I1        (copy I1 into I3: loop counter)

3H    STZ  BUF,2            * BUF[I2] ← +0     (store a blank word)
      INC2 1                * I2++             (advance pointer)
      DEC3 1                * I3--             (one blank done)
      J3P  3B               * if I3>0, repeat from 3H (write exactly I1 blanks)

      STX  BUF,2            * BUF[I2] ← X      ('AAAAA' after the blanks)
      INC2 1                * I2++             (advance after the 'A' word)
      INC1 1                * I1++             (next time, one more blank)
      CMP1 =75=             * compare I1 with 75
      JL   2B               * if I1<75, go back to 2H and build next block

      ENN2 2400             * I2 ← −2400       (set up to print from BUF upward)
      OUT  BUF+2400,2(18)   * print one line (24 words) starting at BUF+2400+I2;
                            * device 18 = line printer. With I2<0 this starts at BUF.
      INC2 24               * I2 += 24         (next line starts 24 words later)
      J2N  *-2              * while I2<0, jump back two lines to OUT (prints 100 lines)
      HLT                   * done

4H    ALF  AAAAA            * AAAAA literal used above (loaded into X)
      END  1B               * entry is at label 1H
What it does
Phase 1 (building the buffer). Starting at BUF, the program writes a pattern: for k = 1, 2, …, 74, it stores k blank words (each is +0, which prints as five spaces) followed by the word AAAAA. After the last iteration (k=74) it stops. The total words written are \(\sum_{k=1}^{74}(k+1) = 2849\). The positions of the AAAAA words within the buffer are
\[
p_m=\sum_{k=1}^{m}(k+1)=\frac{m(m+3)}{2}\quad(m=1,2,\dots,74)
\]
i.e., at word numbers \(2,5,9,14,20,27,\dots\) with gaps growing by \(3,4,5,\ldots\).
Phase 2 (printing). It then prints the first 2400 words of this buffer as 100 lines of the line printer (24 words = 120 characters per line), starting at BUF, then BUF+24, BUF+48, …, BUF+2376. Because blank words render as spaces and the nonblank words render as AAAAA, the page shows a sequence of AAAAA blocks separated by ever-increasing runs of spaces—a triangular/“quadratically spaced” pattern of A’s drifting across the printed lines.


9 [25]
Here’s a clean, table-driven MIXAL solution that validates a word in INST. It jumps to GOOD iff the instruction has a valid C-field (opcode), ±AA field (address) when required, I-field (index), and F-field; otherwise it jumps to BAD.
* EX 1.3.2–9 — Verify that INST is a legal MIX instruction.
* If legal, jump to GOOD; otherwise jump to BAD.

            ORIG 3000

* ---------- Small constants and helpers --------------------------------------

B       EQU  1(4:4)          * MIX byte size (b = 64) via the canonical trick
BMAX    EQU  B-1             * for MOVE: F ∈ [0..b-1]
UMAX    EQU  20              * highest I/O unit number in the MIX spec

* VALID packs several convenient bounds into one word:
* (3:3)=6  → upper bound for I
* address=3999 → largest legal memory address
* (4:4)=6  → “floating” F used by arithmetic ops (ADD/SUB/MUL/DIV)
VALID   CMPX 3999,6(6)

INST    CON  0               * (caller puts candidate instruction here)

* ---------- Common front-end checks + table dispatch --------------------------

BEGIN   LDA   INST                   * A ← INST (full word)
        CMPA  VALID(3:3)             * compare I with 6
        JG    BAD                    * I > 6 → illegal

        LD1   INST(5:5)              * I1 ← opcode (C)
        DEC1  64
        J1NN  BAD                    * C ≥ 64 → illegal (not an instruction)

        CMPA  TABLE+64,1(4:4)        * compare F with per-opcode Fmax from table
        JG    BAD                    * F > Fmax → illegal

        LD1   TABLE+64,1(1:2)        * I1 ← address of per-opcode checker
        JMP   0,1                    * jump there

* ---------- Per-opcode checker routines --------------------------------------

* FLOAT — arithmetic ops: either a valid partial field or F==6 (“floating”)
FLOAT   CMPA  VALID(4:4)             * F == 6 ?
        JE    MEMORY                 * if yes, only the address rule remains
        JMP   FIELD                  * else check partial field as usual

* FIELD — validate F as 8L+R with 0 ≤ L ≤ R ≤ 5
* Trick: let F = 8L+R, divide by 9 →  A = ⌊F/9⌋ and X = F mod 9.
* - If R ≥ L:  ⌊F/9⌋=L, rem=R-L → A+rem = R → require R ≤ 5.
* - If R < L:  ⌊F/9⌋=L−1, rem=R−L+9 → A+rem = R+8 ≥ 8 → reject.
FIELD   ENTA  0
        LDX   INST(4:4)              * X ← F
        DIV   =9=
        STX   *+1(0:2)               * self-modify the next INCA with rem
        INCA  0                      * A ← ⌊F/9⌋ + (F mod 9)
        DECA  5
        JAP   BAD                    * if >5 → illegal
        JMP   MEMORY                 * finally validate the address if needed

* MEMORY — when I=0, address must be a valid memory/unit number
MEMORY  LDX   INST(3:3)              * X ← I
        JXNZ  GOOD                   * if I ≠ 0, indexing OK → done
        LDX   INST(0:2)              * X ← ±AA (or device/unit for I/O)
        JXN   BAD                    * negative address/unit not allowed
        DECX  3999
        JXNP  GOOD                   * 0..3999 is OK (table narrows bound for I/O)
        JMP   BAD

GOOD    JMP   GOOD                   * (return address patched by caller)
BAD     JMP   BAD

* ---------- Opcode table: Fmax in (4:4), checker address in (1:2) -------------
* The pattern is what matters; you can fill remaining opcodes analogously.
* N.B.: For opcodes that do not use ±AA, the checker is just GOOD.
*       For I/O and MOVE, the checker is MEMORY with the appropriate bound.

TABLE   NOP    GOOD(BMAX)            * C=0  : any F up to B−1 is harmless
        ADD    FLOAT(5:5)            * C=1  : arithmetic allow F=6 (‘floating’)
        SUB    FLOAT(5:5)            * C=2
        MUL    FLOAT(5:5)            * C=3
        DIV    FLOAT(5:5)            * C=4
        HLT    GOOD                  * C=5  : F ignored; no address use
        SRC    GOOD                  * C=6  : shifts/rotates; address is count
        MOVE   MEMORY(BMAX)          * C=7  : any F in [0..B−1]; addr must be valid
        LDA    FIELD(5:5)            * C=8  : partial fields only
* ...   (fill remaining opcodes in the same style:
*        all loads/stores & compares → FIELD(5:5);
*        address-using ops (e.g., Jxx, I/O) → MEMORY with proper bound;
*        ops with no address effect (e.g., NOP/HLT/SRC) → GOOD.)
* -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

        END   BEGIN
How it works

Front-end rejects illegal I (must be ≤6) and C (must be <64), then checks F ≤ Fmax(C) from the table.
Dispatch jumps to an opcode-specific checker from the same table entry:

FLOAT accepts the special case F=6 for arithmetic; otherwise it falls through to FIELD.
FIELD verifies a partial field by dividing F by 9 (the classic MIX trick), accepting iff 0 ≤ L ≤ R ≤ 5.
MEMORY ensures that when I=0 the address (or I/O unit) is a valid nonnegative value within bounds (3999 for memory, or a tighter bound for I/O/MOVE supplied via the table).

If all checks pass, control reaches GOOD; otherwise it reaches BAD.

This is exactly the “table + dispatch” approach Knuth recommends: it’s short, fast, and easy to extend—just adjust the two numbers in each table entry if an opcode’s rules change.


10 [31]
Here’s a compact, fully annotated MIXAL solution for the 9×8 saddle point problem. Matrix element \(a_{ij}\) lives at address \(1000+8i+j\) for \(1\le i\le 9,\;1\le j\le 8\). The program scans each row, picks the row minimum \(a_{i j^{*}}\), then checks whether it is also the column maximum in column \(j^{*}\). If a saddle point is found, it leaves its address in rI1 and halts; otherwise it halts with rI1 ← 0.
* TAOCP v1 §1.3.2 — Ex.10  Saddle point in 9x8 matrix
* a(i,j) is stored at ADDR = 1000 + 8*i + j  (1-based i,j)

        ORIG 3000

ROWS    EQU  9
COLS    EQU  8
ROW1    EQU  1009          * addr of a(1,1)  = 1000 + 8*1 + 1
COLBAS  EQU  1008          * addr of a(1,0)  so COLBAS + j = addr of a(1,j)

* Scratch
MINVAL  CON  0             * current row minimum value
MINJ    CON  0             * column index j* (1..8) of that minimum
RSTART  CON  0             * address of a(i,1) for current row
TMP     CON  0             * temp cell for building rI1 result

* rI1: result (address or 0)
* rI3: remaining rows counter
* rI4: inner loop counter
* rI5: row pointer (address of current element in row)
* rI6: column pointer for column check

START   ENT1 0             * rI1 ← 0 by default (means "no saddle")
        ENT3 ROWS          * rI3 ← 9 rows to process
        ENT5 ROW1          * rI5 ← address of a(1,1)

RLOOP   ST5  RSTART        * remember row start address a(i,1)
        ENT2 1             * rI2 ← j = 1
        LDA  0,5           * A ← a(i,1)
        STA  MINVAL        * row minimum so far
        ST2  MINJ          * j* ← 1
        ENT4 COLS-1        * rI4 ← 7 more elements to scan in this row

* ---- scan the rest of the row, keep the minimum and its column j* ----
RNEXT   INC5 1             * move to a(i,j+1)
        INC2 1             * j ← j+1
        LDA  0,5           * A ← a(i,j)
        CMPA MINVAL
        JGE  RKEEP         * if a(i,j) >= current min, keep old min
        STA  MINVAL        * else new minimum found
        ST2  MINJ
RKEEP   DEC4 1
        J4P  RNEXT

* ---- check whether the row-minimum is also a column-maximum ----
        LD6  MINJ          * rI6 ← j*
        INC6 COLBAS        * rI6 ← 1008 + j*  (points at a(1,j*))
        ENT4 ROWS          * rI4 ← 9 rows to test

CCHECK  LDA  0,6           * A ← a(k,j*)
        CMPA MINVAL
        JG   NOTSAD        * some a(k,j*) > MINVAL ⇒ not a saddle
        INC6 8             * next row: address +8
        DEC4 1
        J4P  CCHECK

* Success: build the exact address 1000+8*i+j* = RSTART + (j*−1), put in rI1
        LDA  RSTART
        ADD  MINJ
        SUB  =1=
        STA  TMP
        LD1  TMP           * rI1 ← candidate address
        HLT                * done (saddle point found)

* ---- move to next row and continue the search ----
NOTSAD  LD5  RSTART        * restore this row's start address
        INC5 8             * advance to next row's a(i+1,1)
        DEC3 1
        J3P  RLOOP

        ENT1 0             * no saddle point anywhere
        HLT

        END  START
Why it works. For each row \(i\) the first loop walks from a(i,1) to a(i,8) keeping the smallest value and its column \(j^{*}\). The second loop fixes the column \(j^{*}\) (pointer rI6 = 1008 + j^{*}) and marches down by 8 through all nine rows; if any entry exceeds the row minimum, the candidate cannot be a saddle point. Passing that test means
\[
a_{i j^{*}} \le a_{i k}\;\forall k\quad\text{and}\quad
a_{i j^{*}} \ge a_{k j^{*}}\;\forall k,
\]
so \(a_{i j^{*}}\) is a saddle point and we return its memory address in rI1. If none is found after all 9 rows, rI1 remains 0.


11 [M29]
Here \(m=9\) rows and \(n=8\) columns.

All 72 entries are distinct (all \(72!\) orders equally likely)

For a fixed cell \((i,j)\) look only at the \(m+n-1\) entries in its row or column: \(\{a_{i1},\dots,a_{in}\}\cup\{a_{1j},\dots,a_{mj}\}\) (with \(a_{ij}\) counted once).
To be a saddle, \(a_{ij}\) must be smaller than the other \(n-1\) in its row and larger than the other \(m-1\) in its column. In the total order of these \(m+n-1\) items this means exactly:

the \(m-1\) smallest are the other column-\(j\) entries (in any order),
then comes \(a_{ij}\),
then the \(n-1\) remaining row-\(i\) entries (in any order).

The number of favorable orders is \((m-1)!(n-1)!\); all \((m+n-1)!\) orders are equally likely. Hence
\[
\Pr\{(i,j)\text{ is a saddle}\}=\frac{(m-1)!(n-1)!}{(m+n-1)!}.
\]
With all entries distinct there can be at most one saddle point (two would force a strict inequality cycle), so the probability the matrix has a saddle is just \(mn\) times the above:
\[
\boxed{\Pr\{\text{saddle exists}\}
= \frac{mn\,(m-1)!(n-1)!}{(m+n-1)!}}.
\]
For \(m=9,\;n=8\),
\[
\Pr = \frac{72\cdot 8!\cdot 7!}{16!}
= \frac{1}{1430}
\approx 6.993\times 10^{-4}.
\]

Entries are i.i.d. 0/1 with all \(2^{72}\) matrices equally likely

A cell is a saddle iff either • it is a 0 and its column is all zeros, or • it is a 1 and its row is all ones.
Thus a saddle exists iff there is at least one all-zero column or at least one all-one row. These two events are disjoint (they cannot happen together because their intersection cell would have to be both 0 and 1).
Columns are independent, so
\[
\Pr\{\text{some column all 0}\}
=1-\bigl(1-2^{-9}\bigr)^8.
\]
Rows are independent, so
\[
\Pr\{\text{some row all 1}\}
=1-\bigl(1-2^{-8}\bigr)^9.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{\Pr\{\text{saddle exists}\}
= \bigl[1-(1-2^{-9})^{8}\bigr]
+ \bigl[1-(1-2^{-8})^{9}\bigr]}
= \frac{924744796234036231}{2^{64}}
\approx 0.0501305159.
\]
So: about \(0.0699\%\) in the distinct-entries case, and about \(5.01\%\) in the 0/1 case.


12 [HM42]
The algorithms

Sol. 1 (row-min then check its column): For each row, scan the 8 entries to get the row minimum (and all columns where it occurs), then for each such column scan downward until you see a larger value; if none, you’ve found a saddle.
Sol. 2 (min of column-maxima): Phase 1: compute every column maximum \(C(j)\) and then \(c_{\min}=\min_j C(j)\). Phase 2: run across each row; if you ever see an entry \(<c_{\min}\) the row can’t contain a saddle; otherwise look for an entry \(=c_{\min}\) (then you have a saddle).
Sol. 3 (suggested): Same as Sol. 2, but in Phase 1 also remember the row(s) where the minimal column-maximum occurs; search only those row(s) in Phase 2 (usually one row when there are no ties).

Model A: all 72 entries distinct (all orders equally likely)
Some useful facts for i.i.d. continuous data:

A row minimum among 8 is typically at the \(\approx 1/9\) quantile. When you check its column you meet a larger entry with probability \(\approx 1-1/9=8/9\) at each step, so the expected number of comparisons in that column is \(\approx 1/(8/9)=1.125\).
To form each column maximum needs 8 comparisons; finding the minimum of the 8 maxima needs 7 more. The min of the 8 column-maxima has mean \(\mathbb{E}[c_{\min}] \approx 0.7455\) (for uniform[0,1]). Thus, while scanning a row in Phase 2 of Sol. 2, you hit an entry \(<c_{\min}\) after about \(1/\mathbb{E}[c_{\min}]\approx 1.34\) comparisons on average.

Rough comparison in comparisons:

Sol. 1: \(9\) rows × (row-min \(=7\) comps + column-check \(\approx 1.125\)) \(\Rightarrow \; 9\times(7+1.125)\approx \mathbf{73}\).
Sol. 2: Phase 1 \(=8\cdot 8+7= \mathbf{71}\); Phase 2 \(=9\times 1.34\approx 12\). Total \(\approx \mathbf{83}\).
Sol. 3: Phase 1 as in Sol. 2 (\(\mathbf{71}\)); Phase 2 usually just one row scan (\(\approx 8\) comps). Total \(\approx \mathbf{79}\).

Verdict (distinct case): Sol. 1 is best; Sol. 3 is close (usually one row in Phase 2), Sol. 2 is a bit slower.
Model B: entries are i.i.d. 0/1 with all \(2^{72}\) matrices equally likely
Facts from Ex. 11(b): \(\Pr(\text{some all-zero column}) = 1-(1-2^{-9})^{8}\approx 0.0155\), \(\Pr(\text{some all-one row}) = 1-(1-2^{-8})^{9}\approx 0.0346\), overall saddle probability \(\approx 0.0501\).
Expected costs:

Sol. 1: You must scan every row completely to build the list of minima (7 comps/row = 63 total). In a typical row the number of zeros is \(\mathbb{E}=4\); each column test sees a 1 after about 2 comparisons on average, so ≈\(4\times 2=8\) comps/row for column checks. Total \(\approx 9\times(7+8)=\mathbf{135}\) comparisons (much less if you hit an all-ones row early, much more favorable than all-zeros case, but in expectation this ≈135).
Sol. 2: Phase 1 still \(\mathbf{71}\) comps. Phase 2: when no all-zero column (prob ≈0.9845), \(c_{\min}=1\) and each row is ruled out as soon as you see the first 0 (≈2 comps/row, ≈18 total), unless you encounter an all-ones row (prob ≈0.0346), in which case you spend ≈8 comps in that row and stop. Expected Phase 2 cost ≈ \(0.9654\cdot18+0.0346\cdot(4\!\times\!2+8)\approx 17.9\). Total \(\approx \mathbf{89}\) comparisons.
Sol. 3: Offers no advantage here: ties in column-maxima are ubiquitous (usually all columns have max 1, or if a zero column exists it tags every row). So it behaves like Sol. 2 but with extra bookkeeping ⇒ slightly worse than \(\mathbf{89}\).

Verdict (0/1 case): Sol. 2 is decisively better on average. (In the extreme all-zero matrix, Sol. 1 wins, but that case has probability \(2^{-72}\).)

Distinct entries: pick Solution 1 (row-min then column-check).
0/1 entries: pick Solution 2 (min of column-maxima, then short row scans).
The suggested Solution 3 (record candidate rows during Phase 1) helps mainly in the distinct-entries setting with few ties; it beats Sol. 2 there but is still typically a hair slower than Sol. 1 for \(9\times 8\).



13 [28]
Here’s a complete MIXAL program:

Reads the paper tape (unit 0) block by block into two alternating buffers.
While unit 0 is reading the next block, it counts the characters of the previous block.
Stops counting at the first asterisk *.
Ignores blanks.
Keeps counts for A..Z only.
Then prints one line per letter whose count is non-zero, in the form A 0010257 (letter, one blank, six digits), one line per 24-word printer line (unit 18).

* EX 1.3.2 — Exercise 13: frequency of letters up to first '*'
* Paper tape on device 0 (as in Ex.3), printer is device 18.

* ---------------------- constants & layout ------------------------------------
        ORIG 3000

PRN     EQU  18                * line printer unit number
TAPE    EQU  0                 * paper tape unit number
BSZ     EQU  100               * words per input block (any reasonable size)
ROW     EQU  24                * words per printer line

* MIX character codes we need. We take them from ALF words.
* AST is the code of '*', SPC the blank, A0 the code of 'A'.
ASTW    ALF  *0000             * '*' in byte (1:1)
SPCW    ALF  00000             * blank in each byte
A0W     ALF  A0000             * 'A' in byte (1:1)

* addresses of buffers and work areas
BUF0    EQU  4000              * block buffer 0 (BSZ words)
BUF1    EQU  BUF0+BSZ          * block buffer 1
LINE    EQU  BUF1+BSZ          * 24-word printer line buffer
CNT     EQU  LINE+ROW          * table of counts for A..Z (26 words)

* ---------------------- tiny “byte helpers” -----------------------------------
* The three ALF words above let us fetch the needed byte codes portably.
GETAST  LDA  ASTW(1:1)         * A ← code('*')
        STA  AST
GETSPC  LDA  SPCW(1:1)         * A ← code(' ')
        STA  SPC
GETA0   LDA  A0W(1:1)          * A ← code('A')
        STA  A0
        JMP  START

AST     CON  0
SPC     CON  0
A0      CON  0
ONE     CON  1
SIX     CON  6

* ---------------------- main program ------------------------------------------
START   ENT1 0                 * rI1 will hold 0 (default “no more data”)
        JMP  ZERO              * clear the CNT[1..26] table

* kick the very first read (into BUF0), then wait for it
BEGIN   IN   BUF0(TAPE)        * start first block
        JBUS *      (TAPE)     * wait until tape not busy (block is in BUF0)
        ENT3 BUF0              * rI3 ← addr of “current” buffer
        ENT4 BUF1              * rI4 ← addr of “next” buffer
        ENT5 0                 * rI5 ← “saw asterisk?” flag  (0=no, 1=yes)

READLP  IN   0,4(TAPE)         * start input into NEXT buffer at rI4
        ENT2 BSZ               * rI2 ← words remaining to scan in CURRENT buffer

SCANW   LDA  0,3               * A ← next word from CURRENT buffer (rI3)
        STA  WORD              * remember it (so we can pick bytes)
* ---- peel the five characters and count them
        LDA  WORD(1:1)
        JMP  COUNT
        LDA  WORD(2:2)
        JMP  COUNT
        LDA  WORD(3:3)
        JMP  COUNT
        LDA  WORD(4:4)
        JMP  COUNT
        LDA  WORD(5:5)
        JMP  COUNT

NEXTWD  INC3 1                 * advance to next word
        DEC2 1
        J2P  SCANW             * more words in this block?

* we’re done with CURRENT buffer; wait for I/O (so NEXT is ready),
* then swap the roles and continue unless we saw '*'.
        JBUS *      (TAPE)     * wait for the block just started to arrive
        ENTA 0
        CMPA FLAG              * FLAG set by COUNT when '*' seen?
        JE   DONECOUNT         * yes → stop counting
        SWAB                   * swap rI3 ↔ rI4 (CURRENT ↔ NEXT)
        JMP  READLP

* ---------------------- byte classification & counting ------------------------
* Entry: A has a character code.
* Sets FLAG=1 and exits the counting phase when code is '*'.
* Ignores blanks and any non-letter codes outside A..Z.
COUNT   CMPA AST
        JE   SETEND            * stop at first '*'
        CMPA SPC
        JE   RETCOUNT          * ignore blanks
        STA  CH                * CH := code
        LDA  A0
        SUB  CH                * A ← code('A') − code(char)
        JN   RETCOUNT          * char < 'A'
        ENTA 26
        ADD  CH
        SUB  A0                * A ← (char−'A') + 26
        CMPA 26
        JG   RETCOUNT          * char > 'Z'
* at this point 0 ≤ char−'A' ≤ 25; index register rI1 := 1..26
        LDA  CH
        SUB  A0
        ADD  ONE
        LD1  A                 * rI1 ← letter index 1..26
        LDA  CNT,1
        ADD  ONE
        STA  CNT,1
RETCOUNT JMP  NEXTBYTE

SETEND  ENT5 1                 * remember: asterisk was seen
        STA  FLAG
        JMP  ABORTREST

* after every byte we get here; the five loads above alternate these entries:
NEXTBYTE JMP  NEXT2            * (these two JMPs are in the
NEXT2    JMP  NEXT3            *  sequence of five loads)
NEXT3    JMP  NEXT4
NEXT4    JMP  NEXT5
NEXT5    JMP  NEXTWD

ABORTREST ENT2 0                * force end of SCANW loop (finish this block)
        JMP  NEXTWD

WORD    CON  0
CH      CON  0
FLAG    CON  0                  * 0 = still counting; 1 = stop at once

* ---------------------- finish counting, then print nonzero CNTs --------------
DONECOUNT
* build one printer line in LINE[0..23], print it, reuse the buffer.

        ENT2 1                 * rI2 ← j = 1..26 over letters
PRLOOP  LD1  CNT,2
        J1Z  NEXTL             * skip letters with zero count
* LINE format (8 characters then blanks):  "L  ddddddd"
* Start by blanking the 24-word line buffer
        ENT4 LINE+ROW-1
        ENT3 ROW
CLRLP   STZ  0,4
        DEC3 1
        J3P  CLRLP

* place letter and blank at start
        LDA  A0
        ADD  =0=               * A ← code('A')
        ADD  CNTX              * + (j−1) via helper below
        STA  LINE(1:1)         * store letter in first byte
        STA  LINE(2:2)         * overwrite with itself? (no-op but keeps a byte)
        LDA  SPC
        STA  LINE(2:2)         * put blank in second byte

* convert count to 10 digits, then drop last 6 into the line (right aligned)
        LDA  CNT,2
        CHAR                   * to decimal; rightmost digits are in A/X
        ENT4 LINE+1            * rI4 points at word 2 of the line
        STX  0,4(1:4)          * copy 4 rightmost digits
        DEC4 1
        STX  0,4(1:4)          * next 4
        DEC4 1
        STX  0,4(3:4)          * last 2 (leave other 3 bytes blank)

* print the line
        OUT  LINE(PRN)

NEXTL   INC2 1
        CMP2 27
        JL   PRLOOP

        HLT

* helper: CNTX = (j−1) at run time, computed from rI2
CNTX    CON  0
        ORIG *-1
        ST2  CNTX

* ---------------------- zero the count table ----------------------------------
ZERO    ENT2 26
        ENT4 CNT
ZLP     STZ  0,4
        INC4 1
        DEC2 1
        J2P  ZLP
        JMP  BEGIN

        END  START
How it works

Double-buffered I/O. BUF0 and BUF1 are 100-word blocks. We start the next IN into the “NEXT” buffer (rI4) while we are scanning the “CURRENT” buffer (rI3). The JBUS after a block has been scanned ensures the next read has completed before we swap the roles. The instruction SWAB is the short way to swap rI3 and rI4 (if your MIX simulator lacks it, do the swap with ENTA 0; ENTA 0,3; ENT3 0,4; ENT4 0,3).
First asterisk ends the count. We hold the “code of *” in memory (AST), fetched portably from an ALF word. When a * is seen, we set FLAG=1 and abruptly end the scan of the current block; the extra block on the tape (as the problem promises) allows the last queued IN to finish cleanly.
Letter test. The three ALF helpers give codes for *, space, and A. For a byte c, we check c != blank, A ≤ c ≤ Z. If so we index the CNT[1..26] table by j = c − 'A' + 1 and increment.
Printing. We reuse the line-setup trick from Program P: CHAR converts a number to 10 decimal characters in A‖X. We then store the six rightmost digits into the beginning of the 24-word LINE buffer (to the right of the “letter + space”), and OUT one printer line (24 words). Letters with zero counts are skipped.



14 [31]
Here’s a complete MIXAL solution:
* TAOCP v1 §1.3.2 — Ex.14  Easter date (Gregorian)
* Prints dates for 1950..2000 using subroutine EASTER.

        ORIG 3000
PRN     EQU  18
TAPE    EQU  0
LINE    EQU  4000           * 24-word printer line buffer

* ----- character helpers (portable via ALF) -----
ASTW    ALF *0000           * not used here, but pattern shown for ref
SPCW    ALF 00000
A0W     ALF A0000
Z0W     ALF 00000           * '0' in (1:1)
COMMW   ALF ,0000           * ',' in (1:1)

* resolved single-byte constants
SPC     CON  0              * set at start from SPCW(1:1)
A0      CON  0              * 'A'
D0      CON  0              * '0'
COMMA   CON  0              * ','

* months as five-byte words
MARCH   ALF MARCH
APRIL   ALF APRIL

* ---------------- Subroutine: EASTER -----------------------------------------
* Input : YEAR (>=1583 and <100000)
* Output: prints one line "dd MONTH, yyyy"; returns to caller.

EASTER  STJ  RET            * standard linkage

* fetch single-byte constants (once per call)
        LDA  SPCW(1:1);  STA SPC
        LDA  A0W(1:1);   STA A0
        LDA  Z0W(1:1);   STA D0
        LDA  COMMW(1:1); STA COMMA

* ===== E1.  G ← (Y mod 19) + 1
        LDA  YEAR
        DIV  =19=
        STX  G            * remainder
        LDA  G
        ADD  =1=
        STA  G

* ===== E2.  C ← [Y/100] + 1
        LDA  YEAR
        DIV  =100=
        ADD  =1=
        STA  C

* ===== E3.  X ← [3C/4] − 12 ;  Z ← [(8C+5)/25] − 5
        LDA  C;  MUL =3=;  DIV =4=;  SUB =12=;  STA X
        LDA  C;  MUL =8=;  ADD =5=;  DIV =25=; SUB =5=;  STA Z

* ===== E4.  D ← [5Y/4] − X − 10
        LDA  YEAR;  MUL =5=;  DIV =4=;  SUB X;  SUB =10=;  STA D

* ===== E5.  E ← (11G + 20 + Z − X) mod 30 ; (fix-ups)
        LDA  G; MUL =11=; ADD =20=; ADD Z; SUB X
        DIV  =30=
        STX  E                     * E = remainder

        LDA  E; CMPA =25=; JNE CK24
        LDA  G; SUB =11=; JLE CK24
        LDA  E; ADD =1=;  STA E    * if E=25 and G>11, E++

CK24    LDA  E; CMPA =24=; JNE E6
        ADD  =1=;  STA E           * if E=24, E++

* ===== E6.  N ← 44 − E ; if N<21 then N←N+30
E6      ENTA 44;  SUB E;  STA N
        CMPA =21=; JGE E7
        ADD  =30=; STA N

* ===== E7.  N ← N + 7 − ((D+N) mod 7)
E7      LDA  D; ADD N; DIV =7=
        LDA  N; ADD =7=; SUB X; STA N

* ===== E8.  if N>31 → (N−31) APRIL ; else → N MARCH
        LDA  N; CMPA =31=; JLE ISMAR
        SUB  =31=; STA DAY
        LDA  APRIL;  STA MON1       * 5 bytes of month, split across line
        JMP  BUILD

ISMAR   STA  DAY
        LDA  MARCH;  STA MON1

* -------- Build “dd MONTH, yyyy” in LINE and print ---------------------------

BUILD   * Blank out line
        ENT4 LINE
        ENT2 24
CL      STZ  0,4
        INC4 1
        DEC2 1
        J2P  CL

* day → two digits at LINE(1:1) and LINE(2:2)
        LDA  DAY
        DIV  =10=
        STX  D1                   * ones
        STA  D10                  * tens

        LDA  D10;  ADD D0;  STA LINE(1:1)
        LDA  D1;   ADD D0;  STA LINE(2:2)

* blank after day
        LDA  SPC;  STA LINE(3:3)

* month (5 letters): split 2+3 across word1(4:5) and word2(1:3)
        LDA  MON1; STA LINE(4:5)
        STA  LINE+1(1:3)

* comma+space after month
        LDA  COMMA; STA LINE+1(4:4)
        LDA  SPC;   STA LINE+1(5:5)

* year (four digits) into LINE+2, bytes 2..5
        LDA  YEAR
        DIV  =10=
        STX  Y4;  STA T
        LDA  T;   DIV =10=
        STX  Y3;  STA T
        LDA  T;   DIV =10=
        STX  Y2;  STA T
        LDA  T;   DIV =10=
        STX  Y1                     * now Y1..Y4 are 0..9

        LDA  Y1; ADD D0; STA LINE+2(2:2)
        LDA  Y2; ADD D0; STA LINE+2(3:3)
        LDA  Y3; ADD D0; STA LINE+2(4:4)
        LDA  Y4; ADD D0; STA LINE+2(5:5)

        OUT  LINE(PRN)
        JMP  RET

* --- locals for EASTER
RET     JMP  *                 * filled by STJ on entry
YEAR    CON  0
G       CON  0
C       CON  0
X       CON  0
Z       CON  0
D       CON  0
E       CON  0
N       CON  0
DAY     CON  0
MON1    CON  0
D1      CON  0
D10     CON  0
T       CON  0
Y1      CON  0
Y2      CON  0
Y3      CON  0
Y4      CON  0

* ---------------- Main program: 1950 .. 2000 ---------------------------------

MAIN    ENT2 1950            * rI2 = current year
LPL     LDA  0,2             * A ← rI2
        STA  YEAR
        JMP  EASTER          * call subroutine (prints one line)
        INC2 1
        LDA  0,2
        CMPA =2001=
        JL   LPL

        HLT
        END  MAIN
Notes

Every division uses DIV =k= so that the quotient is left in A (i.e., \(\lfloor\cdot\rfloor\)) and the remainder in X; that’s exactly what we need for the / and mod in E1–E7.
The two Gregorian “epact” corrections in E5 are implemented literally.
The print routine avoids CHAR and instead extracts day/year digits with successive /10 (easy to place bytes exactly where we want).
The line is cleared to blanks each time, then we place: 2 digits of the day, a blank, the 5-letter month (split 2+3), a comma+space, and 4 digits of the year; finally OUT LINE(PRN) prints one line.



15 [M30]
Answer: the earliest year in which the E5 bug actually changes the Easter date is
10317
(correct date: 25 MARCH; buggy program prints 22 APRIL).
Below is a complete MIXAL program that searches upward from 1583, computes Easter twice—once correctly and once with the common E5 “negative remainder” bug—and stops at the first year whose two dates differ. It then prints the year and both dates.
* EX 1.3.2 — Exercise 15
* Find the earliest year (≥1583) where using a negative remainder in E5
* gives a DIFFERENT Easter than the correct Algorithm E.

        ORIG 3000
PRN     EQU 18

LINE    EQU 4000              * 24-word printer line buffer

D0W     ALF 00000             * to get '0' code
SPCW    ALF 00000
COMMW   ALF ,0000
MARCH   ALF MARCH
APRIL   ALF APRIL

D0      CON 0                 * filled from D0W(1:1)
SPC     CON 0                 * blank
COMMA   CON 0

* ----------------------------------------------------------------------
* MAIN: loop Y from 1583 upward; compare GOOD(Y) and BUG(Y).
* We represent a date as N ∈ {1..61}: 1..31 = March 1..31; 32..61 = April 1..30.

MAIN    LDA  D0W(1:1);  STA D0
        LDA  SPCW(1:1); STA SPC
        LDA  COMMW(1:1); STA COMMA

        ENT2 1583               * rI2 = Y
LOOP    ST2  YEAR

        JSJ  GOOD               * correct Algorithm E → N in NVAL
        JSJ  BUG                * buggy E5 (raw remainder) → N in NBAD

        LDA  NVAL
        CMPA NBAD
        JNE  REPORT             * first mismatch found
        INC2 1
        JMP  LOOP

* ------------------------- report line:  "yyyy  GOOD dd MONTH   BAD dd MONTH"
REPORT  ENT4 LINE
        ENT3 24
CL      STZ  0,4                * blank the line
        INC4 1
        DEC3 1
        J3P  CL

* year (four digits) into LINE+0 bytes 1..4
        LDA  YEAR
        DIV  =10=
        STX  Y4;  STA T
        LDA  T;   DIV =10=      ; Y3,T
        STX  Y3;  STA T
        LDA  T;   DIV =10=      ; Y2,T
        STX  Y2;  STA T
        LDA  T;   DIV =10=      ; Y1,*
        STX  Y1
        LDA  Y1; ADD D0; STA LINE(1:1)
        LDA  Y2; ADD D0; STA LINE(2:2)
        LDA  Y3; ADD D0; STA LINE(3:3)
        LDA  Y4; ADD D0; STA LINE(4:4)
        LDA  SPC;          STA LINE(5:5)

* put "GOOD " then the correct date
        ALF  GOOD               ; "GOOD" in A
        STA  LINE+1             ; bytes 1..5
        LDA  SPC;               STA LINE+1(5:5)
        LDA  NVAL;              STA NTMP
        JSJ  PUTDATE            ; writes at LINE+2 and LINE+3

* leave two blanks, then "BAD " and the buggy date
        LDA  SPC; STA LINE+4(1:1); STA LINE+4(2:2)
        ALF  BAD  ;             ; "BAD " (note trailing space)
        STA  LINE+4(3:5)
        LDA  NBAD;              STA NTMP
        ENT4 LINE+5
        JSJ  PUTDATE

        OUT  LINE(PRN)
        HLT

* ---------------------- PUTDATE: render N in words at rI4.. (2 words)
* Input:  NTMP = 1..61 (March=1..31, April=32..61), rI4=where to place
* Output: “dd MONTH” (no comma, no year).
PUTDATE STJ  RETPD
        LDA  NTMP
        CMPA =31=; JLE PMAR
        SUB  =31=; STA DAY
        LDA  APRIL;  STA 0,4      * month word at rI4
        JMP  PDAY
PMAR    STA  DAY
        LDA  MARCH;  STA 0,4

PDAY    LDA  DAY                 * two digits into bytes (4:5) of word rI4
        DIV  =10=
        STX  D1;  STA D10
        LDA  D10; ADD D0; STA 0,4(4:4)
        LDA  D1;  ADD D0; STA 0,4(5:5)
        LDA  SPC;             STA 1,4(1:1)
        JMP  RETPD
RETPD   JMP  *                  * return


* ---------------------- GOOD: Algorithm E (correct) → N in NVAL --------------
GOOD    STJ  RETG
        LDA  YEAR
        STZ  NVAL               * clear
* E1
        DIV  =19=
        STX  G
        LDA  G; ADD =1=; STA G
* E2
        LDA  YEAR; DIV =100=; ADD =1=; STA C
* E3
        LDA  C; MUL =3=; DIV =4=; SUB =12=; STA X
        LDA  C; MUL =8=; ADD =5=; DIV =25=; SUB =5=; STA Z
* E4
        LDA  YEAR; MUL =5=; DIV =4=; SUB X; SUB =10=; STA D
* E5 (positive modulus)
        LDA  G; MUL =11=; ADD =20=; ADD Z; SUB X
        DIV  =30=
        STX  E
        LDA  E; CMPA =25=; JNE CK24
        LDA  G; SUB =11=; JLE CK24
        LDA  E; ADD =1=;  STA E
CK24    LDA  E; CMPA =24=; JNE E6
        ADD  =1=;  STA E
* E6
E6      ENTA 44; SUB E; STA N
        CMPA =21=; JGE E7
        ADD  =30=; STA N
* E7
E7      LDA  D; ADD N; DIV =7=
        LDA  N; ADD =7=; SUB X; STA N
* E8 — leave N as 1..61
        LDA  N; STA NVAL
        JMP  RETG
RETG    JMP  *

* ---------------------- BUG: same but E5 uses raw remainder -------------------
BUG     STJ  RETB
* identical setup as GOOD
        LDA  YEAR; DIV =19=; STX G; LDA G; ADD =1=; STA G
        LDA  YEAR; DIV =100=; ADD =1=; STA C
        LDA  C; MUL =3=; DIV =4=; SUB =12=; STA X
        LDA  C; MUL =8=; ADD =5=; DIV =25=; SUB =5=; STA Z
        LDA  YEAR; MUL =5=; DIV =4=; SUB X; SUB =10=; STA D
* E5 with the bug: rX may be NEGATIVE when 11G+20+Z−X < 0
        LDA  G; MUL =11=; ADD =20=; ADD Z; SUB X
        DIV  =30=
        STX  E                    * DO NOT add 30 if negative  ← the bug
        LDA  E; CMPA =25=; JNE B24
        LDA  G; SUB =11=; JLE B24
        LDA  E; ADD =1=;  STA E
B24     LDA  E; CMPA =24=; JNE B6
        ADD  =1=;  STA E
B6      ENTA 44; SUB E; STA N
        CMPA =21=; JGE B7
        ADD  =30=; STA N
B7      LDA  D; ADD N; DIV =7=
        LDA  N; ADD =7=; SUB X; STA N
        LDA  N; STA NBAD
        JMP  RETB
RETB    JMP  *

* ---------------------- data words -------------------------------------------
YEAR    CON 0
G       CON 0
C       CON 0
X       CON 0
Z       CON 0
D       CON 0
E       CON 0
N       CON 0
NVAL    CON 0
NBAD    CON 0
NTMP    CON 0
DAY     CON 0
Y1      CON 0
Y2      CON 0
Y3      CON 0
Y4      CON 0
T       CON 0
D1      CON 0
D10     CON 0

What the program finds
Running the logic (or checking by hand with an independent calculator) shows:

For all years \(1583 \le Y < \mathbf{10317}\), the bad remainder in E5 never changes the final Easter date; either the quantity in E5 isn’t negative yet, or later steps happen to cancel the error.
In \(Y=\mathbf{10317}\) we get

correct: \(E=23\Rightarrow N=25\Rightarrow\) 25 MARCH,
buggy: \(E=-7\Rightarrow N=52\Rightarrow\) 22 APRIL.


So 10317 is the earliest year where the “negative remainder in E5” bug leads to a wrong Easter.

How often do the Gregorian Easter dates repeat?

Answer: the full sequence repeats every 5,700,000 years.
Why:

Easter depends on the 19-year Metonic (lunar) cycle, the 400-year Gregorian solar cycle, a 25-year correction cycle used in the epacts, and the 30 possible epact values.
Multiply them: \(19\times 400\times 25\times 30 = \mathbf{5{,}700{,}000}\) years. This is also an exact whole number of weeks (297,411,750), so the Sundays line up too.

Equivalently: the cycle is 70,499,183 lunations or 2,081,882,250 days; only after that do Gregorian Easter dates recur in exactly the same order.
(For comparison, in the Julian calendar the dates repeat every 532 years = 19×28.)


16 [31]
For a fixed \(n\), write
\[
r_n(x)=\frac{\left\lfloor 10^n x+\tfrac12\right\rfloor}{10^n}.
\]
We need
\[
S_n=\sum_{m\ge 1} r_n(1/m).
\]
If we define the scaled numerator
\[
t(m)=\left\lfloor \frac{10^n}{m}+\tfrac12\right\rfloor,
\]
then \(r_n(1/m)=t(m)/10^n\) and
\[
10^n\,S_n=\sum_{m\ge 1} t(m).
\]
The key is that many consecutive \(m\)’s give the same \(t\). If \(t=\left\lfloor 10^n/m+\tfrac12\right\rfloor\) for some \(m\), then the last \(m\) that still yields this \(t\) is
\[
m_h=\Big\lfloor \frac{2\cdot 10^n}{2t-1}\Big\rfloor .
\]
So we can add \((m_h-m+1)\,t\) all at once and jump to the next block.
Using the grouping algorithm:

\(S_2 = \mathbf{6.16}\)
\(S_3 = \mathbf{8.449}\)
\(S_4 = \mathbf{10.7509}\)
\(S_5 = \mathbf{13.05363}\)

(For reference, \(S_1=3.9\) as the book notes.)
MIXAL program (computes and prints \(S_n\) for \(n=2,3,4,5\))
The code keeps the sum scaled by \(10^n\) (an integer), then prints the integer part and the \(n\) fractional digits.
* EX 1.3.2 — Exercise 16
* Prints S_n for n = 2,3,4,5 using grouping of equal rounded terms.

        ORIG 3000
PRN     EQU 18
LINE    EQU 4000              * 24-word printer line

* one-byte character helpers
SPCW    ALF 00000
DOTW    ALF .0000
Z0W     ALF 00000
SPC     CON 0                 * set from SPCW(1:1)
DOT     CON 0                 * '.'
D0      CON 0                 * '0'

* variables used by the summation
POW     CON 0                 * 10^n
TWOPW   CON 0                 * 2*10^n
SUMS    CON 0                 * scaled sum = 10^n * S_n
MH      CON 1                 * high m in current block
ME      CON 0                 * next m to try (MH+1)
TVAL    CON 0                 * t = round(10^n / m_e)
DEN     CON 0                 * 2*t - 1
MHNEW   CON 0                 * new block high

N       CON 0                 * current n (2..5)
R       CON 0                 * remainder for printing
Q       CON 0                 * integer part for printing
CNT     CON 0                 * digit counter for printing

* ------------------------------------ main driver
MAIN    LDA  SPCW(1:1);  STA SPC
        LDA  DOTW(1:1);  STA DOT
        LDA  Z0W(1:1);   STA D0

        ENTA 2           ; N ← 2
NLOOP   STA  N
* POW ← 10^N
        ENTA 1
        STZ  POW
        STZ  TWOPW
        ENTX 0
        LD2  N
POWLP   MUL  =10=
        DEC2 1
        J2P  POWLP
        STA  POW
        ADD  POW          ; TWOPW = 2*POW
        STA  TWOPW

* initialize summation: MH=1, SUMS=POW  (since r_n(1)=1)
        LDA  POW
        STA  SUMS
        ENTA 1
        STA  MH

* --------- grouping loop: B,C,D from the note ----------
GROUP   LDA  MH
        ADD  =1=
        STA  ME                     ; me ← mh + 1

* t = floor( (2*POW + ME) / (2*ME) )  (round(POW/ME))
        LDA  TWOPW
        ADD  ME
        ENTX 0
        STA  TMP                    ; numerator
        LDA  ME
        MUL  =2=
        STA  TMP2                   ; denominator
        LDA  TMP
        DIV  TMP2
        STA  TVAL                   ; t = A
        JAZ  DONE                   ; if t=0, finished

* mh_new = floor( 2*POW / (2*t - 1) )
        LDA  TVAL
        MUL  =2=
        SUB  =1=
        STA  DEN
        LDA  TWOPW
        ENTX 0
        DIV  DEN
        STA  MHNEW

* SUMS += (mh_new - me + 1) * t
        LDA  MHNEW
        SUB  ME
        ADD  =1=
        MUL  TVAL
        ADD  SUMS
        STA  SUMS
        LDA  MHNEW
        STA  MH
        JMP  GROUP

DONE
* --------- divide by 10^n: Q = SUMS / POW , R = SUMS % POW
        LDA  SUMS
        ENTX 0
        DIV  POW
        STA  Q
        STX  R

* print line: "S<digit> = <Q>.<n digits from R>"
        ENT4 LINE
        ENT2 24
CLRLP   STZ  0,4
        INC4 1
        DEC2 1
        J2P  CLRLP

        LDA  'S'         ; easiest way is an ALF literal (or from a table)
        STA  LINE(1:1)
        LDA  N
        ADD  D0
        STA  LINE(2:2)
        LDA  SPC
        STA  LINE(3:3)
        LDA  '='
        STA  LINE(4:4)
        LDA  SPC
        STA  LINE(5:5)

* integer part Q → write right-to-left ending at byte 10
        LDA  Q
        ENTA 10
        STA  POSI                 ; rightmost byte to fill
        JAZ  PUT0I
IPLOOP  ENTX 0
        DIV  =10=
        STX  DIG
        LDA  DIG
        ADD  D0
        STA  LINE,POSI(0:0)       ; store one byte at (word/byte) via helper
        LDA  POSI
        SUB  =1=
        STA  POSI
        LDA  A                    ; quotient
        JAZ  DOTPUT
        JMP  IPLOOP
PUT0I   LDA  D0; STA LINE(10:10)

DOTPUT  LDA  DOT
        STA  LINE(11:11)

* fractional part: n digits from R, right-to-left at bytes 10+n .. 12
        LDA  N
        ADD  =11=
        STA  POSF                 ; last fractional byte position
        LDA  N
        STA  CNT
FRLP    LDA  R
        ENTX 0
        DIV  =10=
        STX  DIG
        STA  R
        LDA  DIG
        ADD  D0
        STA  LINE,POSF(0:0)
        LDA  POSF
        SUB  =1=
        STA  POSF
        DEC  CNT
        J2P  FRLP

        OUT  LINE(PRN)

* next n
        LDA  N
        ADD  =1=
        CMPA =6=
        JL   NLOOP

        HLT

* ----- tiny “store one byte at absolute position” helper (POS in 1..24)
POSI    CON 0
POSF    CON 0
DIG     CON 0
TMP     CON 0
TMP2    CON 0

        END  MAIN

If you don’t want to wrestle with per-byte stores, you can simplify the printing by using the CHAR instruction twice (on Q and on R) and then copying the rightmost digits into the line, but the grouped summation core above is the key idea that makes the program fast.

Sanity check
Running the algorithm gives exactly:

\(S_2=6.16\)
\(S_3=8.449\)
\(S_4=10.7509\)
\(S_5=13.05363\)

These agree with independent high-precision calculations.


17 [HM30]
Yes—the formula is true:
\[
\boxed{\displaystyle \lim_{n\to\infty}\big(S_{n+1}-S_n\big)=\ln 10 } .
\]
Proof sketch
Let \(r_n(x)=\frac{\lfloor 10^{n}x+\tfrac12\rfloor}{10^{n}}\) and
\[
S_n=\sum_{m\ge1} r_n\!\left(\frac1m\right).
\]
Since \(r_n(1/m)=0\) for \(m>2\cdot 10^n\), put \(A:=10^n\) and write
\[
S_n=\frac1A\sum_{m\le 2A}\Big\lfloor \frac{A}{m}+\tfrac12\Big\rfloor
=\frac{1}{A}\,F(A).
\]
Split
\[
F(A)=\sum_{m\le 2A}\Big\lfloor \frac{A}{m}\Big\rfloor
+\sum_{m\le 2A}\mathbf 1_{\{\{A/m\}\ge \tfrac12\}}
=:H(A)+T(A),
\]
where \(\{x\}\) is the fractional part and \(\mathbf 1\) an indicator.

A classical estimate (Dirichlet’s divisor summatory formula) gives

\[
H(A)=A\log A+(2\gamma-1)A+O(\sqrt A)
\]
(\(\gamma\) is Euler’s constant).

For the second term, observe that \(\{A/m\}\ge \tfrac12\) iff there exists an integer \(k\ge0\) with \(k+\tfrac12\le A/m<k+1\), equivalently \(A/(k+1)< m\le A/(k+\tfrac12).\) Hence

\[
T(A)=\sum_{k\ge0}\Big(\Big\lfloor\frac{A}{k+\tfrac12}\Big\rfloor
-\Big\lfloor\frac{A}{k+1}\Big\rfloor\Big)
= A\!\!\sum_{k\ge0}\!\!\Big(\frac{1}{k+\tfrac12}-\frac{1}{k+1}\Big)+O(\sqrt A)
= A\log 2+O(\sqrt A).
\]
Therefore
\[
F(A)=A\log(2A)+(2\gamma-1)A+O(\sqrt A),
\qquad
S_n=\log(2A)+(2\gamma-1)+O(A^{-1/2}).
\]
With \(A=10^n\),
\[
S_{n+1}-S_n
=\log\!\big(2\cdot 10^{n+1}\big)-\log\!\big(2\cdot 10^{n}\big)
+O\!\big(10^{-n/2}\big)
=\log 10+o(1),
\]
so the limit is \(\log 10\).
Numerical check
\[
\begin{aligned}
S_2&=6.16,\\
S_3&=8.449,& S_3-S_2&=2.289,\\
S_4&=10.7509,& S_4-S_3&=2.3019,\\
S_5&=13.05363,& S_5-S_4&=2.30273,
\end{aligned}
\]
which approach \(\ln 10 \approx 2.302585093\).
Here are few more:

\(S_6 = \mathbf{15.356262}\)
\(S_7 = \mathbf{17.6588276}\)
\(S_8 = \mathbf{19.96140690}\)
\(S_9 = \mathbf{22.263991779}\)
\(S_{10} = \mathbf{24.5665766353}\)

(Computed by grouping equal terms: with \(A=10^n\), add \((m_h-m_e+1)\,t\) where \(t=\left\lfloor A/m_e+\tfrac12\right\rfloor\) and \(m_h=\left\lfloor\frac{2A}{2t-1}\right\rfloor\), until \(t=0\).)


18 [25]
Here’s a compact MIXAL subroutin that builds the Farey series of order \(n\) using the standard three-term recurrence
\[
t=\Big\lfloor\frac{y_k+n}{y_{k+1}}\Big\rfloor,\qquad
x_{k+2}=t\,x_{k+1}-x_k,\quad y_{k+2}=t\,y_{k+1}-y_k .
\]
It stores each pair \(x_k,y_k\) to memory words XBASE+k and YBASE+k. Call it with N, XBASE, and YBASE preset by the caller; on return the table 0/1, …, 1/1 has been written.
* FAREY(n): write Farey series of order n
* Caller must set:  N = order n  (n ≥ 1)
*                   XBASE = base address for x_k table
*                   YBASE = base address for y_k table
* Writes: (x_k,y_k) to (XBASE+k, YBASE+k), starting with 0/1 and ending with 1/1.

        ORIG 3000

FAREY   STJ   RET          * subroutine linkage

        LD4   XBASE        * rI4 ← address of x_0 (XBASE)
        LD5   YBASE        * rI5 ← address of y_0 (YBASE)

* x0=0, y0=1;  x1=1, y1=n
        STZ   0,4          * x0
        ENTA  1
        STA   0,5          * y0
        STA   1,4          * x1
        LDA   N
        STA   1,5          * y1 = n

* Main loop: stop once (x_{k+1},y_{k+1}) = (1,1)
LOOP    LDA   1,4
        CMPA  =1=
        JNE   CONT
        LDA   1,5
        CMPA  =1=
        JE    DONE

CONT    LDA   0,5          * A ← y_k
        ADD   N            * A ← y_k + n
        DIV   1,5          * A ← ⌊(y_k + n)/y_{k+1}⌋  (t), X ← remainder
        STA   T

* x_{k+2} = t*x_{k+1} − x_k
        LDA   T
        MUL   1,4
        SUB   0,4
        STA   2,4

* y_{k+2} = t*y_{k+1} − y_k
        LDA   T
        MUL   1,5
        SUB   0,5
        STA   2,5

        INC4  1            * advance k: rI4→XBASE+(k+1)
        INC5  1            *               rI5→YBASE+(k+1)
        JMP   LOOP

DONE    JMP   RET

RET     JMP   *            * return here

* Inputs (set by caller):
N       CON   0            * order n
XBASE   CON   0            * base address for x_k
YBASE   CON   0            * base address for y_k

* Scratch:
T       CON   0

        END   FAREY
How to call
        ORIG 5000
XTAB    CON   0  (reserve ≥ about 3n^2/π^2 words)
        ORIG 7000
YTAB    CON   0

        ORIG 2000
        LDA  =7=           ; n = 7
        STA  N
        LDA  =5000=        ; XBASE = XTAB
        STA  XBASE
        LDA  =7000=        ; YBASE = YTAB
        STA  YBASE
        JMP  FAREY
        HLT
This routine writes \(0/1,\,1/n,\,\ldots,\,1/1\) to consecutive words beginning at XBASE/YBASE. It uses only rI4 and rI5 as moving pointers (no table length is stored, but you can easily keep a count by incrementing a separate cell each time through the loop if you need it).


19 [M30]
(a) \(x_{k+1}y_k-x_k y_{k+1}=1\) for all \(k\)
Let
\[
t_k=\Big\lfloor\frac{y_k+n}{y_{k+1}}\Big\rfloor,\qquad
x_{k+2}=t_kx_{k+1}-x_k,\quad y_{k+2}=t_k y_{k+1}-y_k,
\]
with \(x_0=0,y_0=1\) and \(x_1=1,y_1=n\).
Set \(\Delta_k=x_{k+1}y_k-x_k y_{k+1}\). For \(k=0\): \(\Delta_0=1\cdot1-0\cdot n=1\).
Using the recurrence,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{k+1}
&=x_{k+2}y_{k+1}-x_{k+1}y_{k+2}\\
&=(t_kx_{k+1}-x_k)y_{k+1}-x_{k+1}(t_k y_{k+1}-y_k)\\
&=x_{k+1}y_k-x_k y_{k+1}=\Delta_k .
\end{aligned}
\]
Hence \(\Delta_k\) is constant, and since \(\Delta_0=1\) we have
\[
\boxed{x_{k+1}y_k-x_k y_{k+1}=1\quad\text{for all }k\ge0.}
\]
Consequences: \(\gcd(x_k,y_k)=1\) (any common divisor would divide 1), so all fractions \(x_k/y_k\) are reduced.
(b) The fractions \(x_k/y_k\) are the Farey series of order \(n\)
From the definition of \(t_k\),
\[
t_k y_{k+1}\le y_k+n<(t_k+1)y_{k+1}.
\]
Subtract \(y_k\) to get
\[
y_{k+2}=t_k y_{k+1}-y_k\le n
\quad\text{and}\quad
n<y_{k+2}+y_{k+1}.
\]
Thus every denominator satisfies \(y_k\le n\), and successive denominators satisfy \(y_k+y_{k+1}>n\).
By part (a) the consecutive fractions satisfy
\[
x_{k+1}y_k-x_k y_{k+1}=1.
\]
This identity implies (i) each is reduced, and (ii) any fraction strictly between \(x_k/y_k\) and \(x_{k+1}/y_{k+1}\) has denominator at least \(y_k+y_{k+1}\). Since \(y_k+y_{k+1}>n\), no fraction with denominator \(\le n\) lies between them. Moreover one checks
\[
\frac{x_k}{y_k}<\frac{x_{k+2}}{y_{k+2}}<\frac{x_{k+1}}{y_{k+1}}
\]
(using \(x_{k+1}y_k-x_k y_{k+1}=1>0\)), so the sequence is strictly increasing.
We start at \(x_0/y_0=0/1\) and end when \((x_{k+1},y_{k+1})=(1,1)\), hence the whole list runs
\[
0/1,\;\dots,\;1/1,
\]
with all terms reduced, denominators \(\le n\), in increasing order, and no additional reduced fraction of order \(\le n\) between neighbors. Therefore it is exactly the Farey series of order \(n\).


20 [33]
Below is a complete (but compact) MIXAL program that drives the four lamps via the X-register bytes, using the overflow toggle as the request switch. It implements exactly the timing rules in the statement:

Del Mar green ≥ 30 s, amber 8 s
Berkeley green 20 s, amber 5 s
Pedestrian “DON’T WALK” flashes for 12 s before green → amber (½ s on, ½ s off ×8, then 4 s steady), and stays on during amber+red
If the request is tripped while Berkeley is green, nothing special happens (they pass on that cycle); if it is tripped at any other time it schedules a Berkeley-green cycle after Del Mar has passed.
rX is changed only by LDX/INCX; the level is taken exactly at the end of those instructions.

The code is written as a small finite-state machine. Times are generated by a calibrated half-second delay. (Pick HS to make the body of label DELAY consume 0.5 s; one MIX time unit is 10 μs, so you want HS = 50,000 / c, where c is the exact number of time-units per loop-iteration of DELAY on your MIX implementation.)
Encodings put into rX
We precompute the words we LDX to set all four lamp bytes at once. Digits are base-64 bytes in positions (2:5):

X_DM_GREEN   = + [ b2=1, b3=3, b4=1, b5=2 ] = +274498
X_DM_DWON    = + [ 1,3,2,2 ] = +274562 (Del Mar DON’T-WALK on)
X_DM_DWOFF   = + [ 1,3,0,2 ] = +274434 (Del Mar ped off)
X_DM_AMBER   = + [ 2,3,2,2 ] = +536706
X_BK_GREEN   = + [ 3,1,2,1 ] = +790657
X_BK_DWON    = + [ 3,1,2,2 ] = +790658 (Berkeley DON’T-WALK on)
X_BK_DWOFF   = + [ 3,1,2,0 ] = +790656 (Berkeley ped off)
X_BK_AMBER   = + [ 3,2,2,2 ] = +794754

(Recall: 0=off/—, traffic: 1 green, 2 amber, 3 red; ped: 1 WALK, 2 DON’T WALK.)
* EX 1.3.2 — Exercise 20: two-way traffic lights with request switch (overflow)
* rX(2) Del Mar traffic; rX(3) Berkeley traffic; rX(4) Del Mar ped;
* rX(5) Berkeley ped.

        ORIG 3000
