Security policies (W⊕X). Operating systems increasingly enforce write-xor-execute: a page may be writable or executable, not both. That prevents code injection and many exploit techniques; it also means ordinary programs can’t (and shouldn’t) patch their own instruction bytes.
OS/ABI design: text is shared and read-only. Executable code (“text”) pages are mapped read-only and commonly shared among processes for memory efficiency. Letting programs write into those pages would break sharing and isolation.
Performance costs on modern CPUs. Pipelines and split instruction/data caches mean that when code is changed, the CPU must perform special synchronization/serialization and cache/TLB maintenance to make the modification visible to instruction fetch. That’s slow and error-prone if mishandled. Vendors explicitly warn about penalties and coherence requirements for self-modifying code.
Concurrency and reentrancy hazards. Multiple threads may execute the same code simultaneously. Patching instructions in place introduces race conditions, tears, and undefined behavior unless you add heavyweight synchronization—further eroding any perceived benefit. (Read-only text plus normal call/return linkage avoids this.)
Optimization, tooling, and maintainability. Compilers, linkers, profilers, verifiers, and JITs assume code is immutable at run time; immutability enables aggressive optimization, code signing, control-flow integrity, and reliable debugging. Self-modification undermines those assumptions and complicates reasoning about correctness.
Better idioms exist. Modern linkage stores return addresses in a register/stack frame (or a link register), not in the instruction stream; parameter passing and multiple exits are handled by calling conventions and structured control flow, so the MIX-style “patch the code to return here” is unnecessary. (Knuth foreshadows exactly this shift.)

Bottom line. What was once a practical trick on simple machines is now a liability: it conflicts with OS security and memory models, harms performance on cached/pipelined CPUs, breaks reentrancy, and fights today’s toolchains. Hence modern advice—and practice—is to avoid self-modifying code. The exercise list where this appears is on p. 193.

title: 1.4.2 Coroutines
text:
1 [10]
A coroutine is symmetric: two (or more) routines repeatedly transfer control to each other, each time resuming exactly where it last left off (state must persist across transfers). This is unlike a subroutine, where control flows down and then returns once.
Why “short and simple” is elusive
Minimal illustration (conceptual)
To genuinely demonstrate coroutines you need (i) more than one control transfer, (ii) preserved execution context, and (iii) meaningful data exchange. Each of these adds lines and machinery, so examples that are both short and truly illustrative are rare; once they’re short enough, they stop being distinctly coroutine examples and look like plain subroutines instead.

title: 1.4.2 Coroutines
text:
One transfer ⇒ not really a coroutine. If A calls B only once (and B never resumes A mid-run), the interaction is observationally identical to an ordinary subroutine call. So the “smallest” control exchange doesn’t demonstrate anything essentially coroutine-specific. To show coroutine behavior, you need multiple alternations (A↔︎B↔︎A↔︎B…), which already lengthens the example.
State must be preserved between activations. Real coroutine examples must keep local state alive across yields. On a bare machine (like MIX in the text) that means explicit linkage and register saves/restores—extra instructions that swell even toy programs. Knuth’s own coroutine linkage shows saving/restoring registers and jump handshakes, which are inherently not “tiny.” 
Nontrivial data flow is needed to be meaningful. To motivate why coroutines help, you typically show a producer/consumer, scanner/parser, or “turn an n-pass pipeline into a one-pass process” example. But as soon as you pass realistic data (buffers, termination conditions, etc.), the code grows beyond a few lines. (Knuth’s figure contrasting a four-pass pipeline with a one-pass chain of coroutines illustrates the typical use case.) 
Toy examples are either contrived or misleading. Extremely short “ping-pong” snippets (e.g., print A/B alternately) either hide the crucial save/restore machinery or rely on features (like a built-in yield) that the reader hasn’t met yet, making them pedagogically suspect on a machine-language canvas.



Subroutine pattern: main → sub() → return (one descent, one return).
Coroutine pattern: A → B → A → B → … (multiple resumptions), plus explicit context preservation. Without at least two transfers, A↔︎B collapses to subroutine semantics and doesn’t illuminate coroutines.

title: 1.4.2 Coroutines
text:
2 [20]
What actually happens if you start with IN

Initialization still sets rI6 ← 0 and rX ← 0 (lines 58–59), so IN1 will read the first input word if needed and isolate the next character into rA. 
At the end of its step, IN yields to OUT via the coroutine linkage (JMP OUT). Control lands at the OUT linkage stub, which first saves IN’s resume point and then transfers to OUT1. 
OUT1 begins by preparing a fresh output card/buffer: it sets up indices and executes a MOVE that blanks the output area (“Start new output card… Set output area to blanks”), then it asks IN for the “next translated character” by jumping back via the linkage (JMP IN). Those prologue instructions overwrite or otherwise invalidate the character that IN had already placed in rA. Net effect: the very first character fetched by IN is lost before OUT stores anything. 

This is exactly why the text starts with JMP OUT1: the OUT coroutine’s prologue should run before the first request to IN, so that the buffer is ready and the first character fetched by IN is immediately consumed instead of being clobbered by OUT’s setup. The official answer summarizes this as “the first character found by IN would be lost,” and notes that lines 58–59 were chosen to initialize IN; starting with IN would require compensating initialization for OUT. 
How to make “start with IN” work (one correct fix)
If you truly want to begin at IN1, adjust the first handoff into OUT so OUT’s prologue does not run after IN has already put a character in rA:

Pre-initialize OUT’s state that the prologue would otherwise need. Concretely, set rA ← −16 so the initial MOVE count is already in place, and clear the output buffer if it isn’t known to be blank.
Change the first transfer from IN to OUT to jump directly to the code that stores rA into the buffer (the code around line 39) rather than to OUT1 (the prologue). In the book’s notation: modify the first OUTX jump target so it goes to the “store character” logic rather than the “start new card” logic. With that, the very first character produced by IN will be preserved and packed before any further OUT setup runs. 

Bottom line

If you simply replace JMP OUT1 with JMP IN1, the first input character disappears.
To start with IN safely, you must (i) pre-initialize OUT’s prologue effects and (ii) route the first transfer from IN straight into OUT’s “store character” code rather than OUT’s prologue.

title: 1.4.2 Coroutines
text:
3 [20]
False (but “almost” true). Removing all three comparisons breaks correctness in two distinct ways:

The comparison indicator (CI) can be uninitialized at the first JE 9F

In the OUT coroutine the test for end-of-input is done by
… STA …(1:1)
CMPA PERIOD
JE  9F
…
… STA …(2:2)
CMPA PERIOD
JE  9F
…
… STA …(3:3)
CMPA PERIOD
JE  9F
…
9F  JNE OUT1
HLT
(see lines 39–55). Each JE 9F assumes the CI was set by the immediately preceding CMPA PERIOD. If you delete those compares, the very first JE in OUT will consult whatever CI happens to be left over from earlier code (i.e., undefined for some paths), so behavior is not well-defined. In particular, if the first input character is a letter, coroutine IN takes the “nondigit” branch without doing any comparison at all and jumps to OUT; then OUT reaches JE 9F with an undefined CI. Hence correctness already fails. 

A final period after a replication digit isn’t detected

Even when the CI happens to be set, dropping those compares misses the special case “digit followed by final ‘.’”. Coroutine IN reads a replication digit n, then fetches the next character (which might be the terminating ‘.’) and immediately jumps to OUT without doing any compare in between (see lines 24–30). If ‘.’ is preceded by a replication digit, the most recent comparison in IN was the digit test (not a period test), so the CI is not “equal”; without CMPA PERIOD in OUT, the program won’t notice the final period and won’t halt. 
Knuth’s official notes summarize both issues succinctly: removing all three compares is “almost” okay, but “the comparison indicator isn’t initialized; and if the final period is preceded by a replication digit, it won’t be noticed.” 
If you really want to remove the three compares
You can relocate the period test so the CI is reliably set before OUT checks it:

After IN fetches the next character (between lines 26–27), insert a single CMPA PERIOD so the CI reflects whether the just-fetched character is ‘.’ before control jumps to OUT.
Also ensure the CI has a known initial state right before the very first handoff (between lines 59–60), e.g., by comparing rX with PERIOD (one way is to insert a CMPX PERIOD there), so OUT’s first JE/JNE pair isn’t consulting a garbage CI. (Then OUT can safely rely on the CI without doing its own per-position CMPA PERIOD.) 

This preserves the coroutine contract while shaving a few instructions from OUT, but the stock program (with the three local CMPA PERIODs) is the clearest and safest. 
Don’t delete all three CMPA PERIOD in OUT; doing so makes the program incorrect on real inputs.

title: 1.4.2 Coroutines
text:
4 [20]
What “linkage (1)” is doing in MIX
Knuth’s linkage (labels rearranged slightly for clarity) is the tiny handshake at the end of his program:
60  JMP OUT1        ; start with OUT  (exercise 2)
61  OUT  STJ INX    ; store our resume point "OUTX" into IN's jump word
62  OUTX JMP OUT1   ; jump to OUT's entry (will be patched by IN)
63  IN   STJ OUTX   ; ditto, in the other direction
64  INX  JMP IN1    ; jump to IN's entry (will be patched by OUT)
Each coroutine owns one indirect jump word (INX, OUTX). When a coroutine yields, it stores the address of its own resume label (e.g., OUTX) into the other coroutine’s jump word, then jumps to the other’s entry (IN1 ⇄ OUT1). The first time, INX and OUTX contain the entry addresses; thereafter they contain the saved resume addresses. (Knuth later shows a variant “(4)” that also saves/restores register A, but the basic idea above is “(1)”.) 
Below are two concrete, modern implementations that are directly analogous but avoid self-modifying code by using indirect branches through writable data.
x86-64 (NASM/Intel syntax; position-independent)
; Assemble with: nasm -felf64 coro.asm && ld -o coro coro.o
default rel

section .data
INX:   dq IN1        ; IN’s current continuation (entry at first)
OUTX:  dq OUT1       ; OUT’s current continuation (entry at first)

section .text
global _start

_start:
    jmp OUT1         ; Like line 60: begin in OUT

; --- IN coroutine -------------------------------------------------

; Entry for IN: always tail-jump to whatever OUT stored into INX
IN1:
    jmp qword [INX]

; Somewhere inside IN, to yield to OUT do:
;   1) store our resume address into OUTX
;   2) jump to OUT’s entry
IN_yield_to_OUT:
    lea  rax, [rel IN_resume]  ; “return address” for IN
    mov  [OUTX], rax
    jmp  OUT1
IN_resume:
    ; ... execution continues here when OUT yields back ...

; --- OUT coroutine ------------------------------------------------

; Entry for OUT: always tail-jump to whatever IN stored into OUTX
OUT1:
    jmp qword [OUTX]

; To yield back to IN:
OUT_yield_to_IN:
    lea  rax, [rel OUT_resume] ; “return address” for OUT
    mov  [INX], rax
    jmp  IN1
OUT_resume:
    ; ... execution continues here when IN yields back ...
Why this is exactly like (1):

IN1: jmp [INX] and OUT1: jmp [OUTX] play the role of the “indirect jump words” (INX JMP IN1, OUTX JMP OUT1).
IN_yield_to_OUT stores IN_resume into OUTX before jumping to OUT1; OUT_yield_to_IN stores OUT_resume into INX before jumping to IN1.
No stacks or OS help are required; you’re literally passing continuations in memory, just as MIX does by patching a jump word.

If you need register/save semantics like Knuth’s variant (4), push/pop (or move to callee-saved registers/stack slots) around the mov/jmp pair, corresponding to STA HOLDA … LDA HOLDA in his text. 
Portable C (GCC/Clang) with “labels as values”
This uses the compiler extension for computed goto. It mirrors MIX even more transparently:
// gcc -std=gnu11 coro.c -o coro
#include <stdio.h>

static void *INX, *OUTX;

int main(void) {
  INX  = &&IN1;      // initial entries
  OUTX = &&OUT1;

  goto OUT1;         // start with OUT (like line 60)

IN1:
  goto *INX;         // always jump to IN’s current continuation

  // ... IN’s code ...
IN_yield_to_OUT:
  OUTX = &&IN_resume;  // store our resume label into OUT’s jump slot
  goto OUT1;           // jump to OUT’s entry
IN_resume:
  // ... resumes here ...

OUT1:
  goto *OUTX;        // always jump to OUT’s current continuation

  // ... OUT’s code ...
OUT_yield_to_IN:
  INX = &&OUT_resume;  // store our resume label into IN’s jump slot
  goto IN1;            // jump to IN’s entry
OUT_resume:
  // ... resumes here ...
}

INX/OUTX are the indirect jump words.
goto *INX / goto *OUTX replace the “JMP IN1/JMP OUT1 words patched by STJ”.
Assigning &&label is the exact analogue of STJ otherX.

This version is often the simplest way to prototype Knuth’s linkage today, and it avoids any system-dependent assembly.

title: 1.4.2 Coroutines
text:
5 [15]
Whenever OUT executes JMP IN, rA in OUT must be exactly what it was when control returns to the next OUT instruction; likewise for IN when it executes JMP OUT. So each coroutine must (1) save its own rA at exit, and (2) restore its own rA at re-entry, without constraining the other coroutine’s use of rA while it runs.
Knuth already shows the one-sided variant (“if OUT would destroy A, save/restore A around the transfer”), right above Fig. 22; we just symmetrize that pattern. We splice this linkage where the book places the standard two-coroutine linkage (lines 57–65), replacing those four short lines with the version below.
MIX code (two-sided A preservation)
* --- Linkage data (two save slots, one per coroutine) ---
HOLDIN  CON  0        * last-saved rA for IN
HOLDOUT CON  0        * last-saved rA for OUT

* --- Initialization and linkage ---
START   ENT6 0        * as in text: init rI6 for NEXTCHAR
        ENTX 0        * as in text: init rX for NEXTCHAR
        JMP  OUT1     * start with OUT (exercise 2 discusses starting with IN)

OUT     STJ  INX      * standard coroutine bookkeeping (return for IN)
        STA  HOLDIN   * save caller's A (leaving IN -> coming here)
        LDA  HOLDOUT  * restore OUT's own A for re-entry
OUTX    JMP  OUT1     * enter OUT proper

IN      STJ  OUTX     * standard coroutine bookkeeping (return for OUT)
        STA  HOLDOUT  * save caller's A (leaving OUT -> coming here)
        LDA  HOLDIN   * restore IN's own A for re-entry
INX     JMP  IN1      * enter IN proper

        END  START
Why this works

When OUT → IN (JMP IN inside OUT): control lands at label IN. We immediately

save the caller’s A (STA HOLDOUT), then
restore IN’s previously saved A (LDA HOLDIN), and jump to IN1. While IN runs, it may freely clobber rA. When IN later yields back to OUT (JMP OUT), label OUT restores OUT’s A from HOLDOUT before resuming at OUT1. Thus rA in OUT is unchanged across the yield/return pair.

Symmetrically, when IN → OUT (JMP OUT inside IN), label OUT saves IN’s rA to HOLDIN, restores OUT’s rA from HOLDOUT, and resumes OUT. When OUT yields back, label IN reloads HOLDIN. So rA in IN is likewise unchanged across its yield/return.

This is exactly the two-sided generalization of the one-sided example on p. 198 (“if OUT would destroy rA, save at IN and restore at OUT”), but now with two save cells so we can save the caller’s A and simultaneously restore the callee’s A at each transfer.

title: 1.4.2 Coroutines
text:
6 [22]
Here’s a clean coroutine linkage for three MIX coroutines \(A,B,C\) so that any one can transfer to either of the other two, and on activation each resumes exactly where it last left off. (This is the Exercise 6 setup at the end of §1.4.2.) 
First, recall the 2-coroutine pattern (called “(1)” in the text): each coroutine has a resume cell holding a JMP whose address field gets patched by the other coroutine via STJ. Then you enter a coroutine by executing that stored jump (i.e., JMP OUTX executes the word at OUTX).
We generalize that idea by giving each coroutine its own resume cell (AX, BX, CX) and by providing two entry labels per coroutine—one entry for each possible caller—so we know which caller’s resume cell to update.
Linkage (MIX)
* Resume cells (initial jumps to first-time entries A1, B1, C1)
AX      JMP A1
BX      JMP B1
CX      JMP C1

* Entries to B
AB      STJ AX       * called B from A: remember A’s return point in AX
        JMP BX       * now execute B’s stored jump (first time → B1)

CB      STJ CX       * called B from C: remember C’s return point in CX
        JMP BX

* Entries to C
AC      STJ AX       * called C from A: remember A’s return point
        JMP CX

BC      STJ BX       * called C from B: remember B’s return point
        JMP CX

* Entries to A
BA      STJ BX       * called A from B: remember B’s return point
        JMP AX

CA      STJ CX       * called A from C: remember C’s return point
        JMP AX
How you use it

From inside A, jump to AB to activate B, or to AC to activate C.
From inside B, jump to BA (activate A) or BC (activate C).
From inside C, jump to CA (A) or CB (B).

Why it works (invariants)

Each coroutine Q has a resume cell QX that always contains a JMP <where Q should resume>.
When caller P transfers to callee Q, control arrives at entry label QP, which executes STJ PX, storing the caller’s return address (the instruction after the JMP QP) into PX. Thus the caller’s resume cell is freshly patched to “where to continue when we next execute JMP PX.”
The entry then JMPs to QX, executing Q’s stored JMP—first time it jumps to Q1; thereafter it jumps to Q’s last suspended point.

You can verify a round-trip: A → JMP AB → AB: STJ AX; JMP BX (B begins); later B → JMP BA → BA: STJ BX; JMP AX which executes the patched word at AX and resumes A precisely after its JMP AB.

title: 1.4.2 Coroutines
text:
7 [30]
What the original program did (we must invert it)

Input format (2): a stream of characters ending with a period “.”; if a digit n∈{0,…,9} appears, it means “repeat the next character n+1 times,” even if that next character is itself a digit. Nondigits simply denote themselves. 
Output format (3): the fully expanded character stream, printed in groups of three separated by blanks, 16 groups per card; processing stops at the period. 

Our task is the inverse: read the grouped stream (3), ignore blanks, stop at the first period, and re-encode it into the compact code (2) with the fewest characters (e.g., never emit the redundant 0Z, just Z). 
Minimal re-encoding strategy (correctness & optimality)
Let’s call the flat stream (with blanks removed) S, ending at the first .. We want the shortest string over the alphabet Σ ∪ {0,…,9} whose expansion (by “digit→repeat next char n+1 times”) equals S.
Process S run by run: for each maximal block of identical characters c of length L, output the shortest code for that block, then continue.

If c is a nondigit (letter, punctuation, etc.):

We may either write the character itself once (cost 1) or use a “digit + c” pair (cost 2) to say multiple copies.
Optimal encoding is greedy by tens:

Let q = ⌊L/10⌋ and r = L mod 10.
Output q pairs 9c (each pair contributes 10 copies).
For the remainder:

If r = 0: done.
If r = 1: just output c (better than 0c).
If 2 ≤ r ≤ 10: output (r−1)c.



If c is a digit (one of 0..9 appearing as data), we cannot write it bare (because bare digits in (2) are operators). We must always use a pair:

Again q = ⌊L/10⌋, r = L mod 10.
Output q times 9c; if r>0, output (r−1)c.


Why this greedy scheme is optimal:

Each “digit+char” token has fixed cost 2 and yields between 1 and 10 copies (specifically n+1 copies). For nondigit characters we also have a special 1-copy option of cost 1 (just the character). This is a canonical coin system: “coins” of values 1..10 with costs 2 each, except a free single-copy option of cost 1 for nondigits. Greedy by 10s, then one optimal remainder, is optimal under such costs; any non-greedy split that uses more tokens (or replaces a single bare c by 0c) cannot be shorter.

This guarantees “the zero before the Z … would not really be produced” (we would output just Z) since 0Z (cost 2) is strictly worse than Z (cost 1). 
MIX solution architecture
We mirror Knuth’s i/o organization:

NEXTCHAR: reuse the input subroutine from the text that returns the next nonblank character from card reader into rA; it handles card boundaries and blanks. (Exactly as in the example: read card, scan words, extract next character.) 
PUTCH: an output subroutine that appends one character to an 80-col output buffer; when full, punch the card and clear the buffer (similar to OUT’s buffering, but without inserting blanks every three chars). The last card is punched when we see ..
MAIN (reverse encoder):

Read first nonblank char x via NEXTCHAR.
If x='.': output . and finish.
Set CUR=x, CNT=1.
Loop: get next x via NEXTCHAR.

If x='.': flush run (CUR, CNT) using EmitRun (below), then output . and finish.
Else if x==CUR: CNT++.
Else: flush (CUR, CNT), then CUR=x, CNT=1.

EmitRun(CUR, CNT) implements the minimal strategy above, using helper predicates “is digit?” (compare rA with codes 30..39) and a small loop to emit 9CUR q times; remainder as per rules.


Below is a compact MIXAL skeleton that focuses on the core logic (you can drop it straight into your MIXAL environment; i/o linkage follows the style of §1.4.2):
* REVERSE OF (3) -> (2): minimal code
* Units and buffers
READER  EQU     16
PUNCH   EQU     17
INPUT   ORIG    *+16        * input card buffer
OUTPUT  ORIG    *+16        * output card buffer

* =============== SUBROUTINE: NEXTCHAR (nonblank reader) ===============
* rA <- next nonblank char from input; reads new card as needed
* (Same as text's NEXTCHAR)
NEXTCHAR STJ     NRET
         JXNZ    GOTX        * rX holds leftover chars from last word
INIT     J6N     READ        * rI6=0 => need a new card
READ     IN      INPUT(READER)
         JBUS    *(READER)
         ENN6    16          * rI6 points to first word
GOTX     LDX     INPUT+16,6  * rX <- next word
         INC6    1
         ENTA    0
         SLAX    1           * next char -> rA (low byte)
NRET     JANZ    *            * skip blanks
         JMP     NEXTCHAR+1

* =============== SUBROUTINE: PUTCH (buffer & punch) ===================
* input: rA has a character to append
* uses rI4 as OUTPUT pointer: -16,-15,...,-1 then 0 triggers punch
PUTCH    STJ     PRET
         STA     OUTPUT+16,4(1:1)     * store to pos (1:1) of word at rI4
         INC4    1
         J4N     PRET
         OUT     OUTPUT(PUNCH)
         JBUS    *(PUNCH)
         ENT4    -16
         MOVE    -1,1(16)             * blank buffer
PRET     JMP     *

* =============== MAIN ==================================================
START    ENT6    0            * init reader state for NEXTCHAR (as in text)
         ENTX    0
         ENT4    -16          * init output pointer
         MOVE    -1,1(16)     * blank output buffer

         JMP     NEXTCHAR
         CMPA    PERIOD
         JE      FINISH_NOW   * empty stream?
         STA     CUR
         ENTA    1
         STA     CNT

MAINLP   JMP     NEXTCHAR
         CMPA    PERIOD
         JE      FLUSH_LAST
         CMPA    CUR
         JE      INCR
         * new run
         LDA     CUR
         LDX     CNT
         JMP     EMITRUN
         STA     CUR          * EMITRUN returns with rA=CUR', rX=CNT'
         ENTA    1
         STA     CNT
         JMP     MAINLP

INCR     ENTA    CNT
         INC     1
         STA     CNT
         JMP     MAINLP

FLUSH_LAST
         LDA     CUR
         LDX     CNT
         JMP     EMITRUN
         * emit '.' and finish
FINISH_NOW
         ENTA    PERIOD(5:5)  * just the '.' character
         JMP     PUTCH
         OUT     OUTPUT(PUNCH)
         HLT

* =============== EMITRUN(CUR in rA, CNT in rX) ========================
* Emits minimal code for CNT copies of char CUR.
* On return: none (caller reloads CUR/CNT for next run).
EMITRUN  STJ     ERRET
         STA     CCHAR
         STX     CCNT

         * is CUR a digit?  (MIX digit codes 30..39)
         LDA     CCHAR
         DECA    30
         JGE     CHECKHI
NOTDIG   * --- NONDIGIT BRANCH ---
         * q = CNT/10
         ENTA    10
         STA     TEN
         LDA     CCNT
         DIV     TEN           * rA <- q, rX <- r
         STA     Q
         STX     R
         * emit q times '9 CUR'
QLOOP    LDA     Q
         JE      REM
         ENTA    39            * '9'
         JMP     PUTCH
         LDA     CCHAR
         JMP     PUTCH
         DEC     Q,1
         JMP     QLOOP
REM      LDA     R
         JE      ERRET         * r==0
         J1Z     SINGLE        * r==1 -> single CUR
         DEC     R,1           * r in 2..9 -> (r-1)
         ADD     29            * convert 1..8 to digits 1..8 (codes 31..38)
         JMP     PUTCH
         LDA     CCHAR
         JMP     PUTCH
         JMP     ERRET
SINGLE   LDA     CCHAR
         JMP     PUTCH
         JMP     ERRET

CHECKHI  CMPA    =9=           * compare (CUR-30) with 9
         JG      NOTDIG        * >9 means not a digit actually
         * --- DIGIT BRANCH ---
         LDA     CCNT
         DIV     TEN           * q <- rA, r <- rX
         STA     Q
         STX     R
QDLOOP   LDA     Q
         JE      RPART
         ENTA    39            * '9'
         JMP     PUTCH
         LDA     CCHAR
         JMP     PUTCH
         DEC     Q,1
         JMP     QDLOOP
RPART    LDA     R
         JE      ERRET
         DEC     R,1
         ADD     29            * 0..8 -> 0..8 digits (codes 30..38); r=1 -> '0'
         JMP     PUTCH
         LDA     CCHAR
         JMP     PUTCH
ERRET    JMP     *

* Data
CUR      CON     0
CNT      CON     0
CCHAR    CON     0
CCNT     CON     0
Q        CON     0
R        CON     0
TEN      CON     10
PERIOD   ALF     '    .'

         END     START
Notes and alignment with the text

Input side: NEXTCHAR is exactly the “nonblank character” reader used in the coroutine example (read card, step through 5-char fields, skip blanks). 
Output side: Unlike OUT in the text (which groups in threes and inserts blanks), our PUTCH just packs characters into an 80-column buffer and punches on full or at the end; the exercise asks to produce cards like (2), i.e., no grouping.
Termination: We stop at the first period, mirroring the original semantics.

Walk-through on Knuth’s example
Given output (3)
ABB BEE EEE E44 446 66F GZY W22 220 0PQ 999 999 999 R.
Ignoring blanks, the stream ends at the first .. Runs include A(1), B(2), E(3), 4(2), 6(3), F(1), G(1), Z(1), Y(1), W(1), 2(2), 0(3), P(1), Q(1), 9(3), 9(3), 9(3), R(1).

For nondigits (e.g., Z(1)), we output the char itself (Z), never 0Z.
For digits-as-data (e.g., 0(3)), we must use pairs: 2 copies beyond the first encoded as (3−1)=2 → '2' so emit 20. More generally, runs ≥10 break as 9c blocks + remainder.

Composing all runs yields exactly a minimal string of the form (2), and as required, the stray 0Z will not appear in our output.

title: 1.4.3.1 A MIX simulator
text:
1 [14]
What FCHECK does, and where it’s used
Diagnosis
Across the simulator:
That’s a textbook case for Step-3 refactoring: push the common pre/post work into a single place and remove redundant calls.
A better organization (two practical refactorings)
You can do either (A) “pre-decode F once per instruction that needs (L:R)” or (B) “absorb/inline the decode into the two places that actually need it.” Both obey Step-3.
This removes all duplicate decoding in instructions that use the same (L:R) twice (e.g., COMPARE) and also avoids decoding in instructions whose F–field isn’t a partial field (e.g., JUMP, ADDROP). It follows Knuth’s Step-3 guidance exactly: enlarge the central “control” routine slightly to relieve its callees of repeated setup.
If you prefer not to touch the control path or tables:
This keeps code simple, keeps COMPARE optimal, and respects Step-3 (“perhaps several subroutines should be merged into one; or perhaps a subroutine is called only once and should not be a subroutine at all”).
Why this is better
Concrete changes you’d make
Either approach solves Exercise 1: you’ve studied all uses of FCHECK, noticed the duplicated patterns right after it, and reorganized so the decode happens once (or is inlined where it belongs), exactly the kind of Step-3 improvement Knuth advocates.

title: 1.4.3.1 A MIX simulator
text:
Purpose. FCHECK decodes the F–field (in rI3) as a partial field specification of the form \(8L + R\), ensures \(L \le R \le 5\), then leaves the results in convenient places: it stores \(L\) and \(R\) in memory cells L and R, and sets rI1 ← L. (If invalid, it jumps to FERROR.)
In GETV. The value-extraction subroutine begins by calling FCHECK, then fetches memory (MEMORY), and finally uses L and R to select the requested field and normalize the sign. (If the sign isn’t included, it forces the sign to +.)
In STORE. The store routine also begins with FCHECK, then uses L/R to splice the source register into the destination word—handling the special case when the sign-byte (i.e., L=0) is included.
In COMPARE. The program intentionally avoids calling FCHECK twice: it calls GETV (which runs FCHECK once), then enters GETV through the special entrance GETAV (which jumps past the FCHECK code) to reuse the already-decoded L. This is the one place where the code is already organized to keep the FCHECK overhead to a single call.
The exercise note. The exercise explicitly points you to “step 3 (Reexamination)” at the end of §1.4.1, which suggests enlarging or merging subroutines when the caller always does the same standard setup/cleanup around them. The exercise statement itself appears at the bottom of the simulator section.




FCHECK is always followed immediately by very similar boilerplate in its callers (e.g., loading -R into rI2, checking whether the sign is included via L==0, then doing standard shifts).
Only COMPARE already amortizes FCHECK over two uses of the same field (memory and register) by using the GETAV entrance.





Pre-decode once (preferred if you’re willing to touch the control path)


Mark which opcodes use (L:R). Add a small parallel table to OPTABLE that classifies the F–field usage of each opcode (e.g., 0 = not a partial field, 1 = partial field (L:R), 2 = shift count, …). The control routine already fetches F into rI3 (line 027). Use your new table to decide whether to decode now.
Decode centrally. If the current opcode’s F–usage is “partial field,” call a beefed-up decoder once and for all. That decoder should:

Validate and compute L and R (as FCHECK does).
Also set rI2 ← −R since every client immediately needs it.
Optionally set a one-byte flag SFLAG ← (L==0) so clients don’t have to re-test.

Use “prepared” entries.

Change callers that currently do JMP GETV to JMP GETAV (the already-prepared entrance) because rI1/R are now set; GETAV already jumps past the FCHECK block.
Split STORE into two entries: keep STORE (old behavior) and add STORE1 right after the FCHECK call site; have the opcode table jump to STORE1 when the control routine has pre-decoded F. This removes the FCHECK call in the common path.




Inline/merge (FCHECK → callers) with a macro (minimal edits)



Delete FCHECK as a runtime subroutine and replace it by an assembly macro (say, DECODEF) that expands to the 12 lines of its body (compute L, R, checks, set rI1). Expand DECODEF at the start of GETV (before label 1F) and at the start of STORE. Also have the macro optionally do LD2N R so all callers get rI2 “for free.”
Keep GETAV unchanged so COMPARE still jumps past the macro expansion in GETV and benefits from a single decode.




Fewer calls / simpler invariants. Operators that need fields no longer pay to rediscover L/R multiple times, and they get the always-needed rI2 ← −R (and optionally SFLAG) precomputed. See how GETV and STORE currently repeat the same checks right after FCHECK.
Matches the one existing optimization. COMPARE already factors out the second decode via GETAV; the refactor generalizes that cleanliness instead of being a one-off.
Faithful to Step-3. It’s precisely the “enlarge/merge subroutines” advice Knuth gives after completing a first cut: push common pre/post code down so the outer structure is cleaner.



Control routine (option A): after LD3 INST(4:4) (line 027), if the opcode’s F–class is “(L:R)”, call FDECODE (your enhanced FCHECK) which sets L, R, rI1=L, rI2=−R, and SFLAG. Then have the opcode table point to GETAV (instead of GETV) and to STORE1 (the post-decode entry).
GETV: remove the prologue call to FCHECK; enter at the existing label after that call (GETAV already does exactly this).
STORE: add label STORE1 at the point right after the original JMP FCHECK and retarget the table to STORE1 when pre-decoding is in effect; otherwise fall back to STORE for compatibility.

title: 1.4.3.1 A MIX simulator
text:
2 [20]
Here’s a clean, self-contained SHIFT routine for the MIX simulator. It plugs into the program exactly where opcode 6 (“SHIFT”) is dispatched in the operator table (see the listing that maps F=0..5 to SLA, SRA, SLAX, SRAX, SLC, SRC). I keep A/X signs in SIGNA/SIGNX and only rearrange magnitudes in AREG/XREG, matching the simulator’s data layout.
What the routine must do (recap)

F=0: SLA m — shift A left by m bytes, zero-fill on the right; sign unchanged.
F=1: SRA m — shift A right by m bytes, zero-fill on the left; sign unchanged.
F=2: SLAX m — shift the (A,X) 10-byte pair left by m, zero-fill; signs unchanged.
F=3: SRAX m — shift the (A,X) pair right by m, zero-fill; signs unchanged.
F=4: SLC m — rotate the (A,X) pair left by m (circular); signs unchanged.
F=5: SRC m — rotate the (A,X) pair right by m (circular); signs unchanged. Knuth’s exercise simply asks us to “write the SHIFT routine… (operation code 6).”

MIX code
* ------------------------------------------------------------
* SHIFT — opcode 6
* Uses: rI3 = F (variant 0..5), rI5 = M (shift count, possibly signed)
* Touches: AREG/XREG magnitudes only; SIGNA/SIGNX unchanged
* Notes: If tracking timing exactly, add |M| to TIME in this routine,
*        since OPTABLE contributes the base 2u already for SHIFT.
* ------------------------------------------------------------
SHIFT   ENT2    0,5         rI2 ← M (shift count)
        J2NN    *+2         make rI2 ← |M|
        ENN2    0,2
        CMP3    =5=         F must be 0..5
        JG      CYCLE       (defensive: treat out-of-range F as NOP)

        JMP     SHTAB,3     dispatch by F

* --- table of tiny trampolines (same pattern used elsewhere) ---
SHTAB   JMP     S_SLA
        JMP     S_SRA
        JMP     S_SLAX
        JMP     S_SRAX
        JMP     S_SLC
        JMP     S_SRC

* --- F=0: SLA m (A only) ---
S_SLA   LDA     AREG
        SLA     0,2         shift A left by rI2 bytes
        STA     AREG
        JMP     S_DONE

* --- F=1: SRA m (A only) ---
S_SRA   LDA     AREG
        SRA     0,2         shift A right by rI2 bytes
        STA     AREG
        JMP     S_DONE

* --- F=2: SLAX m (A,X together) ---
S_SLAX  LDA     AREG
        LDX     XREG
        SLAX    0,2         shift (A,X) left by rI2 bytes
        STA     AREG
        STX     XREG
        JMP     S_DONE

* --- F=3: SRAX m (A,X together) ---
S_SRAX  LDA     AREG
        LDX     XREG
        SRAX    0,2         shift (A,X) right by rI2 bytes
        STA     AREG
        STX     XREG
        JMP     S_DONE

* --- F=4: SLC m (A,X circular left) ---
S_SLC   LDA     AREG
        LDX     XREG
        SLC     0,2         rotate (A,X) left by rI2 bytes
        STA     AREG
        STX     XREG
        JMP     S_DONE

* --- F=5: SRC m (A,X circular right) ---
S_SRC   LDA     AREG
        LDX     XREG
        SRC     0,2         rotate (A,X) right by rI2 bytes
        STA     AREG
        STX     XREG
        JMP     S_DONE

* --- wrap up; optional timing bump here ---
S_DONE  * If desired:  TIME ← TIME + rI2  (|M|)
        JMP     CYCLE
Why this is correct (brief reasoning)

Signs preserved. The simulator stores signs separately (SIGNA, SIGNX). We only load/store magnitudes from AREG/XREG, so shifting cannot disturb signs.
Zero-fill vs rotate. Host MIX’s SLA/SRA/SLAX/SRAX zero-fill the vacated bytes; SLC/SRC rotate within the 10-byte rAX pair. That exactly matches MIX semantics for opcode 6 variants.
Large or negative counts. We take |M|. For SLA/SRA beyond the register width, the host shift yields zeros (desired). For rotations, host SLC/SRC behave modulo 10 bytes, so any M works sensibly.
Timing (if modeled). The OPTABLE entry already accounts for the base “2u” per SHIFT; if you’re simulating time precisely, add |M| here to reflect per-byte steps. (Knuth’s listing shows SHIFT’s table entry and the pattern for per-op timing updates.)

*  SHIFT operators (opcode = 6)
*  Uses rI3 = F, rI5 = M (already set by the control routine).
*  Count = |M|; signs are preserved by MIX shift ops automatically.

SHIFT   DEC3    5           Is F > 5?
        J3P     FERROR      If so, invalid F for SHIFT.

        LD1     M(1:2)      rI1 ← |M| (shift count, magnitude of M).

        JMP     SFTABL,3    Dispatch to SLA/SRA/SLAX/SRAX/SLC/SRC.

* --- F = 0 : SLA (shift left rA) ---
SLAOP   LDA     AREG
        SLA     0,1         Shift rA left by rI1 bytes, zero-fill right.
        STA     AREG
        JMP     CYCLE

* --- F = 1 : SRA (shift right rA) ---
SRAOP   LDA     AREG
        SRA     0,1         Shift rA right by rI1 bytes, zero-fill left.
        STA     AREG
        JMP     CYCLE

* --- F = 2 : SLAX (shift left rAX as 10-byte word) ---
SLAXOP  LDA     AREG
        LDX     XREG
        SLAX    0,1         Shift rAX left by rI1 bytes, zero-fill right.
        STA     AREG
        STX     XREG
        JMP     CYCLE

* --- F = 3 : SRAX (shift right rAX) ---
SRAXOP  LDA     AREG
        LDX     XREG
        SRAX    0,1         Shift rAX right by rI1 bytes, zero-fill left.
        STA     AREG
        STX     XREG
        JMP     CYCLE

* --- F = 4 : SLC (circular left rAX) ---
SLCOP   LDA     AREG
        LDX     XREG
        SLC     0,1         Circular left shift of rAX by rI1 bytes.
        STA     AREG
        STX     XREG
        JMP     CYCLE

* --- F = 5 : SRC (circular right rAX) ---
SRCOP   LDA     AREG
        LDX     XREG
        SRC     0,1         Circular right shift of rAX by rI1 bytes.
        STA     AREG
        STX     XREG
        JMP     CYCLE

* Jump table for F = 0..5, addressed via "JMP SFTABL,3" after "DEC3 5".
* Place the label on the last entry (same pattern as the book’s JUMP table).
        JMP     SLAOP
        JMP     SRAOP
        JMP     SLAXOP
        JMP     SRAXOP
        JMP     SLCOP
SFTABL  JMP     SRCOP
Why this matches the book’s simulator

Dispatch pattern. The book’s operator routines use DEC3 k followed by JMP TABLE,3, with the table label on the last entry so that negative offsets select earlier entries (see the JUMP operator’s table). We do the same with DEC3 5 and SFTABL.
Where the count comes from. The control routine has already computed the effective address and stored it in the variable M; it then sets rI5 ← M. We take the magnitude of M via LD1 M(1:2) to obtain a nonnegative byte count for shifting. (Signs of M are irrelevant to SHIFT.)
Which registers to touch. SLA/SRA affect only the A register; SLAX/SRAX/SLC/SRC treat rA‖rX as a 10-byte word. In the simulator, magnitudes live in AREG/XREG, and signs in SIGNA/SIGNX. MIX shift operators do not change signs, so we update only AREG/XREG.
Opcode and timing. The switch table ties opcode 6 to SHIFT(2), so no extra timing logic is needed in the routine; the control code already accounts for the two time units when it indexes the table.

Notes and edge cases

If the count exceeds the register width (e.g., SLA/SRA by ≥5, SLAX/SRAX by ≥10), MIX semantics produce all zeros in the shifted region; SLC/SRC are naturally modulo-10 circular shifts. The host MIX instructions used above enforce these behaviors directly.
The routine intentionally ignores the sign of M by using |M| as the count, which matches standard descriptions of MIX shift instructions (“M specifies the number of bytes to be shifted”).

title: 1.4.3.1 A MIX simulator
text:
3 [22]
What MOVE must do

Semantics: move F words, one word at a time, from source block starting at M to destination block starting at the (simulated) rI1 address; when finished, rI1 is increased by F. If F=0, nothing happens. Execution time is 1 + 2F units (the base “1” comes from the opcode table; we add 2 per word moved in the routine). 
Overlap behavior (important!): copying proceeds “forward” (from M to M+1 … while storing to rI1, rI1+1, …). If destination starts inside the source and ahead of it (e.g., rI1 = M+1), the same word may be replicated, exactly as described in the manual. Your simulator should therefore not try to be “memmove-safe”; it must mimic MIX’s specified effect. 
Control state the simulator has already set up for you: rI5 ← M, rI3 ← F, rI4 ← C, and the opcode table has placed the base time in TIME before jumping to the per-opcode routine. The magnitudes/signs of simulated registers live in memory cells like I1REG, SIGN1, etc. 

MOVE routine (MIXAL)
Paste this under the label MOVE that’s wired in the opcode table.
* MOVE — opcode 7
MOVE    J3Z   CYCLE          * If F=0, nothing to do.

        JMP   MEMORY         * Fetch SOURCE word at address rI5:
                             * on return: rX = sign, rA = magnitude (of memory[rI5])

        SRAX  5              * Pack sign+magnitude so the full word sits in rX.

* Validate DEST = simulated rI1 (must be in [0, BEGIN))
        LD1   I1REG          * rI1 ← magnitude of simulated index register 1
        LDA   SIGN1          * rA  ← sign of simulated index register 1
        JAP   OKDEST         * if +, OK
        J1NZ  MEMERROR       * if negative and magnitude ≠ 0 → invalid address
        STZ   SIGN1(0:0)     * normalize -0 as +0 (permit address 0)

OKDEST  CMP1  =BEGIN=        * rI1 ≥ BEGIN ?
        JGE   MEMERROR       * out of simulated memory → error

* Store the fetched word into DEST and account for time
        STX   0,1            * memory[rI1] ← fetched full word
        LDA   CLOCK          * add 2 units per moved word (base 1 was set already)
        INCA  2
        STA   CLOCK

* Advance pointers/count and loop
        INC1  1              * rI1++
        ST1   I1REG          * write back simulated I1
        INC5  1              * rI5++ (SOURCE++)
        DEC3  1              * F--
        JMP   MOVE           * keep moving until J3Z fires at top
This routine follows MIX’s defined behavior exactly:

It moves one word at a time, always reading the source first (via MEMORY) and then writing to the current destination (STX 0,1).
Because we progress SOURCE (rI5) and DEST (rI1) forward in lock-step, the overlap case where DEST > SOURCE produces the same “replication” effect shown in the book (that’s the intended semantics). 
We charge time by adding 2 to CLOCK per moved word; together with the opcode table’s base time, this yields 1 + 2F. 
We update the simulated I1 immediately each step (by writing back I1REG), which is fine because programs can’t observe intermediate state during an instruction; at completion, rI1 has increased by F, as required. 
Address checks ensure both source (handled by MEMORY) and destination are inside simulated memory [0, BEGIN), with -0 treated as +0. 

Quick sanity check on overlap

Example from the text: F=3, M=1000, rI1=999 → you’ll copy 1000→999, 1001→1000, 1002→1001 (no surprise).
If instead rI1=1001, you’ll copy 1000→1001, then (since 1001 now holds the old 1000) 1001→1002, then 1002→1003, so the original CONTENTS(1000) appears three times—exactly as MIX specifies. 

Where these hooks live in the simulator

The control routine unpacks INST, computes M with indexing, stores F in rI3 and M in rI5, looks up the routine in OPTABLE, stores the base time in TIME, and jumps to MOVE. Your routine returns to CYCLE when F becomes zero.

title: 1.4.3.1 A MIX simulator
text:
4 [14]
Here’s the smallest correct change to Knuth’s MIX-simulator so it “starts as if the GO button had been pushed.”
What the GO button does (per §1.3.1, Ex. 26)
When GO is pressed (with a card reader present), MIX (1) reads one card into locations 0000–0015, (2) clears the overflow toggle and sets rJ ← 0, then (3) jumps to location 0000 to begin execution. 
What the simulator already does
In Program M (the simulator), the main loop already:

Clears the overflow toggle (STZ OVTog) and
Begins execution at location 0 by setting the “next instruction” register to 0 (ENT6 0, where rI6 is the location of the next instruction). 

So the only GO-state it doesn’t establish is rJ = 0.
Minimal patch
Add one instruction at program start to zero the simulated J register:
001 * MIX SIMULATOR
002 ORIG 3500
003 BEGIN   STZ TIME(0:2)   ; clear time
004          STZ OVTog      ; clear overflow toggle  (GO requirement)
004a         STZ JREG       ; * new: rJ ← 0       (GO requirement)
005          STZ COMPI
006          ENT6 0         ; start at location 0   (GO requirement)
007 CYCLE    LDA CLOCK
...

STZ JREG makes the simulator’s rJ match the GO button’s post-reset state.
STZ OVTog and ENT6 0 already matched the GO semantics (overflow cleared; “PC” set to 0000, since rI6 is the location of the next instruction). The control routine’s use of rI6 for the fetch is described just above Program M. 

Notes

We aren’t simulating the initial card read (GO step 1) here; I/O operators are intentionally left for later exercises (§1.4.3.1, Ex. 6). This exercise only asks the simulator to begin as if GO had been used.
After this one-line change, the simulator’s initial state matches items (2)–(3) of GO: rJ=0, overflow cleared, and execution begins at 0000.

That’s it—one added STZ JREG at BEGIN.

title: 1.4.3.1 A MIX simulator
text:
5 [24]
Timing rules we’ll use (real MIX)
MIX uses time unit u. From the timing summary:

All LOAD/STORE, ADD/SUB, comparisons, shifts: 2u
MOVE: 1u + 2u per word
MUL/NUM/CHAR: 10u; DIV: 12u
All remaining ops (including jumps, INC/DEC/ENT): 1u In particular, LDA = 2u, ENTA = 1u on real MIX.

Assumptions (to keep the path concrete)

Instruction uses no indexing (I=0), so the indexing block in the control routine is skipped.
No errors (no range, field, or memory errors).
For LDA, the usual field (0:5) (sign included).
For ENTA, the address M ≠ 0 (the “M=0” special sign case is noted later). These are the straight-line, normal paths through the simulator.

The simulator’s fixed control overhead per simulated instruction
Every instruction first goes through the control routine at label CYCLE (load CLOCK, fetch INST, unpack M/I/F/C, consult OPTABLE, and jump). Counting times by instruction class yields 34u before the jump to the operator routine when I=0 (lines 007–035: loads/stores/shift = 2u each; INC/DEC/J? = 1u).
Simulating LDA
Path: CYCLE → OPTABLE → LOAD → GETV → (FCHECK, MEMORY) → LOAD1 → SIZECHK → CYCLE. Key code: LOAD/LOADN (lines 196–205), GETV (149–158), FCHECK (135–146), MEMORY (125–134), SIZECHK/OVCHECK (289–291, 191).

Control overhead to reach operator: 34u.
Operator + subroutines:

JMP GETV: 1u
FCHECK (valid field): STJ, ENTA, ENTX, DIV, CMPX, JG, STX, STA, LD1, CMPA, JLE → 27u
MEMORY: STJ, JS? (not taken), CMP, JGE (not taken), LDX, ENTA, SRAX, LDA, JMP → 14u
Return in GETV (sign included, so skip setting + sign): J1Z, LD2N, SRA, JMP → 6u
LOAD1 store magnitude/sign: ENT1, STA, STX, JMP → 6u
SIZECHK/OVCHECK (no index overflow, no overflow): LD1, J1Z, JNOV → 4u

Sum operator-side = 1 + 27 + 14 + 6 + 6 + 4 = 58u (plus 5u for the two unconditional returns to GETV’s and MEMORY’s JMP * sites) → 63u total operator segment.
Total simulated time for LDA = 34u + 63u = 97u.

Real MIX time for LDA = 2u. Slowdown ≈ 97/2 ≈ 48.5×.
Simulating ENTA
Path: CYCLE → OPTABLE → ADDROP → (LOAD1) → SIZECHK → CYCLE. Key code: ADDROP block (265–278), then LOAD1 and SIZECHK as above.

Control overhead to reach operator: 34u.
Operator path (typical M ≠ 0 branch):

ADDROP prologue & sign/magnitude setup: DEC3, J3P (not taken), ENTX, JXNZ (taken), SRAX, LDA, ENT1, JMP 1F,3, JMP LOAD1 → 11u
LOAD1 store magnitude/sign: STA, STX, JMP SIZECHK → 5u
SIZECHK/OVCHECK: LD1, J1Z, JNOV → 4u

Sum operator-side = 20u.
Total simulated time for ENTA = 34u + 20u = 54u.

Real MIX time for ENTA = 1u. Slowdown ≈ 54/1 = 54×.
Notes on variants

Indexing present (I≠0): the control routine executes the indexing block (lines 018–026), adding ~15u more (several loads/stores/shifts/add/compare/jump).
LDA with field not including sign: GETV executes three extra ops (ENTX; two shifts), adding ~5u.
ENTA with M = 0: executes two extra ops (LDX INST; ENTA 1) to pick up the instruction’s sign rule, adding ~3u.

Final comparison

LDA: simulated ~97u vs real 2u (≈ 49× slower).
ENTA: simulated ~54u vs real 1u (≈ 54× slower).

These figures come straight from counting the executed instructions along the normal paths in Program M and applying MIX’s timing rules.

title: 1.4.3.1 A MIX simulator
text:
6 [28]
Here’s a clean way to add the missing I/O operators to the MIX simulator, limited to unit 16 (card reader) and unit 18 (line printer), and consistent with Knuth’s timing model:

Opcodes and meanings (from the MIX table): 34 = JBUS “unit F busy?”, 35 = IOC “control, unit F”, 36 = IN “input, unit F”, 37 = OUT “output, unit F”, 38 = JRED “unit F ready?” 
Exercise requirement: implement JBUS, IOC, IN, OUT, JRED; allow only units 16 and 18; assume read-card and skip-to-new-page take T=10000u; print-line takes T=7500u. Also special-case JBUS * to avoid the simulator “stopping.” 

Design (what to simulate)
MIX overlaps I/O with computation. Issuing IN/OUT/IOC takes 1 unit of CPU time and makes the device busy for T units; the CPU continues. The queries JBUS/JRED test that busy/ready state. We therefore maintain per-device “ready times” and a CPU clock:

CLOCK — current CPU time (u).
RDRFREE — earliest time at which unit 16 (reader) is ready.
PRTFREE — earliest time at which unit 18 (printer) is ready.
Constants: TREAD=10000, TSKIP=10000, TPRINT=7500.
Overlap rule: when (re)starting an I/O, let start = max(CLOCK, DEVFREE) then set DEVFREE = start + T. CPU time still advances by 1 for the instruction itself (the “1+T” shown in the table), while the T portion accrues on the device timeline. 

Handlers to splice into “INDIVIDUAL OPERATORS”
Below is MIXAL-style code that drops into the simulator’s opcode dispatch, following the conventions used elsewhere in §1.4.3.1 (use your existing MEMORY check and jump simulation sequence, e.g., JSJ JMP MEMORY / ENT6 0,5).
Data and constants (in your simulator’s data area):
TREAD   EQU     10000          * read-card (unit 16)
TSKIP   EQU     10000          * skip-to-new-page (unit 18, IOC)
TPRINT  EQU     7500           * print-line (unit 18)
CLOCK   CON     0
RDRFREE CON     0              * unit 16 ready time
PRTFREE CON     0              * unit 18 ready time
Utility: pick device’s FREE-TIME cell given F (16 or 18). If your decode already provides F in a register, keep it; otherwise extract F the way your other handlers do.
* On entry: r? = F (device number), rI5 = effective M (jump/address).
DEVPTR  CMP?   =16=
        JE     DEVPTR16
        CMP?   =18=
        JE     DEVPTR18
        JMP    FERROR            * only 16 and 18 are supported here
DEVPTR16 LDA   RDRFREE
        JMP    DEVPTRX
DEVPTR18 LDA   PRTFREE
DEVPTRX  STA   FREEBUF           * FREEBUF holds the fetched free time
        JMP    *+1
FREEBUF  CON   0                 * scratch word to hold device free time
36 IN (unit F)

Support: F=16 only (card reader).
Effect: begin a read “now or when free” and mark reader busy until start+TREAD. Optionally deposit a 16-word “card image” at M..M+15 (for a bare simulator this can be zeros or any deterministic pattern). CPU time +1.

INOP    ...                      * you’re in opcode 36 case
        * require F=16
        CMP?   =16=
        JNE    FERROR
        * start := max(CLOCK, RDRFREE)
        LDA    CLOCK
        CMPA   RDRFREE
        JGE    IN_START_READY
        LDA    RDRFREE
IN_START_READY
        ADD    =TREAD=
        STA    RDRFREE
        * (Optional) deposit 16 words at M..M+15:
        *   ENT1 0; LOOP 16x: STZ 0,5; INC5 1; DEC1 1; J1P LOOP
        * advance CPU time by 1
        LDA    CLOCK
        INCA   1
        STA    CLOCK
        JMP    CYCLE
37 OUT (unit F)

Support: F=18 only (line printer).
Effect: schedule a print of one “line” for TPRINT units. CPU time +1.

OUTOP   CMP?   =18=
        JNE    FERROR
        * start := max(CLOCK, PRTFREE)
        LDA    CLOCK
        CMPA   PRTFREE
        JGE    OUT_START_READY
        LDA    PRTFREE
OUT_START_READY
        ADD    =TPRINT=
        STA    PRTFREE
        * (optional: ignore actual text formatting in this bare simulator)
        LDA    CLOCK
        INCA   1
        STA    CLOCK
        JMP    CYCLE
35 IOC (unit F)

Support: F=18 used as “skip to new page,” cost TSKIP. (IOC for 16 is a no-op here.) CPU time +1.

IOCOP   CMP?   =18=
        JE     IOC_PRN
        * F=16 -> no-op for this minimal simulator
        LDA    CLOCK
        INCA   1
        STA    CLOCK
        JMP    CYCLE

IOC_PRN LDA    CLOCK
        CMPA   PRTFREE
        JGE    IOC_START_READY
        LDA    PRTFREE
IOC_START_READY
        ADD    =TSKIP=
        STA    PRTFREE
        LDA    CLOCK
        INCA   1
        STA    CLOCK
        JMP    CYCLE
34 JBUS (unit F busy?)

If the device is still busy (its FREE time > CLOCK), jump to M.
Special case for JBUS *: if busy and M is the current instruction address, don’t spin at zero cost; instead advance CLOCK to the device’s FREE time (this models “wait until ready” and prevents the simulator from “stopping,” per Knuth’s note). If not busy, fall through. CPU time +1 (for the test itself). 

JBUSOP  * load device free time
        JSR    DEVPTR
        * busy if FREEBUF > CLOCK
        LDA    FREEBUF
        CMPA   CLOCK
        JLE    JBUS_NOTBUSY
        * device busy -> conditional jump
        * detect JBUS * (M == current PC). Your simulator already knows how to
        * compare M against “this instruction”; reuse that mechanism.
        JSR    IS_STAR           * sets CC: = if M==*
        JE     JBUS_WAIT
        * normal jump to M
        JSJ    JMP MEMORY        * validate address in rI5, as elsewhere
        ENT6   0,5               * simulate the jump
        * account 1 unit for the instruction itself
        LDA    CLOCK
        INCA   1
        STA    CLOCK
        JMP    CYCLE
JBUS_WAIT
        * wait until ready by jumping time forward
        LDA    FREEBUF
        STA    CLOCK
        JMP    CYCLE
JBUS_NOTBUSY
        * not busy, just consume 1 unit and continue
        LDA    CLOCK
        INCA   1
        STA    CLOCK
        JMP    CYCLE
38 JRED (unit F ready?)

Jump to M iff device is ready (FREE ≤ CLOCK). CPU time +1 either way.

JREDOP  JSR    DEVPTR
        LDA    FREEBUF
        CMPA   CLOCK
        JGT    JRED_NREADY
        * ready -> jump
        JSJ    JMP MEMORY
        ENT6   0,5
        LDA    CLOCK
        INCA   1
        STA    CLOCK
        JMP    CYCLE
JRED_NREADY
        LDA    CLOCK
        INCA   1
        STA    CLOCK
        JMP    CYCLE
Notes and rationale

Unit numbers: 16 is the card reader (e.g., the reset loader does IN 0(16)), 18 is the printer; restricting support to those two matches the exercise. 
Timing matches the MIX table (“1+T” for IN/OUT) and the multiple-buffering discussion: T accrues on the device; the CPU continues after the 1-unit issue time.
JBUS * “waits” by fast-forwarding CLOCK to the device’s free time, exactly to avoid a zero-time busy-wait loop (the note in the problem).

title: 1.4.3.1 A MIX simulator
text:
7 [32]
Here’s a clean, self-contained way to solve this exercies. The point is to keep your MIX I/O simulator realistic: executing IN/OUT must not move data immediately; the actual transfer happens roughly halfway through the device’s service time, while the device remains busy for the full time. This prevents programs from (incorrectly) assuming that IN/OUT are instantaneous. The exercise builds directly on the I/O instructions and device behavior defined earlier (units 16 = card reader, 18 = line printer; IN/OUT/IOC/JBUS/JRED semantics), and on the timing constants given in Ex. 6 (read-card or skip-page: T=10000u; print-line: T=7500u). The exercise’s specific requirement (“make the transmission occur after about half the time”) appears at the end of the section’s exercise list. 
Model the timing
Maintain a global simulator clock now (in MIX time units u) and, for each device d ∈ {16,18}, these fields:

ready_at[d] — time when the device will become ready again.
xfer_at[d] — time when the data movement (memory ↔︎ device buffer) actually occurs; this is start + ceil(T[d]/2).
pending[d] — None | ("IN", M) | ("OUT", M) | ("IOC", op) describing the scheduled action.
Optional device buffers (e.g., next card image for 16; print line buffer for 18).

Timing constants (from Ex. 6): T[16].read_card = 10000, T[18].print_line = 7500, and T[18].skip_to_new_page (IOC) = 10000. 
Event processing hook
Before fetching each instruction in your main CYCLE loop, process any I/O events whose time has arrived:
def service_io_events():
    for d in (16, 18):
        if pending[d] and now >= xfer_at[d]:
            # Perform the scheduled data movement exactly once.
            if pending[d].op == "IN":
                mem[M ... M+block_size(d)-1] = device_in_buffer[d]
            elif pending[d].op == "OUT":
                device_out_buffer[d] = mem[M ... M+block_size(d)-1]
            # IOC with no data movement: nothing to do here.
            pending[d] = None
            xfer_at[d] = +∞
This “half-time” transfer ensures that a program that uses the data too early (after IN) or changes it too late (after OUT) will behave incorrectly—exactly the mistake this exercise wants to catch.
Instruction semantics (with half-time transfers)
Use the architectural rules from §1.3.1: IN/OUT wait until the unit is ready to start; the transfer completes later; IOC initiates a device-specific operation; JRED/JBUS test readiness. 
IN M(F=unit)
d = unit
wait_until_ready(d)               # if now < ready_at[d], advance now to ready_at[d]
start = now
pending[d] = ("IN", M)            # schedule memory ← device
xfer_at[d] = start + ceil(T[d]/2) # "approximately half" the device time
ready_at[d] = start + T[d]        # unit remains busy for full time
Block size: 16 words for unit 16 (card reader), 24 for unit 18 (printer) if you ever support input there; here we limit to 16 for the card reader. 
OUT M(F=unit)
d = unit
wait_until_ready(d)
start = now
pending[d] = ("OUT", M)           # schedule device ← memory
xfer_at[d] = start + ceil(T[d]/2)
ready_at[d] = start + T[d]
For unit 18, the printed line’s content is captured at xfer_at[18]; the physical printing completes at ready_at[18].
IOC M(F=unit)
Wait until not busy, then perform the control; e.g., for the printer “skip-to-new-page” takes 10000 u but does no data transfer:
wait_until_ready(18)
start = now
pending[18] = ("IOC", "SKIP")
xfer_at[18] = +∞                  # no data movement
ready_at[18] = start + 10000
(If you later support IOC on other units, replicate this pattern with their T.)
JRED M(F=unit) and JBUS M(F=unit)
Use the §1.3.1 definitions: JRED jumps iff the unit is ready (now ≥ ready_at[d]), JBUS jumps iff not ready. 
Important “JBUS *” special case
As hinted in Ex. 6, if the target of JBUS is the instruction itself (busy-wait spin), you must advance now to the next interesting time to avoid a frozen simulator:
if op == JBUS and target == PC and now < ready_at[d]:
    now = min(ready_at[d], min(xfer_at[k] for k with pending[k]))
This is the pragmatic fix that keeps the loop progressing. 
Why this satisfies Exercise 7

IN/OUT no longer “complete” at issue time: the simulator defers the data movement to start + T/2, while the device remains busy until start + T.
Programs that incorrectly assume immediate availability (after IN) or immediate consumption (after OUT) will misbehave, reproducing the real-machine hazard the exercise is designed to prevent. 

Quick timeline sanity checks

Card read into 1000 with immediate use (wrong):

t0:     IN 1000(16)           # schedules memory update at t0+5000; device busy until t0+10000
t0+1:   LDA 1000              # reads OLD data (correctly exposing the bug)
...

Printer OUT with later modification (also wrong):

t0:     OUT 2000(18)          # captures line at t0+3750; printer finishes at t0+7500
t0+2000: STA 2000             # too early: the printed line will still reflect the pre-change contents

Proper synchronization:

OUT 2000(18);  JBUS *(18)     # spin; simulator advances now to ~t0+7500
# when we exit, the line has been printed and the unit is ready again

Data layout for I/O timing and pending actions

We keep, per device, three fields:

READY_AT[d] — when the device becomes ready again.
XFER_AT[d] — when the actual data movement occurs.
PEND[d] — pending action kind and base address (encoded in one MIX word: sign = kind; value = base address).

Choose encodings:

PEND sign: + = none; − = has pending. The action code is stored in the most significant byte (B1) and the memory base address in the remaining bytes.
Action codes: 1 = IN, 2 = OUT, 3 = IOC (no data move).

You can keep device constants (TREAD16, TPRINT18, etc.) as you prefer.
          ORIG  2000

* --- Global clock (u time units) ---
NOW       CON   0

* --- Device constants (adapt to your table) ---
* D16 = card reader, D18 = line printer
D16       CON   16
D18       CON   18

* service times (u)
TREAD16   CON   10000          * read card / skip page (example)
TPRINT18  CON   7500
TSKIP18   CON   10000          * IOC skip page

* half-times (ceil(T/2))
HREAD16   CON   5000
HPRINT18  CON   3750
HSKIP18   CON   5000           * not used (no data move), keep for symmetry

* --- Per-device tables: index by (device-16) if you only support 16..18
* We'll allocate a small contiguous array and compute offsets.
NDEV      CON   3              * support devices 16,17,18
BASEDEV   CON   16

READY_AT  CON   0,0,0          * READY_AT[16], [17], [18]
XFER_AT   CON   0,0,0          * XFER_AT[16], [17], [18]
PEND      CON   +0,+0,+0       * +0 = none; −(action<<30 | base) = pending

* encodings
ACT_IN    CON   1
ACT_OUT   CON   2
ACT_IOC   CON   3
NONE      CON   +0

* scratch
TMP       CON   0
IDX       CON   0              * working index = d - BASEDEV
W         CON   0              * generic temp
Helper to compute IDX = d − 16 and leave it in IDX:
IDXOF     STJ   IDXOF_RET
          STA   TMP          * A has d
          LDA   TMP
          SUB   BASEDEV
          STA   IDX
          JBUS  *            * (no-op; keep same timing style)
IDXOF_RET JMP   0

Small utilities

Store a “pending action” word
We encode (action, base) in one word: B1=action, B2..B5=base, sign negative = “pending”.
SETPEND   STJ   SP_RET
          STA   TMP          * A = base address M
          ENT1  0
          ENTA  0
          OR    NONE         * clear A
          LDX   TMP
          SLA   5            * move X into B2..B5 (implementation detail varies)
          OR    ACT          * B1 = action (ACT in A)
          ST1   W            * save if you prefer a different scratch
          NEG   W            * make it pending (negative sign)
          LDA   IDX
          STA   TMP
          * PEND[base+IDX] ← W
          LDX   TMP
          LD2   =PEND=
          ADD   X
          STA   @0           * store W into PEND[IDX]
SP_RET    JMP   0
(If your assembler lacks these exact bit ops, you can lay out two words PENDKIND[ ] and PENDBASE[ ] instead; logic stays the same and code gets simpler.)
Read/write table slots
If your MIXAL doesn’t like indirect addressing macros, you can provide tiny helpers. A simpler, clearer approach is to store each array as separate contiguous words and use ENTx/INCx stepping. To keep this answer concise, I’ll use a compact “index load/store” idiom; translate to your style as needed.

I/O event poller (run before each instruction fetch)

This is the heart of Exercise 7. It performs the deferred transfers at XFER_AT[d] but does not make the device ready until READY_AT[d].
Call IOPOLL at the top of your CYCLE loop, before fetching the next instruction.
IOPOLL    STJ   IOP_RET
          ENT3  0             * R3 = loop index 0..NDEV-1
IOP_LP    CMP3  NDEV
          JGE   IOP_DONE

          * compute absolute device number d = BASEDEV + R3
          LDA   BASEDEV
          ADD   R3
          STA   TMP           * TMP = d
          JSR   IDXOF         * set IDX = d - BASEDEV

          * if PEND[IDX] == NONE or XFER_AT[IDX] > NOW, skip
          * Load XFER_AT[IDX] into A:
          LD2   =XFER_AT=
          ADD   IDX
          LDA   @0
          STA   W             * W = xfer time
          CMPA  NOW
          JG    IOP_NEXT      * not yet time

          * Load PEND[IDX]
          LD2   =PEND=
          ADD   IDX
          LDA   @0
          JPL   IOP_NEXT      * sign + => no pending

          * decode action and base; here I’ll assume you split them
          * If you implemented PENDKIND/PENDBASE arrays, this becomes trivial:
          *   kind = PENDKIND[IDX]; base = PENDBASE[IDX]

          * --- PERFORM THE DATA MOVE IF action == IN/OUT ---
          * For clarity, I’ll branch by device number and action
          LDA   TMP           * d in A
          CMPA  D16
          JE    IOP_D16
          CMPA  D18
          JE    IOP_D18
          JMP   IOP_CLEAR     * unknown device => just clear pending

* --- Device 16 (card reader): move 16 words at XFER time ---
IOP_D16   * if ACT_IN: mem[M..M+15] <- device buffer
          * if ACT_OUT: (rare for reader) ignore or treat as error
          * In most simulators, you keep a prepared card image buffer CARD[16].
          * Copy CARD[k] -> M[k] for k=0..15 (or vice versa for OUT).
          * Pseudocode in MIXAL outline:

          * ... decode base addr into R1 (left to you) ...
          * ... copy loop of 16 words memory <-- CARDBUF ...
          JMP   IOP_CLEAR

* --- Device 18 (line printer): capture line buffer from memory at XFER time ---
IOP_D18   * if ACT_OUT: PRNBUF <- mem[M..M+N-1] (your N; often 24 words)
          * if ACT_IN : usually unsupported
          * MIXAL copy loop outline:
          * ... decode base addr into R1 ...
          * ... copy loop memory --> PRNBUF ...
          JMP   IOP_CLEAR

* After moving data (or deciding no move for IOC), clear PEND and set XFER_AT far in future
IOP_CLEAR LD2   =PEND=
          ADD   IDX
          LDA   NONE
          STA   @0

          LD2   =XFER_AT=
          ADD   IDX
          LDA   =+7777777=    * “infinite” (larger than any NOW)
          STA   @0

IOP_NEXT  INC3  1
          JMP   IOP_LP

IOP_DONE  JMP   IOP_RET
IOP_RET   JMP   0
Notes:

Keep your actual memory copy routines where you already copy between device buffers and core. The point is when you do it: here, inside IOPOLL once NOW ≥ XFER_AT[d], not when IN/OUT is issued.
IOC actions normally do no data move — so IOPOLL simply clears PEND at XFER time only for IN/OUT; IOC needs no XFER slot at all (we’ll set it to “infinite”).


Modifications to instruction semantics

You only change when data moves and how readiness is computed. The opcode timing (issuing cost) stays as in your simulator.
IN M(F=unit d)

If NOW < READY_AT[d], you must not block the whole CPU (MIX uses JBUS/JRED for sync); the architectural spec is that the instruction initiates the operation if the device is ready. Many simulators either (a) trap as “busy” or (b) start the op at READY_AT. For simplicity, I recommend: “if busy, advance NOW to READY_AT[d] and start,” which matches typical exercises.
Upon starting, set:

PEND[d] = (IN, M).
XFER_AT[d] = START + ⌈T/2⌉.
READY_AT[d] = START + T.


* On decode, A holds M (effective address), F holds unit d
IN_OP     STJ   IN_RET
          STA   TMP           * TMP = M
          LDA   FREG          * get device number d
          STA   W
          JSR   IDXOF         * IDX = d - BASEDEV

          * wait until device ready
          LD2   =READY_AT=
          ADD   IDX
          LDA   @0
          CMPA  NOW
          JLE   IN_START
          STA   NOW           * advance NOW to READY_AT[d]

IN_START  * schedule IN
          * XFER_AT = NOW + HALF_T[d]
          LDA   W             * d in A
          CMPA  D16
          JE    IN16
          CMPA  D18
          JE    IN18

          JMP   IN_DONE       * unsupported device -> no-op or error

IN16      LDA   NOW
          ADD   HREAD16
          LD2   =XFER_AT=
          ADD   IDX
          STA   @0

          * READY_AT = NOW + TREAD16
          LDA   NOW
          ADD   TREAD16
          LD2   =READY_AT=
          ADD   IDX
          STA   @0

          * PEND = (ACT_IN, M)
          LDA   TMP          * base M
          STA   W
          LDA   ACT_IN
          STA   ACT
          JSR   SETPEND
          JMP   IN_DONE

IN18      * normally input from printer is invalid; ignore or treat as error
          JMP   IN_DONE

IN_DONE   JMP   IN_RET
IN_RET    JMP   0
OUT M(F=unit d)
Same pattern, with OUT action and device-specific T and HALF_T.
OUT_OP    STJ   OUT_RET
          STA   TMP          * TMP = M
          LDA   FREG
          STA   W
          JSR   IDXOF

          * wait until device ready
          LD2   =READY_AT=
          ADD   IDX
          LDA   @0
          CMPA  NOW
          JLE   OUT_START
          STA   NOW

OUT_START LDA   W
          CMPA  D16
          JE    OUT16
          CMPA  D18
          JE    OUT18
          JMP   OUT_DONE

OUT16     * card reader OUT usually meaningless; either ignore or error
          JMP   OUT_DONE

OUT18     * printer: capture line at half time; finish at full time
          LDA   NOW
          ADD   HPRINT18
          LD2   =XFER_AT=
          ADD   IDX
          STA   @0

          LDA   NOW
          ADD   TPRINT18
          LD2   =READY_AT=
          ADD   IDX
          STA   @0

          LDA   TMP
          STA   W
          LDA   ACT_OUT
          STA   ACT
          JSR   SETPEND
          JMP   OUT_DONE

OUT_DONE  JMP   OUT_RET
OUT_RET   JMP   0
IOC M(F=unit d)
IOC has no data transfer. It just keeps the device busy for its service time (e.g., skip page).
IOC_OP    STJ   IOC_RET
          LDA   FREG
          STA   W
          JSR   IDXOF

          * wait until device ready
          LD2   =READY_AT=
          ADD   IDX
          LDA   @0
          CMPA  NOW
          JLE   IOC_START
          STA   NOW

IOC_START LDA   W
          CMPA  D18
          JE    IOC18
          JMP   IOC_DONE

IOC18     * skip to new page, no data movement
          LDA   NOW
          ADD   TSKIP18
          LD2   =READY_AT=
          ADD   IDX
          STA   @0

          * Ensure XFER_AT “infinite” and PEND cleared
          LD2   =XFER_AT=
          ADD   IDX
          LDA   =+7777777=
          STA   @0

          LD2   =PEND=
          ADD   IDX
          LDA   NONE
          STA   @0
          JMP   IOC_DONE

IOC_DONE  JMP   IOC_RET
IOC_RET   JMP   0
JRED / JBUS

JRED jumps iff NOW ≥ READY_AT[d].
JBUS jumps iff NOW < READY_AT[d].

Exercise-friendly special case: if the target is the instruction itself (busy spin JBUS *), advance NOW to the next interesting time (the earlier of READY_AT[d] and any XFER_AT[k] that is still in the future), then fall through (don’t infinite-loop).
JRED_OP   STJ   JR_RET
          LDA   FREG
          JSR   IDXOF
          LD2   =READY_AT=
          ADD   IDX
          LDA   @0
          CMPA  NOW
          JLE   JR_TAK       * ready → jump
          JMP   JR_NOT
JR_TAK    JMP   TARGET       * fill by your assembler
JR_NOT    JMP   NEXTPC
JR_RET    JMP   0
JBUS_OP   STJ   JB_RET
          LDA   FREG
          STA   W
          JSR   IDXOF

          * check readiness
          LD2   =READY_AT=
          ADD   IDX
          LDA   @0
          CMPA  NOW
          JLE   JB_NOT       * ready => do not jump

          * device busy → would jump.
          * If target == PC (self-loop), advance NOW to min(next READY or next XFER across all devices).
          * Detect self-loop using your decoded target; here assume flag SELF is set by decoder.
          LDA   SELF
          JZE   JB_TAK       * not self → just jump

          * compute NEXT = READY_AT[d]
          STA   W            * preserve A
          LD2   =READY_AT=
          ADD   IDX
          LDA   @0
          STA   TMP          * TMP = candidate time

          * scan XFER_AT for an earlier future event
          ENT3  0
JB_SCAN   CMP3  NDEV
          JGE   JB_SET
          LD2   =XFER_AT=
          ADD   R3
          LDA   @0
          CMPA  NOW
          JLE   JB_NEXT
          CMPA  TMP
          JGE   JB_NEXT
          STA   TMP          * tighter min

JB_NEXT   INC3  1
          JMP   JB_SCAN

JB_SET    LDA   TMP
          CMPA  NOW
          JLE   JB_TAK       * nothing ahead → just jump
          STA   NOW          * advance time to next event
          JMP   JB_EXIT

JB_TAK    JMP   TARGET
JB_NOT    JMP   NEXTPC
JB_EXIT   JMP   NEXTPC
JB_RET    JMP   0

Hook IOPOLL into your fetch/execute loop

At the very top of CYCLE (or just before fetching the next instruction), call IOPOLL. This guarantees that once NOW ≥ XFER_AT[d], one (and only one) transfer happens before any instruction that might observe memory.
CYCLE     * ... your loop prologue ...
          JSR   IOPOLL
          * fetch instruction at PC
          * decode & dispatch
          * execute
          * advance PC
          JMP   CYCLE

Notes on memory moves


I’ve left the actual buffer copies as comments because every simulator lays out buffers differently. The change the exercise asks for is purely temporal: move those copies from the IN/OUT handlers to IOPOLL at NOW ≥ XFER_AT[d].
Typical choices:

Card reader: fixed 16 words CARDBUF[0..15] prepared by your card-file driver. At IN half-time: mem[M..M+15] ← CARDBUF.
Printer: copy mem[M..M+N-1] → PRNBUF (N = your line-encoding footprint) at OUT half-time; the physical “printing” (e.g., writing to host file) can happen either immediately at half-time or at the moment the device becomes ready; pedagogically, capturing the line at half-time and writing it at READY_AT is clean.



Quick sanity tests


Unsafe early use after IN: IN 1000(16); LDA 1000 should load the old contents if executed before XFER_AT[16].
Unsafe late modification after OUT: OUT 2000(18); STA 2000 before XFER_AT[18] must not change the captured line; after XFER_AT[18], it must be too late.
Synchronization: OUT 2000(18); JBUS *(18) will advance NOW to min(READY_AT[18], next XFER) and then proceed when ready.

That’s the complete wiring you need. If you share the specific buffer layouts you’re using (sizes and labels for the card/printer buffers), I can fill in the tiny copy loops to match your memory map exactly.

title: 1.4.3.1 A MIX simulator
text:
8 [20]
Answer: False.

In the control routine, the simulator fetches the next instruction with line 010: LDA 0,6 (i.e., from simulated location held in the host index register rI6). Immediately after storing it, the simulator increments rI6 at line 012 with INC6 1. Thus the fetch uses the current value of rI6, and the increment prepares the next value.
The simulator initializes rI6 ← 0 at line 006. Later, jumps set rI6 directly to the validated target address using ENT6 0,5 in the JSJ/JMP handler (after a range check done by the MEMORY subroutine). This guarantees that when a jump occurs, the new rI6 is within [0, BEGIN).

However, there is no range check before the fetch at line 010. Consider what happens when the simulated program executes an instruction located at address BEGIN − 1 that does not change the location counter (i.e., not a jump):

At the start of that iteration, rI6 = BEGIN − 1, so line 010 fetches from that address.
Line 012 executes INC6 1, so rI6 becomes BEGIN.
The operator returns to the control routine; on the next iteration, line 010 will be executed with rI6 = BEGIN, which violates \(rI_6 < \texttt{BEGIN}\). 

A concrete two-instruction counterexample in the simulated memory (addresses 0..BEGIN−1) is:

At location 0: JSJ BEGIN−1 (unconditional jump to the last simulated cell; the simulator validates the target via MEMORY, then sets rI6 ← BEGIN−1).
At location BEGIN−1: any non-jump such as STZ 0 (store zero). After executing it, line 012 makes rI6 = BEGIN; the next fetch (line 010) now uses an out-of-range address.

For completeness, note that:

The MEMORY subroutine explicitly enforces 0 ≤ address < BEGIN when the simulator accesses simulated memory via rI5, but this protection does not cover the line-010 fetch via rI6.
Therefore \(0 \le rI_6\) is preserved, but \(rI_6 < \texttt{BEGIN}\) is not guaranteed at every execution of line 010.

So the statement is false.

title: 1.4.3.2 Trace routines
text:
1 [22]
On MIX, the only way to set rJ is to execute a jump whose address is located at position α; executing JMP … from location α sets rJ to α+1. Therefore, to restore rJ to the “start” value saved earlier (call that value J0), we can:
Patch (drop-in replacement for the leave block)
Below is a compact, self-contained replacement for the “leave” part of the trace routine. It assumes your routine already saved the original J in JREG (as in the text), and it uses I1 and X temporarily but restores them.
Why each step is safe
Where this fits in the printed routine
You replace the printed lines that restore rA and jump out (the LEAVE … / LEAVEX … pair near the end) with the block above. Those two lines, as printed, don’t fix rJ; the patch does.

title: 1.4.3.2 Trace routines
text:
Temporarily write the instruction JMP *+1 into memory at address J0−1;
Execute that instruction in place; this sets rJ ← J0;
Put the original word back (so we don’t alter the traced program);
Finally, exit the trace routine without disturbing rJ (so use JSJ, not JMP). Knuth’s answer notes this last point explicitly: using JSJ is crucial.



* --- NEW LEAVE SEQUENCE (restores rJ) -------------------------
XSV    ORIG  *+1          * temp: save X
I1SV   ORIG  *+1          * temp: save I1
ONE    JMP   *+1          * literal word used as a copier: "JMP *+1"

LEAVE  STX   XSV          * save rX; we’ll need it for the exit jump later
       ST1   I1SV         * save rI1; we’ll use I1 as a pointer
       LD1   JREG(0:2)    * I1 := saved start address J0
       LDA   -1,1         * A  := original contents at address J0-1
       LDX   ONE          * X  := the word "JMP *+1"
       STX   -1,1         * write that jump at address J0-1
       JMP   -1,1         * execute it → rJ := (J0-1)+1 = J0

* we’re now back here, with rJ restored to J0
       STA   -1,1         * restore the original word at J0-1
