       LD1   I1SV         * restore rI1
       LDX   XSV          * restore rX
       LDA   AREG         * restore rA (as in the book)
LEAVEX JSJ   *            * exit without changing rJ


No persistent self-modification: we restore the overwritten word at J0−1 before leaving.
Minimal register disturbance: only rA, rX, rI1 are touched temporarily, and all three are restored; the comparison indicator and overflow toggle remain unaffected (matching the section’s design goals).
Correct exit: JSJ leaves rJ as we’ve just set it to J0; using JMP here would clobber rJ again.

title: 1.4.3.2 Trace routines
text:
2 [26]
Design

A 100-word circular buffer at label BUF collects ten 10-word records; when full, we OUT it to tape 0.
We keep an index pointer in rI1 that advances by 10 after each record. To persist the pointer across iterations, we use a neat MIX trick: self-modify the address field of the instruction PTR ENT1 -100, i.e., store the current rI1 back into PTR(0:2) each time; on the next pass, ENT1 reloads the saved pointer.
We never disturb the traced program’s state (except briefly using rA and rI1 inside the tracer); rA is restored before the traced instruction executes, as in the original routine.
We wait for the device when needed and flush the final partial buffer at the end (the final flush routine is a small add-on you call once after tracing stops).

Patch (delta against the routine on pp. 212–213)
The comments say exactly where to splice these lines relative to the original labels/lines.
* --- Reserve a 100-word buffer and constants (can be placed near the end) ---
        ORIG    *+99            * reserve 100 words below BUF
BUF     CON     0               * BUF-99 .. BUF are the 100-word block
PTR     ENT1    -100            * rI1 := saved pointer (will be self-modified)
EIGHT   CON     8

I1SAVE  CON     0               * local save for rI1

* --- At ENTER: after lines 02–04 (save tape-pointer register) ---
        ST1     I1SAVE          * save caller’s rI1; tracer will use rI1

* --- After line 05–07 (after CYCLE STA PREG(0:2)) ---
        JBUS    *(0)            * ensure tape 0 not busy from a prior OUT
        STA     BUF+1,1(0:2)    * Word1 (0:2): location (A holds it here)
        ST1     I1REG           * (if you keep a global I1REG like the book)

* --- After lines 08–11 (after fetching the instruction into INST) ---
        STA     BUF+2,1         * Word2: instruction word

* --- Still before the traced instruction executes: dump “before” registers ---
        LDA     AREG            * Word3: A (before execution)
        STA     BUF+3,1

        LDA     I1SAVE          * Word4: I1 (before) — we changed rI1
        STA     BUF+4,1
        ST2     BUF+5,1         * Words5–9: I2..I6 (unchanged by tracer)
        ST3     BUF+6,1
        ST4     BUF+7,1
        ST5     BUF+8,1
        ST6     BUF+9,1

        STX     BUF+10,1        * Word10: X (before)
        LDA     JREG(0:2)
        STA     BUF+1,1(4:5)    * Word1 (4:5): J (before)

* --- Pack CI (2/1/0) + 8*OVF into Word1 (3:3) ---
        ENTA    0               * start with 0 (= “<”)
        JL      CI_DONE         * if “<”, keep 0
        JE      CI_EQ
        ENTA    2               * “>” → 2
        JMP     CI_OV
CI_EQ   ENTA    1               * “=” → 1
CI_OV   JNOV    CI_NOOV         * if overflow off, skip add
        ADD     EIGHT
CI_NOOV STA     BUF+1,1(3:3)
CI_DONE NOP

* --- Advance pointer and flush when block is full ---
        INC1    10              * next record slot within the 100-word block
        J1N     KEEP_FILLING    * still negative → not yet full
        OUT     BUF-99(0)       * write the 100-word block to tape unit 0
KEEP_FILLING
        ST1     PTR(0:2)        * persist pointer by self-modifying ENT1
        LD1     I1SAVE          * restore caller’s rI1 before continuing

* (the rest of the original routine continues: execute INST, etc.)
Notes and rationale:

Where values come from. Right after CYCLE STA PREG(0:2) the A-register holds the next instruction’s location, so we can store Word 1’s (0:2) immediately. After PREG LDA * / STA INST we have the instruction word (Word 2). AREG, JREG, and I1..I6 are the tracer’s saved copies of the machine registers before the step; we read them before executing the instruction so the “before” requirement is met.
CI/OV packing. We derive the comparison indicator via branches (JL/JE) and add 8 iff overflow was set, then store that byte into field (3:3) of Word 1, exactly as specified by the exercise.
Buffering & I/O. The ORIG *+99 trick makes BUF-99 .. BUF a contiguous 100-word area. We fill records using indexed addresses BUF+… ,1, starting with rI1 = −100; after ten records (INC1 10 ten times) rI1 becomes 0 and we OUT BUF-99(0) to tape 0. We also issue JBUS *(0) up front to avoid overlapping I/O. A one-time “final flush & rewind” routine should be called after tracing stops to write a partial block and rewind unit 0, as suggested in the official notes.

Why this is correct

Every required field is captured before the traced instruction executes (we only restore rA just before execution, per the original routine’s discipline).
The 100-word block layout matches the exercise’s request for “nine more ten-word groups in the same format,” enabling efficient tape output with minimal space in core.
We preserve the traced program’s state: only rA and rI1 are touched inside the tracer and then restored; CI and OV are left unchanged (except for the unavoidable tests), as the text advises.

title: 1.4.3.2 Trace routines
text:
3 [10]
Why tape > printer for a trace routine

Throughput and latency Line printers are slow, mechanical devices. Knuth’s own timing note for printing a line is \(T=7500u\); if you print after (nearly) every instruction, output time dominates and the trace becomes painfully slow. Tape is block-oriented and written in large chunks, so per-word overhead is vastly lower; you can buffer many words and write them as a single I/O, reducing device start/stop costs and the number of waits. Knuth also emphasizes that, for trace programs, “the running time is consumed by output anyway,” so minimizing per-step output costs is crucial—tape helps do exactly that. 
Buffering and structure Exercise 2 explicitly formats the trace as fixed ten-word groups packed into 100-word tape blocks. That’s ideal for efficient sequential I/O and easy post-processing. Printing would force you to convert binary register contents into text and paginate—more CPU time and more I/O operations. 
Non-interference with the program under test A traced program “must not use the output device on which tracing information is being recorded.” If you occupy the printer for tracing, the program can’t print its own output. Using tape unit 0 for the trace keeps the printer free for the target program (or human-readable summaries later). 
Post-processing flexibility A tape log can be rewound, scanned, filtered, summarized, or reformatted into human-readable reports after the run (e.g., reconstructing flow, computing instruction frequencies, or converting only selected steps to text). With direct printing you pay the formatting cost up front and flood paper with data you may not actually need.
Lower perturbation of behavior Because tape output is faster and can be buffered, it slows the traced program less than constant printing—giving a trace that is closer to the program’s “natural” behavior (though still slower, as the text notes).

In short: tape is faster, buffer-friendly, nonintrusive, and analyzable offline, whereas printing is slow, formatting-heavy, and monopolizes a device the program might also want to use.

title: 1.4.3.2 Trace routines
text:
4 [25]
Here’s what happens if you make the trace routine trace itself by inserting, just before label ENTER, the two instructions

ENTX LEAVEX (set rX to the address of LEAVEX), then
JMP **+1 (jump into ENTER),

as the exercise proposes. (This is the routine on pp. 212–214.)
What the routine expects at entry
The trace routine is designed to be entered with rJ already set to the starting address to trace and rX set to the address where tracing should stop. The code then saves rA and rJ, takes the starting location from rJ, and begins its fetch–decode–execute cycle on the “external” program’s instruction at that location.
Step-by-step with your two extra instructions

Prelude executes. ENTX LEAVEX makes rX = address(LEAVEX); JMP **+1 transfers control to ENTER. But a JMP also loads rJ with the address of the JMP itself (the prelude location). Thus, when the trace routine starts, its idea of the “start address” (from rJ) is the prelude JMP, not the first instruction of the routine proper. The routine saves rA and rJ, then sets its “next instruction” pointer to that JMP.
First traced instruction is that prelude JMP **+1. The tracer recognizes a jump and rewrites the instruction word it will execute so that execution flows through its JUMP handler, which updates the simulated J-register and sets the next location to ENTER. (That is the normal way this tracer executes an external jump.)
Now it begins tracing the trace routine’s own body. It will fetch and execute, under trace, the instructions beginning at ENTER (e.g., STX TEST(0:2), STA AREG, …). So far, so good.
Self-destruction on STJ. Very soon the tracer reaches the routine’s own STJ JREG (the instruction that saves rJ early in the routine). The tracer deliberately rewrites any external STJ into STA before executing it, so the external program can’t disturb its simulated J-register. But now the “external program” is the tracer itself, so the tracer changes its own STJ JREG into STA JREG in memory and then executes that store-A. This destroys the saved J value (JREG now holds whatever was in rA), and the code itself has been permanently modified.
Loss of the starting location and derailment. Immediately afterward the tracer does LDA JREG(0:2) to get the next instruction location from JREG. Since JREG no longer contains a proper address (it was just overwritten with A), the tracer loads a garbage address, points its “next instruction” there, and proceeds to fetch/execute nonsense. From this point the trace prints nonsense or loops/crashes; it certainly doesn’t produce a coherent self-trace.

With only those two added instructions, the given trace routine cannot trace itself. It wrecks its own code/data because its “don’t let the external program alter J” trick (rewriting STJ to STA) applies to the tracer’s own STJ, corrupting JREG and sending execution off the rails.
This is exactly why the text restricts traced programs so they don’t store into the trace routine’s locations, and why a robust “self-trace” needs extra machinery (e.g., two copies tracing each other or special guards), as hinted by the following exercises.

title: 1.4.3.2 Trace routines
text:
5 [28]
What actually happens

The tracer that you start keeps control. Suppose we start tracer T₁ with J pointing at the entry of tracer T₂. T₁ runs its own code, repeatedly:


fetches the current instruction from T₂’s memory at address PREG₁,
copies it into T₁’s INST, possibly edits it,
executes that copy at T₁’s INST,
increments T₁’s PREG to the next address.

Crucially, the instruction that actually executes is always the copy in T₁’s INST, not the one in T₂. So T₂ never “takes over.”

T₂ “tries” to trace T₁, but only as data. Because T₂’s code is what T₁ is stepping through, T₂ will execute its own statements like “store the next address in PREG₂,” “read the instruction from T₁ into INST₂,” etc. But when the time comes to “execute the external instruction,” the execution point is T₁’s INST (by design of the trace routine), not T₂’s INST. Hence T₂’s attempt to trace T₁ never actually runs any of T₁’s instructions; it merely manipulates T₂’s bookkeeping cells (PREG₂, INST₂, AREG₂, …). This respects the routine’s restriction that the traced program mustn’t clobber the tracer’s own working cells, because each tracer uses its own PREG, JREG, AREG, etc. (the “(a)–(c)” restrictions listed under the exercises).
No infinite regress, no mutual recursion of control. When T₁ encounters a jump in T₂’s code, it rewrites the copied instruction so the jump targets T₁’s JUMP label; control therefore stays inside T₁, which then updates the simulated J and continues. T₂ never gains control flow; it remains merely the subject being stepped.

Net effect

T₁ simply traces T₂ as if T₂ were an ordinary program. You get a trace of T₂’s instructions executing; T₂’s internal attempt to trace T₁ never actually executes T₁’s instructions.
No paradox or endless slowdown chain occurs here (contrast with the self-trace thought experiment of exercise 4, where self-modification can immediately derail the trace via PREG being clobbered).
If both copies tried to print tracing information to the same device, that would violate restriction (6b) and garble output, but the skeleton routine shown doesn’t output registers at all (that part was explicitly omitted).

Conclusion. With two copies placed in disjoint memory and each set to trace the other, the one you start (T₁) retains control and cleanly steps through the other (T₂). There’s no infinite regress; it behaves just like tracing any other (nontrivial) program, only slower because that program happens to be a tracer itself.

title: 1.4.3.2 Trace routines
text:
6 [40]
Here’s a clean way to do 1.4.3.2 — Exercise 6 (“write a trace routine that is capable of tracing itself … at slower and slower speed, ad infinitum, until memory is exceeded”), built directly on Knuth’s trace routine in §1.4.3.2. I’ll (1) recall how the given trace routine works, (2) show the minimal self-bootstrapping change that makes it trace itself once (the idea of Ex. 4), then (3) turn that into an infinitely nestable, re-entrant trace by giving it tiny per-level “frames,” so multiple copies of the trace can be active at once without stepping on each other’s working cells, and (4) point to where to splice in the “print one step” action. Citations refer to the exact listing and the exercise page in the book PDF you linked.

What the provided trace routine already does

Knuth’s routine is invoked when the driver jumps to label ENTER with:

rJ = start address of the code to trace,
rX = address where tracing must stop (exclusive).

On entry it saves rA and rJ, loads the simulated program counter from rJ, fetches the next instruction word, rewrites certain instructions (e.g., jumps) so the monitor keeps control, executes the instruction via the pseudo-op line INST NOP *, advances to the next location, and repeats; special logic handles jumps and JSJ so the simulated J is correct. Finally it restores rA and exits at LEAVE/LEAVEX. (See lines 02–08 for setup, 10–16 decode, 27–47 jump handling, 34/36–38 execute-and-advance, 48–49 leave.)
Knuth explicitly notes this listing is just the control core of a trace: it doesn’t print; printing/register-dump is left to exercises.

Make it trace itself once (the Ex. 4 idea you build on)

Place a tiny prologue immediately before label ENTER:
SELF   ENTX  LEAVEX      ; inner trace stops at our LEAVEX
       JSJ   ENTER       ; start a fresh trace instance, sets rJ to SELF+1
ENTER  ...               ; (the original routine follows)
Why this works (and why we use JSJ, not JMP):

When the outer monitor first “executes” JSJ ENTER as an instruction of the traced program, its jump logic recognizes a jump and treats JSJ specially: it does not overwrite the simulated J with a “fall-through” value (lines 39–47 check for JSJ and avoid updating JREG in that case), then sets the simulated PC to ENTER. Thus the inner monitor instance starts with rJ = SELF+1 and begins tracing the trace program itself starting at ENTER. From now on, the outer level prints every step the inner monitor takes, and the inner monitor will (once we add printing) print every step of its own traced program.

Knuth asks you to think about the simpler version with ENTX LEAVEX; JMP *+1 in Ex. 4; swapping the second instruction to JSJ ENTER is the right subroutine-style call that seeds the next level with a proper J. The exercise statement for 6 explicitly says “in the sense of exercise 4,” i.e., the same bootstrapping trick but continuing indefinitely.
If you stop here, you can get one active monitor tracing itself, but as soon as that inner instance tries to start another instance, all copies will fight over the same scratch words (AREG, JREG, PREG, INST, INST1, TEST, …). We need re-entrancy.

Make the trace re-entrant with per-level frames (the key to “ad infinitum”)

The original routine uses fixed global cells (labels like AREG, JREG, PREG, INST, INST1, TEST, LEAVEX). To support unlimited nesting, give each active “level” its own copy of those words—i.e., a frame—and index all accesses via, say, rI1 as a frame pointer.
3.1 Define the frame layout (somewhere near the routine)
; One frame per nesting level (10 words is enough for the listed cells)
FRAME_SZ  EQU  10
FR_AREG   EQU  0     ; saved rA
FR_JREG   EQU  1     ; saved rJ (simulated)
FR_PREG   EQU  2     ; simulated PC (location being traced)
FR_INST   EQU  3
FR_INST1  EQU  4
FR_TEST   EQU  5
FR_LEAVEX EQU  6     ; we keep its absolute addr here for this level
; ... a couple spare words
Reserve a small stack of frames (e.g., 100 levels):
FRAMES    ORIG *+100*FRAME_SZ
Maintain rI1 = base of current frame. On each entry, bump it to the next frame; on each leave, drop it.
3.2 Patch the routine to index frame fields by ,1
Example patches (showing the idea; you’d apply this uniformly):

Save/restore rA:

old: STA AREG / LDA AREG
new: STA FR_AREG,1 / LDA FR_AREG,1

Save rJ and use it later:

old: STJ JREG / LDA JREG(0:2) etc.
new: STJ FR_JREG,1 / LDA FR_JREG,1(0:2)

Keep the “next instruction address”:

old: STA PREG(0:2) / LDA PREG(0:2)
new: STA FR_PREG,1(0:2) / LDA FR_PREG,1(0:2)

INST and INST1 similarly become FR_INST,1 and FR_INST1,1.
The exit address:

On entry, copy rX into FR_LEAVEX,1(0:2) and test against that field instead of a single global TEST/LEAVEX.


Concretely, the very first lines change to (comments show what moved):
SELF      ENTX  FR_LEAVEX,1(0:2)   ; stop at this level’s LEAVEX
          JSJ   ENTER

ENTER     ENTA  FRAME_SZ
          ENT2  FRAMES             ; rI2 = base of all frames
          ADD   LEVEL              ; LEVEL holds current depth (init 0)
          MUL   =1=                ; (cheap way to scale by FRAME_SZ if needed)
          ADD   rI2                ; compute next frame base
          ST1   LEVEL              ; bump depth counter
          LDX   =0=
          STX   FR_AREG,1          ; clear a few words if desired

          STX   FR_TEST,1(0:2)     ; put stop addr from rX
          STX   FR_LEAVEX,1(0:2)
          STA   FR_AREG,1
          STJ   FR_JREG,1
          LDA   FR_JREG,1(0:2)     ; start location for tracing
CYCLE     STA   FR_PREG,1(0:2)
...
TEST      DECA  *                   ; compare FR_PREG vs FR_LEAVEX for exit
          JAZ   LEAVE
PREG      LDA   *                   ; fetch instr at FR_PREG
          STA   FR_INST,1
...
(You don’t need to literally copy/paste the above; the point is every variable access acquires a ,1 and the constant cells become offsets within the current frame.)
3.3 Leave drops the frame and returns
At LEAVE:
LEAVE     LDA  FR_AREG,1
          ; rI1 := rI1 - FRAME_SZ (restore previous level’s frame)
          ENTA  -FRAME_SZ
          ADD   rI1
          ENT1  0,0                ; move pointer
          ; also decrement LEVEL (optional book-keeping)
          LEAVEX JMP  *            ; same “stop tracing”
The only global state left is the code itself and the tiny depth/stack base variables; all per-step scratch lives in that level’s frame.
With the routine re-entrant, executing the two-instruction SELF prologue starts a fresh instance that uses its own frame, while the outer instance continues stepping and printing. When the inner instance reaches its own SELF, it starts yet another one, and so on—until your FRAMES area runs out, which is exactly the “until memory capacity is exceeded” stopping condition the exercise asks for.

Where to “print one step”

Knuth’s core doesn’t print; he says you must add that (e.g., Ex. 2 writes a 10-word snapshot per step to tape 0). The natural place to emit output for the current step is after you’ve decoded the instruction and before executing it (i.e., after lines 10–16 in the listing), or immediately after execution (line 35). In a short solution, printing just the location and the instruction word is fine:

Just before INST NOP * (line 34), do:

          LDA  FR_PREG,1(0:2)
          STA  BUF                     ; word 1: location
          LDA  FR_INST,1
          STA  BUF+1                   ; word 2: instr word
          OUT  BUF(18)                 ; print one short line on device 18
Replace BUF with a tiny two-word buffer you’ve put near the routine. (If you already solved Ex. 2 and wrote the richer 10-word record to tape unit 0, call that writer here instead.)

Why the speeds keep dropping


Level 0 (outermost) prints every step the program under trace performs.
When the under-trace program is itself a trace, that inner trace prints too—so each inner step now costs “execute the step” + “inner print” + “outer print”.
The inner trace’s own under-trace is another trace, etc. Each level adds another layer of output and control logic around a single MIX instruction; hence the geometric slowdown the problem statement describes.


Driver to start the cascade

A tiny starter sets rJ to SELF of your trace and rX to its LEAVEX, then jumps to ENTER. (In MIX, you can seed rJ by entering via JSJ SELF and arranging that SELF immediately “calls” ENTER as in the prologue above, so the first inner instance starts correctly; your outermost instance is started by your hand—e.g., from a small harness.)

Safety/notes


Knuth lists three simple restrictions on the traced program (don’t store into the trace’s cells; don’t use the output device the trace uses; expect slowdown). Using frames fixes the first; just be sure your traced program doesn’t OUT to the same unit you’re using.
His jump-handling already special-cases JSJ, which is crucial for the self-bootstrapping prologue to work.

title: 1.4.3.2 Trace routines
text:
7 [25]

What the exercise asks

Design a jump trace that emits far less output than a full trace. Instead of dumping registers every step, it should output a sequence of pairs \((x₁, y₁), (x₂, y₂), \dots\) meaning “the program jumped from location \(x_i\) to \(y_i\), then ran straight-line code from \(y_i\) through \(x_{i+1}\), then jumped from \(x_{i+1}\) to \(y_{i+1}\), …”. From such pairs, a post-pass can reconstruct control flow and count how often each instruction executed. 

Where to hook into the given trace routine

Knuth’s trace routine already detects jumps and centralizes their handling at label JUMP. The fetch/decode loop keeps the fetch address in PREG(0:2) (the “location of the next instruction”), and when a jump occurs execution transfers to JUMP, where the target address (effective address of the jump) is computed into register A just before control returns to CYCLE (which then loads that A-value into PREG for the next iteration). This is the perfect point to record our pair \(x, y\). 
Concretely (from the text routine):

CYCLE stores the fetch location into PREG(0:2), fetches the instruction at that address, and identifies jumps.
When a jump is seen, code branches to JUMP. There the routine finalizes jump semantics (special-cases JSJ, updates JREG when appropriate), then does INST1 ENTA * so A = target address; finally it JMP CYCLE to continue from the jump target. 

Therefore, we can log:

\(x\) = the address of the jump instruction = PREG(0:2) at the time the jump is recognized,
\(y\) = the jump target = the value placed into A at label JUMP just before JMP CYCLE. 


Minimal recording logic (code sketch)

Goals: (a) very low overhead per jump, (b) no register clobbering visible to the traced program, (c) buffered output (pairs take two words), (d) output to a device not used by the program being traced (e.g., tape unit 0). 
We’ll modify the block at JUMP (after it has computed the target in A), insert a small snippet to append (x, y) to a memory buffer, and flush the buffer to tape when full.
Data (reserved somewhere outside the traced program’s memory):
BUF     CON  0              * start of a 100-word block
...     (reserve 100 words total for one tape block)
END     EQU  BUF+100        * end of block
PTR     CON  BUF            * current write position (absolute address)
I1REG   CON  0              * saved I1
YHOLD   CON  0              * temp to hold y
ONE     CON  1
TWO     CON  2
At routine entry (ENTER): save I1 once (we’ll use I1 as an internal pointer) and ensure tape 0 is idle.
ST1   I1REG
JBUS  *(0)                  * wait until tape 0 is not busy
STA   AREG                  * (this is already in Knuth’s code)
STJ   JREG                  * (already)
...
(“Use minimal registers” is an ideal; for practical, simple buffering we momentarily use I1 and restore it on exit. The original trace avoided using registers other than A; here we use I1 carefully and restore it. )
At label JUMP (after lines that update JREG and just after INST1 ENTA * puts the target into A):
// Existing code:
JUMP    LDA   8B(4:5)       * as in the book
        SUB   INST(4:5)     * detect JSJ, etc.
        JAZ   *+4
        LDA   PREG(0:2)
        INCA  1
        STA   JREG(0:2)
INST1   ENTA  *             * <= A now holds jump target "y"

// INSERT begin: record (x,y) to [PTR], [PTR+1]

        STA   YHOLD         * save y (target) temporarily
        LDA   PREG(0:2)     * load x = address of jump instr
        LD1   PTR           * I1 := current dest address
        STA   0,1           * store x at M[PTR]
        LDA   YHOLD         * restore y
        STA   1,1           * store y at M[PTR+1]
        ENT1  2,1           * I1 := I1 + 2
        ST1   PTR           * PTR := PTR + 2

        * If buffer full, flush to tape 0 and reset PTR:
        CMP1  =END=         * compare I1 with END
        J1N   TRACE_CONT    * not full yet
        * flush:
        OUT   BUF,0         * start writing 100 words to tape unit 0
        JBUS  *(0)          * wait completion (conservative)
        ENT1  BUF           * I1 = BUF
        ST1   PTR           * PTR := BUF
TRACE_CONT:

// INSERT end

        JMP   CYCLE         * existing: continue at new address
Remarks:

We only touch I1, and we save/restore it at the beginning/end of the whole trace; the traced program never observes a changed I1 because the trace wrapper owns it during tracing. (If you want to be extra strict, you can re-save/re-restore I1 inside JUMP too, but it’s unnecessary if the trace routine always retains control.) 
The snippet is constant-time per jump: a handful of STA/LDA plus one indexed STA pair and a simple two-word pointer bump.
On exit from tracing (LEAVE/LEAVEX), flush any partial block (if PTR != BUF) and then LD1 I1REG to restore I1, just like we restored A and J in the base routine. (Knuth already notes J needs restoring; exercise 1 in this section has you fix that; do both.) 


Buffering/output strategy and why tape


Why tape (unit 0)? It’s far faster and far less intrusive than printing lines; the book explicitly hints that tape is preferable to printing for trace output. 
Asynchronous I/O: We write a full 100-word block when the buffer fills (50 jump pairs). In typical code, the number of jumps is far less than the number of executed instructions, so output volume and I/O frequency are much smaller than with a full step trace.
Exclusivity: The traced program must not use the same device; Knuth calls this out (don’t use the output device being used for the trace) — so dedicate tape 0 to the jump trace, and choose another device for the program (or vice versa). 


Notes, correctness, and limitations


What counts as a “jump”? The existing trace detects all jump opcodes (JMP, Jx, Jxx, JSJ) and also treats JBUS specially (since it can effect control transfer). We piggyback on that detection; our code runs only when a jump actually occurs. 
Source and target correctness: At jump time, PREG(0:2) is the address of the jump instruction being executed (our \(x\)); after INST1 ENTA *, A holds the effective destination (our \(y\)). That aligns exactly with the exercise’s notion of pairs. 
Post-pass reconstruction: A reader can process the pair stream (x₁,y₁), (x₂,y₂), … and:

mark basic blocks as \([y_i, x_{i+1}]\),
accumulate execution counts by adding the length of each straight-line interval,
reconstruct the control-flow graph edges from each \(x_i \to y_i\).

Self-modifying code caveat: The book notes that self-modifying code can defeat a jump-trace profiler; e.g., if an instruction changes another instruction into or out of a jump, the pair stream may no longer reflect the original static flow. Keep this limitation in mind. 
Register hygiene: We intentionally restrict ourselves to A and a temporary use of I1 (saved/restored), in keeping with the trace routine’s philosophy of not disturbing the program state. 

By inserting ~15 tight instructions at JUMP, storing \((x,y)=(\text{PREG(0:2)},\text{A})\) to a buffered block and flushing to tape, you get an efficient jump trace with tiny runtime overhead and dramatically reduced output size, while preserving the traced program’s state. This meets the exercise’s specification and leverages the exact structure of the trace routine given in §1.4.3.2.

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
1 [05]
Context you need
On MIX, IN initiates an input transfer and returns immediately; the program may continue executing, but it must not rely on the input buffer’s contents until the device is no longer busy (e.g., until JBUS shows “ready”). Sequence (3) relies on that rule: it waits with JBUS, then copies from the just-filled buffer, and finally starts the next input into that same buffer.
Incorrect. If you copy from locations 2000–2099 before JBUS has confirmed completion, you may be moving words that haven’t arrived yet (or are only partially written). The model explicitly warns that input completes only after a subsequent I/O op or a successful “not busy” test; otherwise the buffer contents are not guaranteed. So the copied block could be incomplete/corrupted.
Verdict: No. The input may not yet be complete.
Also incorrect (race hazard). If you start the next IN 2000(5) first and only then perform MOVE 2000(50); MOVE 2050(50), you are copying from a buffer that the device is simultaneously overwriting with the next block. If the input device outpaces the CPU even slightly, some words you copy will belong to the next block rather than the previous one, yielding a mixed/corrupted result.
Verdict: No. The device might outrun the MOVE, so the copy can be contaminated by the new transfer.
Final answers
(a) No. You might move data before the current input has finished. (b) No. Starting IN first risks the device overwriting the buffer while you copy, so the MOVE can pick up the wrong data.

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
Putting MOVEs before JBUS




Putting MOVEs after IN

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
2 [10]
Here’s a buffered-output scheme that mirrors Knuth’s buffered-input setup (his items (2) and (3)) but for writing tape unit 6. We’ll stage each 100-word block for output in an auxiliary buffer at addresses 2000–2099, while your program prepares the next 100-word block at 1000–1099. This overlaps CPU work with device I/O, just as his input pipeline does. The exercise statement appears at the end of §1.4.4; Knuth’s earlier unbuffered pair for output is OUT 1000(6); JBUS *(6) and his input buffering sequences (2)–(3) are the model we’re copying. 
Setup and notation (MIX reminders)

A tape OUT A(6) starts writing 100 words beginning at A to unit 6.
JBUS *(6) waits until unit 6 is no longer busy.
MOVE A(50) copies 50 words starting at A into the destination pointed to by rI1, then advances rI1 by 50 (so two MOVE’s copy 100 words). Knuth’s input example sets rI1 to the destination just before the two MOVE’s. 

Buffered output: two phases

Prime the pipeline (first block)

Before the device can “trail” your computation, you must place the first block into the staging buffer and start the first write:
ENT1 2000          ; rI1 ← 2000 (destination = buffer)
MOVE 1000(50)      ; (1000–1049) → (2000–2049)
MOVE 1050(50)      ; (1050–1099) → (2050–2099)
OUT  2000(6)       ; start writing the staged block to tape unit 6
Explanation: you’ve copied the current block (prepared at 1000–1099) into 2000–2099 and kicked off the OUT. While the device is busy writing that buffer, your program is now free to compute/fill the next 100-word block at 1000–1099. (This mirrors Knuth’s “read-ahead” input, but here it’s “write-behind”: the device is finishing the previous buffer while the CPU prepares the next one.) 

Steady-state cycle (each subsequent block)

Whenever the next 100-word block at 1000–1099 is ready to be sent:
ENT1 2000          ; rI1 ← 2000 (dest = buffer), harmless if repeated
JBUS *(6)          ; wait until the prior OUT from 2000–2099 has finished
MOVE 1000(50)      ; (1000–1049) → (2000–2049)
MOVE 1050(50)      ; (1050–1099) → (2050–2099)
OUT  2000(6)       ; start writing this staged block
Why this order? You must not overwrite 2000–2099 while the device is still taking data from it; therefore the JBUS precedes the MOVE instructions, just as in Knuth’s input sequence (3) the JBUS precedes the MOVE to ensure the fresh input has arrived before moving it out of the staging area. This is the exact analog of his (2)–(3), but with roles reversed. 
Finishing up
After you’ve initiated the final OUT 2000(6), wait for the device to complete before you stop or reuse the buffer:
JBUS *(6)          ; ensure the last block has been fully written
(Stopping early risks data loss; the same caveat applies to buffered input and is discussed around Knuth’s examples.) 
Why it works

Safety. The JBUS *(6) before each pair of MOVEs prevents clobbering the very buffer the device is still using, exactly like waiting for input completion before moving data out of the input buffer in (3).
Overlap. While the device streams the previous block from 2000–2099, your program is free to prepare the next block at 1000–1099, so computation and I/O overlap. This is the write-side counterpart of Knuth’s “buffering” idea that overlaps I/O with computation.

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
3 [22]
Design notes

Two 100-word buffers live in memory, each followed by a single “link” word that holds the address of the other buffer. This is exactly analogous to the input routine in the text (which links its two buffers in lines 07, 11, 14 of (4)).
We keep the base address of the current output buffer in CUR.
Index register I5 holds the current position in the buffer as a negative count: it starts at −100 and is incremented each word; when it hits 0 the buffer is full. This lets us test fullness with a single J5N (jump if negative).
When a buffer fills, we self-modify the address field of a single OUT instruction to the “old” buffer’s base, execute it, then swap CUR to the other buffer and reset I5 to −100. (This is the same self-modifying trick used for subroutine linkage and in many MIX examples; see the standard STJ EXIT … EXIT JMP * pattern. )
No JBUS is needed during steady-state operation (same reason as for input (4)): we start the next I/O before we touch the previous buffer again.
At program end we pad with zeros if needed, OUT the final partial block, and then do a single JBUS *(V) to wait for the last I/O to complete.

* ====== Constants / device selection ======
V     EQU 1              * Tape unit to use (pick any valid unit id)
ZERO  EQU 0

* ====== Buffer areas (100 words each) + one link word ======
OUTBUF1 ORIG *+100       * 100 data words
LINK1   CON OUTBUF2      * address of the other buffer

OUTBUF2 ORIG *+100
LINK2   CON OUTBUF1

* ====== State ======
CUR    CON OUTBUF1       * base address of current buffer

* ====== Subroutine: WORDOUT =================================
* Calling sequence:  JMP WORDOUT
* Entry:  rA = next word to output
* Uses:   rI5 = position in current buffer (negative count), CUR = base
* Exit:   word stored; if buffer became full, a 100-word OUT is started
* Clobbers: rA (temporarily), rJ (by standard linkage), CI
*
WORDOUT STJ 9F                 * standard MIX subroutine linkage
        STA CUR+100,5          * store rA at CUR + (100 + rI5)  [slots 0..99]
        INC5 1                 * advance position
        J5N  9F                * if still negative, buffer not full => return

* -- Buffer just filled: start OUT on the full buffer, swap to the other
*    Save OLD base into OUTCMD's address field, then switch CUR to link.
        LDA CUR
        STA OUTCMD+1(0:2)      * self-modify OUT address field
        LDA CUR+100            * fetch link (address of other buffer)
        STA CUR                * CUR := other buffer base
        ENT5 -100              * reset position for the new buffer

OUTCMD  OUT OUTBUF1(V)         * OUT 100 words starting at (modified) address
9H      JMP *                  * return to caller
* ============================================================

* ====== Subroutine: FLUSHOUT ================================
* Pads current (possibly partial) buffer with zeros, OUTs it, and waits
* for the final I/O to finish. Safe to call even if buffer is exactly empty/full.
*
FLUSHOUT STJ 9F

        J5Z  FINISHWAIT        * rI5==0 => a full block was just started; just wait
* Pad with zeros until rI5 hits 0, then OUT the partial block.
PADZ    STZ CUR+100,5
        INC5 1
        J5N PADZ

        LDA CUR
        STA OUTCMD+1(0:2)      * OUT the now-full padded buffer
        OUTCMD OUT OUTBUF1(V)

FINISHWAIT
        JBUS *(V)              * wait until last OUT completes
        JMP 9F
* ============================================================

* ====== Program start (minimum required initialization) =====
*   - Choose initial buffer and set position to start of buffer.
*   - (Buffers’ link words are assembled above; no runtime init needed.)
START   ENT5 -100              * I5 = -100  (next store goes to slot 0)
        LDA =OUTBUF1=
        STA CUR                * CUR := OUTBUF1
* ... your program computes, and whenever it has a word to output:
*     LDA <word>
*     JMP WORDOUT
* ... repeat as needed ...

* ====== Program end (must flush final block) =====
DONE    JMP FLUSHOUT
        HLT
        END START
Why this works

“Buffer-swapping … analogous to (4)”: Two buffers linked to each other; when one fills we OUT it and immediately continue filling the other, with no busy-wait in the steady state.
“Index register 5 … current buffer position”: rI5 is the running position (negative count). We store at CUR+100,5 and detect a full buffer with a single J5N/rI5==0 test.
“Write 100 words onto tape unit V”: The OUT instruction is self-modified to the old buffer base so we write exactly that 100-word block on device V.
“Layout of buffer areas”: As shown—each 100-word area followed by a 1-word link holding the other buffer’s base (same linking idea as the text’s WORDIN).
“Beginning and end … first and last blocks properly written”:

Beginning: set CUR to your first buffer and rI5 := -100. That’s all.
End: call FLUSHOUT. It pads with zeros to the next 100-word boundary (if needed), OUTs the final block, then does a single JBUS *(V) to wait for completion before halting. (Padding is explicitly required.)

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
4 [M20]
Consider processing \(B\) blocks. For block \(k\) let

\(c_k\) = CPU time spent computing on that block’s data,
\(i_k\) = device time for the I/O of that block (read or write).

Define totals
\[
C=\sum_{k=1}^B c_k,\qquad I=\sum_{k=1}^B i_k.
\]
With unbuffered I/O the CPU waits during each I/O, so the elapsed time is
\[
T_{\text{unbuf}}=C+I.
\]
With buffered I/O (double buffering, for example), computing on one buffer can overlap I/O on the other. There is only one CPU and one device, so at any instant at most one CPU task and at most one device task can be active.
Fundamental lower bound (why speedup ≤ 2)
In any schedule (buffered or not):

You cannot finish earlier than \(C\), because the CPU must perform \(C\) units of work.
You cannot finish earlier than \(I\), because the device must perform \(I\) units of I/O.

Hence every buffered schedule satisfies
\[
T_{\text{buf}} \;\ge\; \max\{C,I\}.
\]
Comparing to the unbuffered time \(C+I\),
\[
\frac{T_{\text{unbuf}}}{T_{\text{buf}}}
\;\le\;
\frac{C+I}{\max\{C,I\}}
\;\le\; 2,
\]
with equality only when \(C=I\) and overlap is essentially perfect. Therefore no single-device buffering scheme can yield more than a 2× speedup relative to unbuffered execution.
Constructive schedule (why ~2× is attainable)
Assume steady per-block times \(c_k=c\) and \(i_k=i\) and use two buffers:

Start I/O for block 1 (takes \(i\)).
While that I/O runs, compute block 0’s results or prepare block 2, etc. After the pipeline fills, each additional block takes \(\max\{c,i\}\) time because CPU and device run in parallel.
The exact makespan is

\[
T_{\text{buf}} = i + (B-1)\max\{c,i\} + c,
\]
(first and last blocks incur the pipeline fill/drain). For large \(B\),
\[
T_{\text{buf}} \approx B\max\{c,i\}.
\]
Thus the asymptotic speedup is
\[
\frac{T_{\text{unbuf}}}{T_{\text{buf}}}
\approx
\frac{B(c+i)}{B\max\{c,i\}}
=
\frac{c+i}{\max\{c,i\}}
\le 2.
\]
Best case. If \(c=i\) (balanced compute and I/O), then
\[
T_{\text{unbuf}}=2Bc,\qquad
T_{\text{buf}}\approx Bc \quad(\text{plus small }O(c)\text{ edge effects}),
\]
so the speedup approaches \(2\) as \(B\) grows. This is the “favorable circumstance” where buffering nearly halves total time.
Other cases. If \(c\ll i\) (I/O-bound) or \(i\ll c\) (compute-bound), then \((c+i)/\max\{c,i\}\) is close to \(1\); buffering still helps by overlapping the smaller resource with the larger, but the improvement is strictly \(<2\) and can be modest.

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
5 [M21]
Let

\(C\): total time the program spends computing (CPU time) not counting any I/O wait.
\(D_i\): total active time needed by I/O device \(i\) (sum of the times of all blocks handled by device \(i\)), for \(i=1,\dots,n\).

Assumptions are exactly those of §1.4.4: Each device works independently once an I/O is initiated; with buffering, the CPU can run while a device is busy; multiple devices may be busy concurrently. (This is the same setting used for MIX’s buffered I/O with concurrent devices.) 
Two totals to compare:

Unbuffered baseline. I/O is blocking; devices don’t overlap with the CPU or each other.
\[
T_{\text{unbuf}}=C+\sum_{i=1}^n D_i.
\]
Buffered/overlapped. With sufficient buffering, CPU and devices can run concurrently; multiple devices can be active at once. Any schedule must last at least as long as the heaviest-used resource (CPU or any one device), hence
\[
T_{\text{buf}} \;\ge\; \max\!\bigl(C,\,D_1,\dots,D_n\bigr).
\]

(You can view this as a simple “makespan” lower bound: each of the \(n{+}1\) resources—CPU and the \(n\) devices—must process its assigned load; the overall time cannot be less than the largest load.)
General bound on speedup
Define the speedup \(S\) from buffering as
\[
S \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{unbuf}}}{T_{\text{buf}}}
\;\le\;
\frac{C+\sum_i D_i}{\max(C,\max_i D_i)}.
\tag{★}
\]
Since the maximum of \(n{+}1\) nonnegative numbers is at least their average,
\[
\max(C,D_1,\dots,D_n)\;\ge\;\frac{C+\sum_i D_i}{n+1},
\]
and plugging into (★) gives the universal bound
\[
S \;\le\; n+1.
\]
This generalizes Exercise 4’s “at most 2×” for one device (\(n=1\Rightarrow S\le 2\)). 
Tightness (when can we reach \(n{+}1\)?)
The bound is tight in “balanced” cases where
\[
C=D_1=\cdots=D_n=T.
\]
Then \(T_{\text{unbuf}}=(n+1)T\) and the lower bound for any overlapped schedule is \(T\). With well-designed multiple buffering (as in §1.4.4’s coroutine-based control) you can keep all \(n\) devices busy continuously while the CPU also computes for the full \(T\). Thus
\[
T_{\text{buf}}=T,\qquad S=\frac{(n+1)T}{T}=n+1.
\]
This is the natural multi-device analogue of the single-device “at most two, achieved when compute time equals I/O time” result. 
Intuition and scheduling sketch

Lower bound: You cannot finish earlier than the longest of \(C,D_1,\dots,D_n\).
Achieving near the bound: Use multiple buffers per device and the coroutine-style controller (§1.4.4) so that:

Each device is kept busy end-to-end (initiate the next transfer as soon as the previous completes), and
The CPU has work to do at all times (no waiting on I/O), by computing against already-filled input buffers or producing output into free buffers. This is exactly the kind of overlap Knuth’s buffer-control algorithms are designed for, and the time–action charts in the section illustrate how the overlap hides I/O behind computation (and vice versa). 


What the formula tells you in general
For an arbitrary workload, the best possible (ideal) speedup is bounded by
\[
S\;\le\;\frac{C+\sum_i D_i}{\max(C,\max_i D_i)}.
\]

If one device dominates (say \(D_k\gg C\) and \(D_k\gg D_j\) for \(j\neq k\)), then \(S\approx (C+\sum_i D_i)/D_k\), which is much smaller than \(n{+}1\).
If CPU dominates (\(C\gg D_i\)), speedup is near 1 (little to hide).
If loads are well balanced (\(C\approx D_i\)), you approach the ideal \(n{+}1\).

This fully generalizes the single-device “≤2× speedup” to \(n\) devices.

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
6 [12]
You have two symmetric choices (pick either):
IN   INBUF1(U)
ENT6 INBUF2+99
or
IN   INBUF2(U)
ENT6 INBUF1+99
Optionally precede with IOC 0(U) if you might need to rewind/ready the unit. 
Why this is the right start (step-by-step)
Recall the subroutine (4) (WORDIN) uses rI6 as a pointer and begins with:

INC6 1
LDA 0,6 and compare to SENTINEL If sentinel → refill this buffer: IN -100,6(U), then switch buffers: LD6 1,6 (loads rI6 from the “other-buffer” link), and loop to fetch the word. 

Important details from that listing:

Each buffer reserves 100 data words; at offset +100 sits a sentinel constant; at +101 sits a link to the other buffer.
rI6 “addresses the last word of input” when you enter WORDIN; the subroutine immediately does INC6 1, then fetches. 

Now follow the first call if you used the first init (IN INBUF1(U); ENT6 INBUF2+99):

Before the first call:

Buffer 1 has just been filled by IN INBUF1(U) (block #1).
rI6 = INBUF2+99 (the last data word slot of Buffer 2).

On the first WORDIN call:

INC6 1 ⇒ rI6 becomes INBUF2+100 (the sentinel slot of Buffer 2).
LDA 0,6 ⇒ you read the sentinel, so WORDIN concludes “this buffer is exhausted.”
IN -100,6(U) ⇒ reads the next block (#2) into Buffer 2.
LD6 1,6 ⇒ rI6 ← contents at INBUF2+101, which is the address of the other buffer (i.e., INBUF1).
Loop back to fetch the word: LDA 0,6 now loads the first actual data word of Buffer 1 — exactly what you want for the very first word delivered to the program — while the tape is already busy reading the subsequent block into Buffer 2.


Net effect: the very first call returns word 0 from Buffer 1, and you’re already overlapped with I/O that fills Buffer 2. From that point on, WORDIN alternates smoothly between the buffers, always keeping the device busy when possible (the “pipeline is primed”). This is exactly the behavior intended by (4).
What you must have in the data area
Your buffers must be laid out exactly as in (4):

INBUF1 and INBUF2 each reserve 100 data words.
At INBUF1+100 and INBUF2+100 place SENTINEL.
At INBUF1+101 put the address of INBUF2, and at INBUF2+101 put the address of INBUF1 (the links).

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
7 [22]
Instead of testing for a sentinel to detect “end of buffer”, keep a running count. A neat MIX trick is to keep rI6 negative and let it run from −100, −99, …, −1 inside each block:

Addressing: load the next word from BASE+100+rI6. With rI6 negative, this indexes positions 0..99 of the current buffer.
After each fetch, increment rI6. If it’s still negative, we’re still in the block; if it becomes 0, we’ve just consumed the last word, so we:

start an IN on the buffer we just finished (to read the next block into it),
reset rI6 to −100, and
switch to the other buffer.


Using a tiny coroutine hand-off lets the subroutine “enter” at the correct buffer each call without extra state variables, which is exactly the style Knuth points to as one good way to solve this exercise.
MIX program (sentinel-free WORDIN)
* Sentinel-free WORDIN: double-buffered, coroutine style.
* U = the input device number (e.g., tape 5). rI6 is our negative index counter.

INBUF1     ORIG *+100           * 100-word buffer #1
INBUF2     ORIG *+100           * 100-word buffer #2

WORDIN     STJ  NEXT            * Patch NEXT with caller's return address.
NEXT       JMP  USE2            * First entry jumps into buffer #2’s path (can choose either).

* --- Use buffer #2 until it’s exhausted, then refill #2 and switch to #1 ---
USE2       LDA  INBUF2+100,6    * A ← INBUF2[100 + rI6]  (rI6 is negative ⇒ 0..99)
           JMP  RET             * Return this word to caller.
           INC6 1               * Advance position in the current block.
           J6N  USE2            * Still negative? More words in this block → stay.
           IN   INBUF2(U)       * rI6 just hit 0 ⇒ start reading next block into buffer #2.
           ENN6 100             * rI6 ← −100  (start of next block)
           JMP  USE1            * Switch to buffer #1 for subsequent words.

* --- Use buffer #1 until it’s exhausted, then refill #1 and switch to #2 ---
USE1       LDA  INBUF1+100,6
           JMP  RET
           INC6 1
           J6N  USE1
           IN   INBUF1(U)
           ENN6 100
           JMP  USE2

* --- Return site. STJ above patched NEXT to point back to the proper entry on the next call. ---
RET        STJ  NEXT            * Store rJ into the address field of NEXT (classic MIX return trick).
           JMP  *               * Jump to that saved address (i.e., return to caller).
How to bootstrap (same spirit as Ex. 6)
Before the first call to WORDIN, do the usual startup so the first fetch is correct and I/O is overlapped:

Initialize the counter: ENN6 100 (so rI6 = −100).
Prefetch one buffer (either is fine), e.g.: IN INBUF2(U).
Leave NEXT as shown (JMP USE2) so the first call uses buffer #2’s path. (You could swap the roles of 1/2 if you prefer.) These are exactly the kind of “get it started right” steps noted next to program (4).

Why it works (and why it’s fast)

Correctness. Each call returns exactly one word (from the current buffer at offset 100+rI6). When rI6 reaches 0, we (i) initiate an input on the buffer we just finished, (ii) reset rI6 to −100, and (iii) continue from the other buffer. That reproduces the overlapped “read-ahead” behavior of (4), but without testing a sentinel; the sign of rI6 is our end-of-buffer test. The coroutine hand-off (STJ NEXT + JMP USE1/USE2) selects the correct entry for the next call, so the caller remains oblivious.
Cost. As in the sentinel version, each word costs one indexed load plus a couple of very cheap instructions, and at block boundaries we pay for one IN and one ENN6. With read-ahead active, the per-block time is max(C, T) (C = compute per block, T = device time), just like the buffered methods in the text.

Notes

The routine assumes full 100-word blocks from the device, as in (4). If your data format has a separate notion of logical end-of-input (e.g., a trailing card with a special mark), handle that in the surrounding control coroutine/program exactly as in the section’s examples.
If you prefer not to use coroutines, you can keep a “current-buffer base” pointer in memory and branch between two short “use buffer” paths with an explicit test on rI6; the coroutine version above simply removes that extra pointer by letting the entry point itself encode which buffer is current. (Knuth’s official answer illustrates the coroutine approach.)

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
8 [11]
What the colors mean for output

Green (G) = a free buffer that can be ASSIGNed and filled with the next line image.
Yellow (Y) = the current buffer the program is building between ASSIGN and RELEASE.
Red (R) = a buffer that has been RELEASEd and now contains a complete line image, ready for the printer (or actively being printed if it’s the one pointed to by NEXTR). 

The pointers are as in the text: NEXTG → “next green” (where the next ASSIGN will hand you a free buffer), NEXTR → “next red” (what the output device will print next), CURRENT → the yellow (assigned) buffer, if any. 
Interpreting the figure sequence for output
State shown in Fig. 23 (before we start the three snapshots in Fig. 24)
At that instant there are several green buffers (free space is available) and two red buffers; the red buffer indicated by NEXTR is currently being printed by the line printer. At the same time, the program is computing between RELEASE and ASSIGN; i.e., it has finished one line (so that buffer turned red) and hasn’t yet requested a fresh buffer for the next line. 

After ASSIGN

The program calls ASSIGN. The buffer pointed to by NEXTG turns yellow and becomes CURRENT; NEXTG advances clockwise to the next green. The program now begins to compose the next line image into this yellow buffer while the printer continues with its current red buffer. 

After output completion (I/O complete)

When the printer finishes with the red buffer it was printing, that buffer turns green (it’s now free). NEXTR advances clockwise to the next red buffer (if any). If another red buffer exists, the printer immediately starts on it; otherwise the device becomes idle and will resume only when a red buffer appears later. 

After RELEASE

When the program RELEASEs the yellow buffer (line image completed), that buffer turns red (ready to print). If the device had been idle (no red buffers), it can now begin printing this newly red buffer (NEXTR will pick it up next); otherwise it joins the queue of red buffers that the printer will reach when NEXTR cycles around.

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
9 [21]
Model of operation (quick recap)

Buffers cycle through colors: green (free) → yellow (current/being filled) → red (ready for output) → green.
ASSIGN picks the next green buffer (if none are green, ASSIGN must wait).
RELEASE turns the current yellow buffer red and increments the count n of red buffers.
The CONTROL coroutine:

If the device is idle and n>0, it initiates OUT on the buffer pointed to by NEXTR (taking 7500u), then advances NEXTR and decrements n when output completes.

All “compute for T” durations are CPU time only; if ASSIGN has to wait for a green buffer, the CPU is idle during that wait (this is explicitly part of the exercise).

Deriving the schedule (walkthrough)
Let buffers be BUF1 → BUF2 → BUF3 in the ring. Initially all are green; NEXTR = NEXTG = BUF1; device idle.
t = 0: ASSIGN(BUF1) → BUF1 turns yellow. Compute 1000u.
t = 1000: RELEASE → BUF1 becomes red (n=1). Device is idle and n>0, so OUT BUF1 starts (finishes at 8500). Compute next 1000u.
t = 2000: ASSIGN(BUF2) (green) → yellow. Compute 1000u.
t = 3000: RELEASE → BUF2 red (n=2). Device busy (till 8500). Compute 1000u.
t = 4000: ASSIGN(BUF3) (green) → yellow. Compute 1000u.
t = 5000: RELEASE → BUF3 red (n=3). Device still busy. Compute 1000u.
t = 6000: Attempt ASSIGN, but no green buffers (all three are red or in output), so ASSIGN waits. CPU idle from 6000 onward until a buffer frees.
t = 8500: OUT BUF1 completes → BUF1 turns green, n drops to 2. The pending ASSIGN now succeeds immediately: BUF1 assigned (yellow). Also, since device idle and n>0, CONTROL immediately starts OUT BUF2 (finishes at 16000). Compute 1000u.
t = 9500: RELEASE → BUF1 red (n=3 again). Device busy (till 16000). Compute continues; the next ASSIGN arrives after 1000u, but there are still no greens, so it will wait:
t = 10500: ASSIGN waits (second idle stretch for the CPU).
t = 16000: OUT BUF2 completes → BUF2 green, n→2. Waiting ASSIGN succeeds: assign BUF2 (yellow). Device immediately starts OUT BUF3 (finishes at 23500). Continuing this same reasoning—each time a RELEASE makes a buffer red it increments n; when the device goes idle and n>0 it starts the next OUT; when ASSIGN encounters no greens it waits until the next OUT completion—produces the full time–action table below.
Time–action chart (result)
The complete schedule (events in time order) is:

0 ASSIGN(BUF1)
1000 RELEASE, OUT BUF1
2000 ASSIGN(BUF2)
3000 RELEASE
4000 ASSIGN(BUF3)
5000 RELEASE
6000 ASSIGN (wait)
8500 BUF1 assigned, OUT BUF2
9500 RELEASE
10500 ASSIGN (wait)
16000 BUF2 assigned, OUT BUF3
23000 RELEASE
23500 OUT BUF1
28000 ASSIGN(BUF3)
31000 OUT BUF2
35000 RELEASE
38500 OUT BUF3
40000 ASSIGN(BUF1)
46000 Output stops
47000 RELEASE, OUT BUF1
52000 ASSIGN(BUF2)
54500 Output stops
59000 RELEASE, OUT BUF2
64000 ASSIGN(BUF3)
65000 RELEASE
66000 ASSIGN(BUF1)
66500 OUT BUF3
68000 RELEASE
69000 Computation stops
74000 OUT BUF1
81500 Output stops.

Totals and idle times
From the chart:

Total elapsed time: 81500u.
CPU idle (waiting at ASSIGN): 6000–8500, 10500–16000, 69000–81500 ⇒ 20500u.
Device idle: 0–1000, 46000–47000, 54500–59000 ⇒ 6500u.

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
10 [21]

Use the same compute sequence from Ex. 9 (A = ASSIGN, R = RELEASE), where the numbers are pure compute times (waiting time isn’t included): A, 1000, R, 1000,  A, 1000, R, 1000,  A, 1000, R, 1000,  A, 1000, R, 7000,  A, 5000, R, 7000,  A, 5000, R, 7000,  A, 5000, R, 7000,  A, 5000, R, 1000,  A, 1000, R, 2000,  A, 1000, R. (Same list as Fig. 27/Ex. 9.) The output device runs at 7500u per block.
For output: ASSIGN grabs a green buffer (turns it yellow), RELEASE turns that yellow buffer red; the control starts OUT on the next red buffer whenever idle; when the output finishes, that buffer becomes green. With N = 4 buffers, the program need only wait at an ASSIGN if all four buffers are red (no greens).

Time–action chart (N = 4)
Below, “OUT BUFk” marks the instant an output of BUFk begins; “Output stops.” marks instants when the device goes idle because no red buffers remain.
Time   Action
-----  ---------------------------------------
0      ASSIGN(BUF1)
1000   RELEASE, OUT BUF1
2000   ASSIGN(BUF2)
3000   RELEASE
4000   ASSIGN(BUF3)
5000   RELEASE
6000   ASSIGN(BUF4)
7000   RELEASE
8500   OUT BUF2
14000  ASSIGN(BUF1)
16000  OUT BUF3
19000  RELEASE
23500  OUT BUF4
26000  ASSIGN(BUF2)
31000  OUT BUF1
31000  RELEASE
38000  ASSIGN(BUF3)
38500  OUT BUF2
43000  RELEASE
46000  OUT BUF3
50000  ASSIGN(BUF4)
53500  Output stops.
55000  RELEASE, OUT BUF4
56000  ASSIGN(BUF1)
57000  RELEASE
59000  ASSIGN(BUF2)
60000  RELEASE
61000  Computation stops.
62500  OUT BUF1
70000  OUT BUF2
77500  Output stops.
How this unfolds (sketch): early on the program is producing red buffers faster than the first output can finish, so red buffers accumulate until the first finish at 8500u; from then through 53500u the device is almost continuously busy draining reds as they arrive. There is no “ASSIGN (wait)” event with four buffers, because whenever an ASSIGN occurs there is always at least one green available (n never reaches 4 at an ASSIGN instant).
Totals and idle intervals

Total elapsed time: 77500u (last “Output stops.”).
Computation stops: at 61000u (there were no ASSIGN waits at all).
Computer idle time: 61000–77500 ⇒ 16500u.
Output device idle intervals: 0–1000 and 53500–55000 ⇒ 2500u total. (Contrast with the three-buffer case in Ex. 9’s chart, where total time was 81500u and there were two mid-run computation waits.)

If you’d like, I can also tabulate buffer colors (G/Y/R) over time or show the evolving queue of reds to make the coroutine interplay completely transparent.

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
11 [21]
We apply the text’s buffering algorithms (A for ASSIGN, R for RELEASE, B for buffer control): at ASSIGN the program waits if all buffers are red; at RELEASE we increment the red-buffer count and the controller starts output if the device is idle; when I/O completes, the controller advances NEXTR and decrements the red-buffer count.
Because N=1, once we RELEASE the only buffer, the device is busy until it finishes that block; the program cannot ASSIGN again (A1 stalls) until I/O completes. Thus computation and output cannot overlap with a single buffer. (Contrast with the three-buffer timeline and chart for Fig. 27.)
