(Style follows the sample chart in the book: times at which ASSIGN, RELEASE, and I/O starts/stops occur as the system runs. For reference, the sample chart for three buffers appears just before the exercise list, with its accompanying time-action table. )
Totals and discussion

Total elapsed time: 118 500u (final “Output stops”).
Device busy time: 11 blocks × 7 500u = 82 500u; device idle: 36 000u.
Computer idle time (stalls at A1): 49 000u (the sum of all “ASSIGN (wait) → BUF1 assigned” gaps).

With three buffers, the text’s chart finishes in 81 500u for the same program; with a single buffer we stretch to 118 500u, because every post-RELEASE ASSIGN must wait for the device to finish before computation can continue. Buffering depth is exactly what allows overlap between computing and I/O; with N=1 there is no overlap at all.

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
12 [24]
Minimal algorithmic change (Algorithm B → B′)
Add a single global flag EOF (initially 0). Then:

B2′ (gate before initiating I/O): If EOF = 1 or n = 0, go to B1 (compute). Otherwise proceed to B3.
B4′ (just after I/O completes, before advancing NEXTR): Inspect the 80th column of the buffer that was just filled (i.e., at address NEXTR + 15, byte 5). If it is '.', set EOF ← 1. Then continue with B5/B6 (advance NEXTR; n ← n − 1).

Everything else (ASSIGN, RELEASE, pointer motion, colors) stays as Knuth describes. The placement is important: you test for '.' after the IN you started in B3 has finished (B4) and before you move NEXTR in B5. 
MIX patch (Program B → Program B′)
Below is a drop-in patch to Knuth’s Program B (CONTROL). It assumes:

rI6 ≡ n (count of red buffers), rI5 ≡ NEXTR (address of current red buffer’s start), exactly as in the text.
CARDS EQU 16 elsewhere; U already holds the unit (as in the book’s code).
EOF is a one-word flag (0/1) you define in static storage (initialized to 0).
DOT is the MIX character code for '.' (you can define it as a constant; any correct value for your MIXAL assembler is fine).
Field (5:5) denotes the rightmost byte in a word.

Only the new and changed lines are annotated; unchanged lines are shown for context:
* --- New data ---
EOF     CON 0            * 0 = keep reading; 1 = stop initiating input
DOT     CON 46           * MIX code for '.' (period); use your table/assembler symbol

* --- CONTROL coroutine (Program B′) ---
* rI6 ≡ n, rI5 ≡ NEXTR
CONT1   JMP COMPUTE          * B1. Compute (unchanged)

1H      LD1 EOF              * NEW: test end-of-input latch
        J1NZ CONT1           * If EOF=1, skip initiating more reads (go compute)
        J6Z *-1              * B2′: if n = 0, wait (as before)

        IN  0,5(U)           * B3. Initiate I/O into buffer at NEXTR
        JMP COMPUTE          * B4. Compute until I/O completes (JRED brings us back)

* --- We return here when the reader signals ready (after IN has finished) ---

        LDA 15,5(5:5)        * NEW (B4′): load col 80 = byte 5 of word (NEXTR+15)
        CMPA =DOT=           * Is it a '.' ?
        JNE  2F              * If not, continue normally
        ENTA 1               * Else set EOF ← 1
        STA  EOF
2H      LD5 -1,5             * B5. Advance NEXTR to next buffer (via link at [NEXTR-1])
        DEC6 1               * B6. n ← n - 1
        JMP  1B

The gate at label 1H enforces “no more IN” once EOF is set.
The check immediately after the JMP COMPUTE (on return) looks at exactly the last column of the just-read card and latches EOF if it’s '.'.
We deliberately perform the test before LD5 -1,5 so rI5 still points at the buffer we just filled.

Why this is correct

In Algorithm B, the moment a read completes (B4), the freshly read card still resides at NEXTR; only then do we advance NEXTR (B5). Inserting the test between B4 and B5 lets us examine the right card every time. 
Once EOF is latched, B2′ prevents any further IN from being initiated. Already-read buffers will continue to flow to the program (green→yellow→red) via ASSIGN/RELEASE exactly as before.
The mapping “card reader → 16 words, 5 chars/word” makes column 80 equal to (NEXTR + 15, byte 5), which is the byte we test. 


Here’s a clean way to shut input down the moment the “end card” is read (a card whose column 80 is ‘.’), by minimally extending Knuth’s multiple-buffer control coroutine.
What changes conceptually
In the textbook, the CONTROL coroutine (Algorithm B / Program B) repeatedly:

waits until there is at least one red buffer (n > 0),
starts an IN on the buffer at NEXTR,
yields back to computation,
when the I/O finishes, advances NEXTR and decrements n.

For card input, each card image occupies 16 MIX words (5 bytes/word = 80 columns); column 80 is byte 5 of word 16, i.e., byte (5:5) of address NEXTR+15.
We’ll add a single sticky flag EOF (=0 initially). CONTROL will:

After each input completes (just before advancing NEXTR), inspect the last byte of the just-read card ((NEXTR+15)(5:5)).
If it equals the MIX character code for ‘.’, set EOF ← 1.
At the top of the loop, treat EOF exactly like “no red buffers”: if EOF=1, do not start more IN operations ever again.

This leaves already-anticipated (green) cards, including the sentinel card itself, available for the COMPUTE coroutine to consume; it simply prevents any further anticipation past the end. (Knuth explicitly leaves such end-handling details to the reader; this is precisely the point of Ex. 12.)
Algorithm B′ (CONTROL) — deltas only
Relative to Algorithm B, change B2 and insert a test between B4 and B5:

B2′. If n = 0 or EOF = 1, go to B1 (keep computing; do not initiate I/O).
B4½. [Test end card.] If byte (5:5) of word at NEXTR+15 equals ‘.’, set EOF ← 1.

Everything else (B3, B5, B6) is unchanged.
Program B′ (MIX) — minimal patch
Below is a small, self-contained patch to Knuth’s Program B. It preserves the original register discipline (CONTROL already uses only rI6 ≡ n and rI5 ≡ NEXTR), so we save/restore A around our tests to avoid surprising COMPUTE.
New data (somewhere in CONTROL’s static area):
EOF   CON 0          * sticky “seen end card” flag (0/1)
SAVEA CON 0          * temp to save A
DOT   CON <code-for-'.'>   * MIX code of '.' (any correct constant is fine)
Patched code (comments show where lines go in Knuth’s listing):
Program B (CONTROL). rI6 ≡ n, rI5 ≡ NEXTR.

CONT1  JMP  COMPUTE           * B1. Compute

1H      J6Z  *-1              * B2. wait while n=0
        STA  SAVEA            * save A before we look at EOF
        LDA  EOF
        JAZ  2F               * if EOF==0, OK to start I/O
        LDA  SAVEA            * else (EOF!=0): restore A and just yield
        JMP  COMPUTE          *   behave like n=0 forever (no more IN)

2H      IN   0,5(U)           * B3. Initiate I/O
        LDA  SAVEA            * restore A before yielding
        JMP  COMPUTE          * B4. Compute until I/O completes

* ---- we return here when the read just finished on buffer NEXTR ----
* B4½. Test last column (byte 5 of word 16 in this card image).
        STA  SAVEA            * save A again
        LDA  15,5(5:5)        * A ← column 80 from buffer at NEXTR
        CMPA DOT
        JNE  3F               * if not '.', skip setting EOF
        ENTA 1
        STA  EOF              * EOF ← 1
3H      LDA  SAVEA            * restore A

        LD5  -1,5             * B5. Advance NEXTR
        DEC6 1                * B6. Decrease n
        JMP  1B
Notes:

15,5(5:5) addresses “word 16 indexed by rI5, field 5:5”, i.e., byte 5 of the card’s last word.
Use any correct assembler literal for DOT (the MIX code for ‘.’). If you like, assemble ALF ..... into a word and extract the rightmost byte once; a plain numeric constant also suffices.

Why this is correct

If the card just read is not the end card, CONTROL behaves identically to the book: advances NEXTR, decrements n, and continues.
If it is the end card, EOF is set. From then on, the modified B2 treats EOF as a permanent “don’t start I/O” condition. That freezes anticipation beyond the sentinel while leaving already-green buffers for COMPUTE to finish processing.
The sentinel card itself is available to COMPUTE (it turned the buffer from red→green when the read completed), so the main program can recognize “end of input” in its normal logic after assigning that buffer.

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
13 [20]

In the multi-buffer algorithms, n = current number of red buffers (blocks waiting to be output). 
The control coroutine (CONTROL) performs I/O and, after an output completes, does B5 (advance NEXTR) and B6 (decrease n). 
The machine instruction JRED CONTROL(U) in COMPUTE is how CONTROL is re-entered when the device becomes ready; the same framework is used for output as for input, differing only in initial conditions. 

When computation is finished, there may still be red buffers (so n > 0) and/or an output currently in progress. You must:

keep giving CONTROL a chance to run whenever the device becomes ready (so it can execute B5–B6 and drain n), and
only return once no red buffers remain (which also implies the device is idle, since n is decremented after an output completes).

A spin on n without JRED CONTROL(U) would deadlock, because CONTROL wouldn’t get to run.
Minimal MIX trailer to append at end of COMPUTE (output case)
* rI6 ≡ n   (number of red buffers)
* U   ≡ output device number used by CONTROL

FLUSH  JRED CONTROL(U)     * if the device has just become ready,
                           * jump into CONTROL to do B5/B6
       J6NZ FLUSH          * loop until n == 0 (no red buffers remain)
* Optional belt-and-suspenders wait (normally redundant once n==0):
WAIT   JBUS WAIT,U         * if device were somehow still busy, wait
* … now it’s safe to exit/return
Why this works:

JRED CONTROL(U) hands control to CONTROL exactly when the device signals ready; CONTROL then finishes the just-completed output and executes B6: Decrease n. 
The loop continues until n = 0, meaning there are no red buffers left to output. Since n is decremented only after an output finishes, n = 0 implies the device isn’t in the middle of an output. 

That’s all you need to guarantee that every buffered block has been sent before the program terminates.
Two-instruction solution (MIX)
JRED CONTROL(U)
J6NZ *-1
Why this works

JRED CONTROL(U) yields to the CONTROL coroutine whenever the device is ready. CONTROL then performs step B5/B6 (advance NEXTR; decrement n) for one finished output operation, and returns to COMPUTE. 
J6NZ *-1 loops while rI6 = n ≠ 0. Thus we repeatedly give CONTROL chances to complete outstanding writes until no red buffers remain. When n finally becomes zero, the jump fails and the program may safely terminate.

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
14 [20]
Recap of the buffering model

Buffers are arranged in a circle; each buffer is colored green, yellow, or red. • Green = ready to be ASSIGNed (for input: already anticipated; for output: free). • Yellow = currently ASSIGNed (the program may read/write it). • Red = RELEASEd (for input: free area; for output: filled, awaiting device). 
Pointers: NEXTG points to the next green buffer; NEXTR to the next red buffer; CURRENT names “the” yellow buffer when there is one. Two processes run as coroutines: Algorithm A (ASSIGN) makes CURRENT ← NEXTG and advances NEXTG. Algorithm R (RELEASE) increments the count n of red buffers. Algorithm B (Buffer control) starts I/O on NEXTR when n>0, then advances NEXTR and decrements n when I/O completes. 

What if we do ASSIGN, ASSIGN, then RELEASE, RELEASE?
Think in terms of counts. At any instant:
#green + #yellow + #red = N ,
and (by definition of R1) n = #red. Every ASSIGN consumes a green and creates a yellow; every RELEASE turns a yellow into a red (and raises n by 1). If we perform two ASSIGNs in a row while at least two greens exist, we simply end up with two yellow buffers (two outstanding assignments). Later RELEASEs convert those yellows to reds one by one; Algorithm B then services those reds in FIFO pointer order. The control logic keeps working because B only depends on n (reds) and the NEXTR ring, not on how many yellows exist. This can actually be useful when the computation wants to look at two buffers at once (e.g., a boundary-crossing comparison, merging adjacent blocks, etc.), at the cost of tying up an extra buffer that is temporarily unavailable for anticipation/output. 
Important edge case and the safe bound

N = 1 (one buffer). A burst ASSIGN, ASSIGN has no second distinct green to consume. The written Algorithm A’s gate (“wait for n < N”) isn’t sufficient to guarantee a green exists when we allow multiple simultaneous ASSIGNs; with one buffer you can wind up re-assigning the same area or, worse, overlapping program use with I/O. In short, the scheme breaks down for N=1. 
General bound. Let y = (#ASSIGNs − #RELEASEs) = “#yellow at the moment.” Necessarily

0 ≤ y ≤ N .
(If y exceeded N, there would be no green left to assign; if y were negative, we’d be releasing more than we assigned.) Practically, keep the excess of ASSIGNs over RELEASEs nonnegative and ≤ N. 

With N ≥ 2, allowing ASSIGN bursts just produces two (or more) simultaneous yellow buffers; the control coroutine continues to function, and this can be handy when the computation needs concurrent access to several buffers. It does, however, tie up buffer space, leaving fewer greens available for anticipation.
With N = 1, the idea is unsafe/inapplicable; the multiple-buffer algorithm as given relies on there being enough buffers that an extra ASSIGN can legitimately consume another green.

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
15 [22]
Here’s a fast, 3-buffer MIX solution for (“copy 100 blocks from tape unit 0 to tape unit 1, using just three buffers”). The trick is to pipeline I/O so the input and output units stay busy almost all the time, with only a one-time “prime” and a one-time “drain.” This is exactly the “same-circle input+output” idea behind Fig. 26 and the 1.4.4 exercises on pp. 225–228.
MIX program (3-buffer tape-to-tape copy)
* Tape-to-tape copy with triple buffering (fast)
INUNIT  EQU 0              * input tape unit
OUTUNIT EQU 1              * output tape unit

BUF1    ORIG *+100         * three 100-word buffers
BUF2    ORIG *+100
BUF3    ORIG *+100

START   IN   BUF1(INUNIT)  * prime the pipeline: read block #0 -> BUF1
        ENT1 99            * 99 more blocks to output after the prime

LOOP    IN   BUF2(INUNIT)  * queue read of next block -> BUF2
        OUT  BUF1(OUTUNIT) * write the block that was in BUF1
        IN   BUF3(INUNIT)  * queue read -> BUF3
        OUT  BUF2(OUTUNIT) * write the block now in BUF2
        IN   BUF1(INUNIT)  * queue read -> recycle BUF1
        OUT  BUF3(OUTUNIT) * write the block now in BUF3
        DEC1 3
        J1P  LOOP

        JBUS *(INUNIT)     * wait for final input to finish
        OUT  BUF1(OUTUNIT) * write the very last block
        HLT
        END  START
Why this is correct and fast

Pipeline structure.


We “prime” with one input (BUF1).
Each loop body initiates three inputs and three outputs in a fixed rotation (…B2 in, B1 out; B3 in, B2 out; B1 in, B3 out…). This schedules operations so that every OUT uses a buffer whose IN started two I/O steps earlier, i.e., long enough before to have finished by the time the device consumes it (CPU time is negligible compared to device time).
We decrement by 3 because each loop iteration produces 3 completed outputs. Starting from 99, we do 33 iterations (99/3), then drain the final pending input and write the final block.


No buffer hazards. Each buffer is reused only after its previous read and subsequent write have had time to complete; the triple spacing prevents “read-while-write” on the same memory. We never need to poll during the pipeline; a single JBUS *(INUNIT) at the end synchronizes the very last read before the last write.
Minimal idle time ⇒ “as fast as possible.” After the one-time prime, both devices are kept busy continuously (as in the multiple-buffer timelines and exercises in §1.4.4), so throughput is device-limited. Only the leading and trailing edges incur unavoidable bubbles. This matches the “same circle” buffering method discussed around Fig. 26/27 and the surrounding exercises.
Why ENT1 99 and DEC1 3? We must output 100 blocks total. One block is primed into BUF1 before the loop, so 99 outputs remain; each loop does 3 outputs, hence 33 passes. After the loop, the pending final read completes (JBUS) and we perform the 100th output.

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
16 [29]
Here’s a clean formulation of the “green–yellow–red–purple” multiple-buffer algorithm Knuth asks for, written in the same style as Algorithms A/R/B in §1.4.4, but now with three coroutines: one for input, one for output, and one for the computation. I stick to his color semantics and ring-of-buffers pointers.
Model and invariants

We have a single circle of N equal buffers.
Colors (states) and their meaning (same naming as the text and Fig. 26): Green = anticipated input is ready to be ASSIGNed to the program; Yellow = currently assigned to the program (between ASSIGN and RELEASE); Red = RELEASEd by the program; block is ready to be output; Purple = output finished; buffer is free for new input. (Purple is turned to green by input; yellow is turned to red by release; red is turned to purple by output.) 
Pointers on the ring:

NEXTG → first G (next buffer the program may assign)
NEXTR → first R (next buffer the output device will transmit)
NEXTP → first P (next buffer the input device will fill)
CURRENT → Y (present only while the program holds a buffer)

Counters we maintain:

g = # of green buffers
r = # of red buffers
p = # of purple buffers
y ∈ {0,1} (0 if no current assignment; 1 if a buffer is yellow)
Invariant: g + r + p + y = N.

The ring is always segmented in the cyclic order P*  G*  (Y?)  R* with the pointers at the segment heads; this ensures none of NEXTG, NEXTR, NEXTP “passes” another. (This is the three-pointer analogue of the “no passing” story for two pointers in Fig. 24.) 

Initial/terminal conditions

Start: all buffers purple (the natural “free for input” state). So p = N, g = r = y = 0, and set NEXTP = NEXTG = NEXTR to some buffer. Input begins immediately and produces the first G’s for the program. 
End: after the program’s last RELEASE, we must allow output to drain until r = 0 (all finished) before declaring completion (the analogue of the drain step discussed for two-color algorithms). 

Program-side primitives (as in §1.4.4)
We keep ASSIGN and RELEASE as program-visible actions; their algorithms mirror Knuth’s A and R, but they operate on g, r, p, y and NEXTG:
Algorithm A′ (ASSIGN, inside the COMPUTE coroutine)
A1′. Wait for g > 0. (Stall the program until some green buffer exists.) A2′. CURRENT ← NEXTG. (The chosen G becomes the current buffer; color changes G→Y.) A3′. Advance NEXTG clockwise to the next G. A4′. Update counts. g ← g − 1, y ← 1. ■
Algorithm R′ (RELEASE, inside the COMPUTE coroutine)
R1′. Finish the buffer. (Color Y→R.) R2′. Update counts. y ← 0, r ← r + 1. ■
These are exactly the same “shape” as Knuth’s A/R; only the bookkeeping differs (his single counter n was the number of red buffers in the two-party case). 
Device controllers as coroutines
The two devices now have independent controllers (each analogous to Knuth’s Algorithm B, but specialized to one color transition). Each controller runs “in parallel” with COMPUTE via coroutine linkage (COMPUTE periodically yields with JRED … as in the text). 
Coroutine IN (input controller; turns P→G)
I1. Compute (yield to COMPUTE) for a short while; re-enter here later at a device-ready instant. I2. If p = 0, go to I1. (No purple → nothing to input.) I3. Initiate input from the input device into the buffer at NEXTP. I4. Compute (yield to COMPUTE) while the device is busy. I5. On input completion: advance NEXTP clockwise to the next P; update counts: p ← p − 1, g ← g + 1. Go to I2. ■
Coroutine OUT (output controller; turns R→P)
O1. Compute (yield to COMPUTE) for a short while; re-enter here later at a device-ready instant. O2. If r = 0, go to O1. (No red → nothing to output.) O3. Initiate output from the buffer at NEXTR to the output device. O4. Compute (yield to COMPUTE) while the device is busy. O5. On output completion: advance NEXTR clockwise to the next R; update counts: r ← r − 1, p ← p + 1. Go to O2. ■
Coroutine linkage (skeleton)
Exactly as Knuth does, insert “jump-when-ready” instructions inside the COMPUTE code so the device controllers can preempt when their unit becomes ready. One practical arrangement is:

COMPUTE periodically executes JRED INCTRL(U_in) and JRED OUTCTRL(U_out) (once every ~50 instructions as he suggests), letting each controller run steps I1/I4 and O1/O4 at device-ready times; each controller returns to COMPUTE after its brief action. 

Why the pointers never “pass”
At every transition we convert the head element of one contiguous color segment to the head of the next segment in the cyclic order:

IN does P→G at NEXTP and then advances NEXTP to the next P; thus the P-segment shrinks at its head, the G-segment grows at its tail, and no pointer can jump over another.
ASSIGN does G→Y at NEXTG, then advances NEXTG to the next G; the single-element Y sits between G and R, preserving order.
RELEASE does Y→R (no pointer move).
OUT does R→P at NEXTR and advances NEXTR to the next R; the R-segment shrinks at its head and the P-segment grows at its tail.

Hence the circular order P*  G*  (Y?)  R* is invariant and pointers cannot “lap” each other (the three-party generalization of the two-pointer races discussed with Fig. 24). 
Liveness (no deadlock)

If the program needs a buffer and g = 0, then either some output is pending (r > 0, so OUT will run and eventually increase p), or some input is possible (p > 0, so IN will run and increase g).
If an output is needed but r = 0, eventually a RELEASE will occur (COMPUTE makes Y→R) or new input will feed the pipeline to produce future R’s.
If input is needed but p = 0, eventually an output will complete (R→P) or the program advances and releases something.

Provided each coroutine is scheduled fairly (as in Knuth’s set-up with periodic JRED), progress continues. 
Practical MIX skeleton (minimal)
You can mirror Knuth’s tiny MIX control loops, one per device, and keep A′/R′ as subroutines in COMPUTE. For example (pseudocode at the level of his Program B):
; INCTRL (P->G)
INCTRL:   JMP   COMPUTE         ; I1
IWAIT:    J6Z   IWAIT           ; I2: if p==0, spin/yield
         IN    0,U_in           ; I3
         JMP   COMPUTE          ; I4
IDONE:   LDX   NEXTP            ; I5: advance NEXTP
         ; p-- ; g++ (update)
         JMP   IWAIT

; OUTCTRL (R->P)
OUTCTRL:  JMP   COMPUTE         ; O1
OWAIT:    J6Z   OWAIT           ; O2: if r==0, spin/yield
         OUT   0,U_out          ; O3
         JMP   COMPUTE          ; O4
ODONE:   LDX   NEXTR            ; O5: advance NEXTR
         ; r-- ; p++ (update)
         JMP   OWAIT
(Exactly how you hold g, r, p, y in MIX registers or memory, and the device unit numbers, follows the same conventions as his Programs A/R/B; the linkage is the same pattern, only the colors and counts differ.)
MIX setup (symbols and state)
* ======= DEVICE UNITS (choose any legal MIX device #s) =======
UIN     EQU     0               * input device unit
UOUT    EQU     1               * output device unit

* ======= RING & POINTERS =======
N        CON     6              * number of buffers in the ring (example)
RING     ORIG    *+N            * table of N buffer base addresses (filled elsewhere)

NEXTG    CON     0              * pointer index into RING for next Green
NEXTR    CON     0              * pointer index into RING for next Red
NEXTP    CON     0              * pointer index into RING for next Purple
CURRENT  CON     -1             * pointer index for Yellow (=-1 when none)

* ======= COUNTS (G,R,P) and Y-flag =======
GCT      CON     0              * # green
RCT      CON     0              * # red
PCT      CON     0              * # purple
YFLG     CON     0              * 0|1 whether CURRENT is valid (yellow in use)

* ======= PER-BUFFER COLOR (compact: 0=P, 1=G, 2=Y, 3=R) =======
COL0     CON     0              * COL[i] holds color code for buffer i
COL1     CON     0
COL2     CON     0
COL3     CON     0
COL4     CON     0
COL5     CON     0
COLTAB   EQU     COL0           * base for COL[i] = COLTAB+i

* ======= WORK REGS (indices) =======
* We’ll use rI1 as ‘i’, rI2 as N, rI3 as scratch.
INIT:   ENTI2   N               * load N into rI2 at boot
        ENTJ    MAIN            * fall into MAIN
Initialization (as in the text): start with all buffers purple (P), so PCT=N, GCT=RCT=YFLG=0, and set NEXTP = NEXTG = NEXTR to the same position. From then on, the three coroutines keep the circle moving without pointers passing each other. 
Subroutines used by A′/controllers
To keep the listing compact (à la TAOCP), the “advance to next buffer with color = k” is a tiny helper that walks clockwise modulo N.
* ADVANCE(i_ptr, want_color) -> updates memory word at i_ptr to next index having color
* rI2 = N, rI3 scratch. Argument want_color in rA low byte (0..3). i_ptr is an address.
ADVANCE STJ     ADVX
         LDA     0,0(5:5)      * want_color already in rA’s (5:5) on entry (see callers)
         LDX     0,0           * rX free
         LDA     0,1           * load i_ptr? (we’ll pass as literal via callers)
ADVLP    LD1     0,0           * load current index i from [i_ptr] (caller does LDA then ST1)
         INC1    1
         CMP1    N             * wrap
         J1L     *+2
         ENT1    0
         STA     SAVI          * save i
         ADD     =COLTAB=      * compute &COL[i]
         STA     ADRC
         LDA     0,ADRC
         CMPA    WANTC         * matches desired color?
         JE      ADVOK
         JMP     ADVLP
ADVOK    LDA     SAVI
         STA     0,1           * store back to [i_ptr]
ADVX     JMP     0,0

SAVI     CON     0
ADRC     CON     0
WANTC    CON     0
(NB: In a real MIX listing you’d pass the i_ptr and the desired color more directly; I’ve spelled this out minimally to show intent while keeping the style close to Knuth’s short library helpers.)
Program A′ — ASSIGN (in COMPUTE)
Algorithm A′: wait until there’s at least one G; take NEXTG as CURRENT (G→Y), advance NEXTG to next G, update counts. This is inserted where the computation needs a fresh input block. 
* Program A′ — ASSIGN
ASSIGN  STJ     AEX             * A′ entry

A1      LDA     GCT             * A1. Wait for g > 0
        JAZ     AWAIT
        JMP     A2
AWAIT   JRED    INRD,UIN        * let INCTRL run if input just finished
        JRED    OUTRD,UOUT      * let OUTCTRL run if output just finished
        JMP     A1

A2      LDA     NEXTG           * A2. CURRENT ← NEXTG (G->Y)
        STA     CURRENT
        LDA     2               * color code 2 = Yellow
        STA     0,COLTAB(CURRENT)  * COL[CURRENT] ← Y
        STA     YFLG,=1         * y ← 1

A3      LDA     =1=             * A3. g-- ; advance NEXTG to next G
        DEC     GCT,1
        LDA     =1=
        STA     WANTC           * want_color = 1 (Green)
        LDA     NEXTG
        STA     PTRG
        JSR     ADVANCE         * scan to next Green
        LDA     PTRG
        STA     NEXTG
AEX     JMP     0,0

PTRG    CON     0
Program R′ — RELEASE (in COMPUTE)
Algorithm R′: mark the current buffer R (Y→R), clear Y-flag, r++.
* Program R′ — RELEASE
RELEASE STJ     REX
R1      LDA     CURRENT         * R1. Y->R; r++ ; y=0
        LDA     =3=             * color code 3 = Red
        STA     0,COLTAB(CURRENT)
        INC     RCT,1
        STA     YFLG,=0
REX     JMP     0,0
Coroutine INCTRL — input controller (P→G)
Matches the B-style controller in §1.4.4 but specialized to P→G. Two entries: an idle entry that is polled via JRED (I1/I4), and a ready entry (I5) that runs when the unit signals “ready.” 
* INCTRL: turns Purple -> Green
INCTRL  JMP     I1              * I1. Idle entry (reached via JRED return)

I1      LDA     PCT             * I2. If p==0, nothing to start
        JAZ     IRET
        * I3. Start IN to buffer at NEXTP
        LDA     NEXTP
        STA     TMPP
        * compute buffer address RING[NEXTP] into rX:
        LD1     NEXTP
        LDX     RING,1
        IN      0,UIN           * device starts filling buffer rX
        JMP     IRET            * I4. Yield to COMPUTE

INRD    * ready-entry label (target of JRED UIN)
        * I5. Completion: P->G ; advance NEXTP ; counts
        LDA     TMPP
        STA     IDX
        LDA     =1=             * 1 = Green
        STA     0,COLTAB(IDX)
        DEC     PCT,1
        INC     GCT,1
        * advance NEXTP to next Purple
        LDA     =0=
        STA     WANTC           * want_color=0 (Purple)
        LDA     NEXTP
        STA     PTRP
        JSR     ADVANCE
        LDA     PTRP
        STA     NEXTP
IRET    JMP     COMPUTE

TMPP    CON     0
PTRP    CON     0
IDX     CON     0
Coroutine OUTCTRL — output controller (R→P)
Symmetric to input: when r>0 and device idle, start an OUT from NEXTR. On ready, mark P, advance NEXTR, update counts. 
* OUTCTRL: turns Red -> Purple
OUTCTRL JMP     O1              * O1. Idle entry

O1      LDA     RCT             * O2. If r==0, nothing to start
        JAZ     ORET
        * O3. Start OUT from buffer at NEXTR
        LDA     NEXTR
        STA     TMPR
        LD1     NEXTR
        LDX     RING,1
        OUT     0,UOUT
        JMP     ORET            * O4. Yield

OUTRD   * ready-entry label (target of JRED UOUT)
        * O5. Completion: R->P ; advance NEXTR ; counts
        LDA     TMPR
        STA     IDX2
        LDA     =0=             * 0 = Purple
        STA     0,COLTAB(IDX2)
        DEC     RCT,1
        INC     PCT,1
        * advance NEXTR to next Red
        LDA     =3=
        STA     WANTC
        LDA     NEXTR
        STA     PTRR
        JSR     ADVANCE
        LDA     PTRR
        STA     NEXTR
ORET    JMP     COMPUTE

TMPR    CON     0
PTRR    CON     0
IDX2    CON     0
COMPUTE coroutine (skeleton)
A trivial loop that periodically gives time to device controllers via JRED, and uses ASSIGN/RELEASE around “work”. The JRED polling cadence (e.g., “every ~50 instructions”) follows §1.4.4’s guidance. 
MAIN    * ===== Boot: all buffers Purple, set pointers equal =====
        STZ     GCT
        STZ     RCT
        LDA     N
        STA     PCT
        STZ     YFLG
        STZ     NEXTG
        STZ     NEXTR
        STZ     NEXTP
        * set COL[i]=P for all i
        ENT1    0
CLP     LDA     =0=
        STA     0,COLTAB(1)
        INC1    1
        CMP1    N
        J1L     CLP

COMPUTE * ===== Example compute loop =====
        * A′: need an input block
        JSR     ASSIGN
        * (use CURRENT buffer: user’s computation goes here)
        * --- do some work; sprinkle JRED calls periodically ---
        JRED    INRD,UIN
        JRED    OUTRD,UOUT
        JRED    INRD,UIN
        JRED    OUTRD,UOUT
        * done with current block:
        JSR     RELEASE
        * repeat/exit per program logic
        JMP     COMPUTE
        HLT
END     MAIN
Notes on correctness and style

The three pointers NEXTG, NEXTR, NEXTP and the single CURRENT (yellow) are kept in the cyclic order P* G* (Y?) R*, so no pointer can ever “pass” another; each controller converts the head of its segment to the next color and advances its own pointer only within its segment, exactly mirroring the invariant under Fig. 26. 
ASSIGN/RELEASE are drop-in analogs of Programs A/R (here A′/R′) from the section’s “multiple buffers” discussion; the only differences are the extra counts and the scan to the next green. 
JRED INRD,UIN and JRED OUTRD,UOUT provide the coroutine linkage (B-style control) so that device “ready” events run the I/O completion steps (I5/O5) promptly while the computation proceeds.

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
17 [40]
Data structures and invariants
Buffers: Each buffer b has fields (state, dev), where state ∈ {FREE, GREEN, YELLOW, RED} and dev is a device id.
Global pool/list:

FREE: list of free buffers.
For each input device d: queue G[d] of GREEN buffers (ready for compute), and counter g[d] = |G[d]|.
For each output device o: queue R[o] of RED buffers (ready for device), and counter r[o] = |R[o]|.
Device status idle(d) / idle(o) and an “in-flight” counter for I/O requests in progress.

Anti-flood control (the key to the exercise):

Per-input token bucket. For each input device d, maintain tok[d] with bounds 0 ≤ tok[d] ≤ cap[d] (small cap, e.g., 2). – Consume one token when scheduling an IN for d. – Produce one token when computation takes a GREEN buffer of d (i.e., on ASSIGN_IN(d)), reflecting actual consumption. This ensures anticipation never runs more than cap[d] blocks ahead of use.
Free-buffer reserve. Maintain reserve_free ≥ 1 (e.g., reserve_free = #output_devices, one per output). Do not start new input if |FREE| ≤ reserve_free, unless compute is currently blocked waiting for that specific input device d (the text’s exception). 
Priority: Output drains (RED→device) always take precedence over more input anticipation.

These three rules are sufficient to keep pooled buffers from stalling due to over-prefetch.
Algorithms (pooled variants of A/R/B)
Algorithm A_pool — ASSIGN (normal state)
There are two entry points:
A_pool.IN(d): hand compute the next input block from device d. A1. If G[d] nonempty: pop head b from G[d], set state(b)←YELLOW, return b. Also tok[d] ← min(tok[d]+1, cap[d]) (credit for consumption). A2. Otherwise: mark need[d]←true, invoke B\_pool (control), and wait until G[d] becomes nonempty, then go to A1.
A_pool.OUT(o): obtain a workspace buffer destined for output device o. A3. If FREE nonempty: take b from FREE, set state(b)←YELLOW, dev(b)←o, return b. A4. Otherwise: invoke B\_pool and wait until FREE nonempty, then A3.
Algorithm R_pool — RELEASE (normal state)
R1. If b was obtained by ASSIGN_IN, computation has consumed it: set state(b)←FREE, push to FREE. R2. If b was obtained by ASSIGN_OUT(o), computation has produced it: set state(b)←RED, enqueue to R[o]. R3. Optionally call B\_pool (helps prompt draining).
Algorithm B_pool — CONTROL (runs on I/O completion or when A/R asks)
B1. Drain outputs first (priority). For each idle output device o with R[o] nonempty:  pop b from R[o], start OUT(o, b) (device becomes busy).
B2. Start essential inputs (to unblock compute). For each input device d with need[d]==true and idle(d)==true:  If FREE nonempty (even if |FREE| ≤ reserve_free), take b from FREE, set state(b)←(in-flight IN for d), tok[d]−− (if tok[d]==0, set to 0; we allow this unblocking read even if tokens are 0), start IN(d, b), clear need[d]. (Exactly the exception suggested in the text: we may use the final buffer for input only if compute is stalled for that input.) 
B3. Conservative anticipation for inputs. For each idle input device d, if tok[d] > 0 and |FREE| > reserve_free:  take b from FREE, set (in-flight IN for d), tok[d]−−, start IN(d, b).
B4. On I/O completion (interrupt or poll): – If an OUT(o, b) completes: set state(b)←FREE, push FREE. – If an IN(d, b) completes: set state(b)←GREEN, enqueue to G[d]. Then re-enter B1 (outputs may now drain again).
This is the entire pooled-buffer controller. It’s short because the token bucket + reserve encode the needed policy compactly.
Why this prevents slowdown

No green-pool flood. Tokens ensure each input device anticipates at most cap[d] blocks beyond what compute actually consumes; you can’t fill the pool with “anticipated input” because consumption is the only way to mint new tokens.
Output always proceeds. Output drains have strict priority (B1) and at least reserve_free buffers are kept out of anticipatory reads, so produced blocks can turn RED and get scheduled without starvation.
Progress when blocked. If compute stalls waiting for d, B2 violates both the token and reserve limits only for that d, matching Knuth’s exception: you may use the last buffer to get unstuck for the specific input that blocks compute. 

Tuning notes

Typical choices: reserve_free = #output_devices and cap[d] ∈ {1,2}. With fast outputs or many outputs, increase reserve_free. With bursty inputs, cap[d]=2 smooths latency while still preventing flood.
You may add a small hysteresis (high/low watermark) around reserve_free to avoid thrashing (start anticipation only when |FREE| ≥ reserve_free+H, stop when |FREE| ≤ reserve_free).


Overview: how the algorithms map to MIX


A_pool.IN(d) takes a ready (GREEN) input buffer for device d or blocks (setting NEED[d]) and calls B_pool.
A_pool.OUT(o) takes a FREE buffer for output device o or blocks and calls B_pool.
R_pool.RELEASE returns a consumed input buffer to FREE or enqueues a produced output buffer to R[o] (RED).
B_pool runs the scheduler: drain outputs first, then do essential inputs that unblock compute, then conservative anticipatory inputs (respecting tokens and the free-buffer reserve).
CHECK_IO polls devices with JBUS to detect completions and moves finished buffers to FREE (OUT complete) or G[d] (IN complete).


Symbols, constants, and layout

        * ---------- PARAMETERS ----------
M       EQU     3               * number of input devices
N       EQU     2               * number of output devices
B       EQU     16              * number of buffers in pool (>= M + N + working set)
BUFSZ   EQU     100             * words per buffer (tune per device)
RESV    EQU     N               * reserve_free: keep at least N buffers free

        * Device numbers (example; change to your MIX)
D1      EQU     16              * paper tape reader, say
D2      EQU     17              * disk input unit
D3      EQU     18
O1      EQU     19              * line printer, say
O2      EQU     20

        * ---------- BUFFER DESCRIPTORS ----------
        * Each buffer descriptor (BD[i]) holds: NEXT, STATE, DEV, ADDR
        * STATE: 0=FREE, 1=GREEN, 2=YELLOW, 3=RED, 4=INFLIGHT_IN, 5=INFLIGHT_OUT
        * DEV:   device id (for GREEN/RED/INFLIGHT states)
        * ADDR:  start address of the data area for this buffer
STATE_FREE  EQU 0
STATE_GREEN EQU 1
STATE_YEL   EQU 2
STATE_RED   EQU 3
STATE_INFLI EQU 4
STATE_INFLO EQU 5

NIL     EQU     0

        ORIG    1000            * (choose a safe origin)

FREEHD  CON     NIL             * head pointer to FREE list

        * G queues (one per input d) and R queues (one per output o)
GHD1    CON     NIL
GHD2    CON     NIL
GHD3    CON     NIL
RHO1    CON     NIL
RHO2    CON     NIL

        * "Need" flags: compute blocked waiting for input from d?
NEED1   CON     0
NEED2   CON     0
NEED3   CON     0

        * Token buckets: TOK[d] ∈ [0..CAP[d]]
TOK1    CON     1               * initialize to small positive values
TOK2    CON     1
TOK3    CON     1
CAP1    CON     2               * caps can be 1 or 2 (prefetch depth)
CAP2    CON     2
CAP3    CON     2

        * For each device, track in-flight buffer (0 if none)
INFLI1  CON     0               * input d1
INFLI2  CON     0
INFLI3  CON     0
INFLO1  CON     0               * output o1
INFLO2  CON     0

        * Busy flags are implicit via JBUS; you can also mirror them if you like
        * (not required).

        * Buffer descriptor table
BDTAB   EQU     *               * BD[1..B], 3 words each + ADDR
        * We also allocate data areas per buffer behind BD table.
        * Layout helper: BD[i].NEXT at BDNXT+(i-1), etc.
BDNXT   CON     NIL             * BD[1].NEXT
BDST1   CON     STATE_FREE      * BD[1].STATE
BDDEV1  CON     0               * BD[1].DEV
BDADR1  CON     0               * BD[1].ADDR
        * ... replicate for BD[2..B] (macro expands this in a real assembly)
        * For brevity, assume you generate these with a macro.
        * Also compute BDADR[i] to point to a BUFSZ block per buffer.

        * Free-list bootstrapping (done once at init):
        * Link all BD[i] in a singly linked list: FREEHD -> 1 -> 2 -> ... -> B -> NIL

List primitives (ENQ/DEQ on singly linked heads)

We use head insertion for O(1) enqueue; dequeue also from head (so queues act as stacks — fine for buffers). If you want FIFO queues, maintain both head and tail; the scaffold keeps it simple.
* ENQ b into list at (HEADPTR)
*   IN: A = address of HEADPTR (the word containing head pointer)
*       X = buffer index b (1..B)
ENQ     STJ     ENQ_RET
        LDA     0,1             * A := HEADPTR
        LD1     0,1             * X1 := [HEADPTR] (current head)
        STA     TMPHEAD
        ENT2    0               * X2 := 0
        * Set BD[b].NEXT = old head
        LDA     =BDNXT-1=,X     * address of BD[b].NEXT; (use proper base math in real code)
        LDX     TMPHEAD
        STA     0,X             * BD[b].NEXT = old head
        * Update head to b
        LDX     X               * X holds b already
        STA     0,1             * [HEADPTR] = b
        JMP     ENQ_RET
ENQ_RET NOP

* DEQ from list at (HEADPTR), returns X = b or X = 0 if empty
DEQ     STJ     DEQ_RET
        LDA     0,1             * head
        JAZ     DEQ_EMPTY
        LDX     0,1             * X := head b
        * head := BD[b].NEXT
        LDA     =BDNXT-1=,X
        LDA     0,X
        STA     0,1
        JMP     DEQ_DONE
DEQ_EMPTY
        ENTX    0               * X := 0
DEQ_DONE
        JMP     DEQ_RET
DEQ_RET NOP

TMPHEAD CON     0
(Notes: In real MIXAL you’ll implement the “address arithmetic” with proper EQU/macros so BDNXT-1 + b hits BD[b].NEXT. I’m showing intent; translate to your macro system.)

Helpers to read/write BD fields

For clarity, define tiny helpers/macros (or inline):

SET_STATE(b, s)
SET_DEV(b, d)
SET_NEXT(b, nx)
GET_ADR(b) -> A := BDADR[b]

I’ll inline them below to keep the listing compact.

Core routines

5.1 A_pool.IN(d)
* A_IN(d): try G[d], else set NEED[d]=1, call B_pool, wait until G[d] nonempty
* Example for d = D1 (duplicate for D2, D3 or generate with macro)

A_IN1   STJ     AIN1_RET

        * Try dequeue from GHD1
        LDA     =GHD1=
        ENTA    =GHD1=
        ENTX    0               * X acts as result
        JMP     AIN1_DEQ
AIN1_DEQ
        * call DEQ with A=addr(GHD1)
        * (Assume A already = addr(GHD1))
        JSR     DEQ
        JXZ     AIN1_BLOCK      * X=0 means empty -> block

        * Got buffer b in X: mark YELLOW and credit token
        * STATE[b] := YELLOW
        LDA     =BDST1-1=,X
        ENTA    STATE_YEL
        STA     0,X
        * TOK1 := min(TOK1+1, CAP1)
        LDA     TOK1
        ADD     ONE
        CMPA    CAP1
        JGE     AIN1_TOKCAP
        STA     TOK1
        JMP     AIN1_DONE
AIN1_TOKCAP
        STA     TOK1            * (A ≥ CAP → store A anyway; CAP1 already bounds)
AIN1_DONE
        JMP     AIN1_RET

AIN1_BLOCK
        * Signal need and invoke scheduler
        STA     NEED1           * store nonzero to NEED1
        JSR     B_POOL

        * Busy-wait until GHD1 nonempty (you may also yield via CHECK_IO)
AIN1_WAIT
        LDA     GHD1
        JAZ     AIN1_SPIN
        JMP     A_IN1           * try again
AIN1_SPIN
        JSR     CHECK_IO        * poll devices and reschedule
        JMP     AIN1_WAIT

AIN1_RET NOP
ONE     CON     1
5.2 A_pool.OUT(o)
* A_OUT(o): take FREE or block; example for o = O1
A_OUT1  STJ     AOUT1_RET
AOUT1_TRY
        LDA     =FREEHD=
        JSR     DEQ
        JXZ     AOUT1_BLOCK
        * X=b; STATE[b] := YELLOW; DEV[b] := O1
        LDA     =BDST1-1=,X
        ENTA    STATE_YEL
        STA     0,X
        LDA     =BDDEV1-1=,X
        ENTA    O1
        STA     0,X
        JMP     AOUT1_RET
AOUT1_BLOCK
        JSR     B_POOL
AOUT1_WAIT
        LDA     FREEHD
        JAZ     AOUT1_SPIN
        JMP     A_OUT1
AOUT1_SPIN
        JSR     CHECK_IO
        JMP     AOUT1_WAIT
AOUT1_RET NOP
5.3 R_pool.RELEASE
* RELEASE for an input buffer (consumed): return to FREE
REL_IN  STJ     RELI_RET
        * IN: X = b
        * STATE[b] := FREE; ENQ b -> FREEHD
        LDA     =BDST1-1=,X
        ENTA    STATE_FREE
        STA     0,X
        ENTA    =FREEHD=
        JSR     ENQ
        JMP     RELI_RET
RELI_RET NOP

* RELEASE for an output buffer (produced): enqueue RED to R[o]
* Example for o=O1, X=b
REL_OUT1 STJ    RELO1_RET
        LDA     =BDST1-1=,X
        ENTA    STATE_RED
        STA     0,X
        LDA     =BDDEV1-1=,X
        ENTA    O1
        STA     0,X
        ENTA    =RHO1=
        JSR     ENQ
        * hint scheduler
        JSR     B_POOL
        JMP     RELO1_RET
RELO1_RET NOP

Scheduler (B_pool)

Drain outputs → essential inputs (to unblock compute) → anticipatory inputs (tokens + reserve).
B_POOL  STJ     BRET

        * ----- B1: drain outputs first -----
        * if O1 idle and RHO1 nonempty and no inflight out: start OUT(O1)
B1_O1   LDA     RHO1
        JAZ     B1_O2           * nothing ready
        LDA     INFLO1
        JAZ     B1_O1_TRY       * none inflight
        JMP     B1_O2           * already busy for O1
B1_O1_TRY
        ENTA    =RHO1=
        JSR     DEQ             * X=b
        JXZ     B1_O2
        * mark inflight and start OUT
        STA     INFLO1          * INFLO1 := b
        LDA     =BDST1-1=,X
        ENTA    STATE_INFLO
        STA     0,X
        * OUT mem, O1
        LDA     =BDADR1-1=,X
        OUT     0(O1)           * (syntax varies by assembler; concept: OUT at BDADR[b] to O1)
B1_O2
        * repeat for O2 similarly...
        * (macro in real code)
        
        * ----- B2: essential inputs (unblock compute) -----
        * For each d with NEED[d]!=0 and INFLI[d]==0 and FREE available (even if count <= RESV)
B2_D1   LDA     NEED1
        JAZ     B2_D2
        LDA     INFLI1
        JNZ     B2_D2
        * Try FREE
        LDA     FREEHD
        JAZ     B2_D2           * truly no free buffers; cannot help now
        ENTA    =FREEHD=
        JSR     DEQ             * X=b
        JXZ     B2_D2
        * Start IN on D1 without consuming token (exception)
        STA     INFLI1
        LDA     =BDDEV1-1=,X
        ENTA    D1
        STA     0,X
        LDA     =BDST1-1=,X
        ENTA    STATE_INFLI
        STA     0,X
        * IN mem, D1
        LDA     =BDADR1-1=,X
        IN      0(D1)
        * clear NEED1
        ENTA    0
        STA     NEED1
B2_D2
        * Repeat similarly for D2, D3 ...

        * ----- B3: conservative anticipation -----
        * Only if FREE count > RESV and TOK[d] > 0 and no inflight
        * (Here we approximate "FREE count" with head != NIL and a spillover test,
        *  or keep a FREE_COUNT counter updated by ENQ/DEQ in real code.)

        * We'll implement a minimal guard: require FREEHD != NIL and also
        * require a second FREE to exist (head.next != NIL) when RESV=1, etc.
        * For clarity, use FREE_COUNT (recommended).

        * Example for D1:
B3_D1   LDA     INFLI1
        JNZ     B3_D2
        LDA     TOK1
        JAZ     B3_D2
        LDA     FREE_COUNT
        CMPA    RESV
        JLE     B3_D2
        * OK: schedule anticipatory IN for D1; consume one token
        ENTA    =FREEHD=
        JSR     DEQ             * X=b (must exist by guard)
        JXZ     B3_D2
        STA     INFLI1
        LDA     =BDDEV1-1=,X
        ENTA    D1
        STA     0,X
        LDA     =BDST1-1=,X
        ENTA    STATE_INFLI
        STA     0,X
        * IN mem, D1
        LDA     =BDADR1-1=,X
        IN      0(D1)
        * TOK1 := TOK1 - 1
        LDA     TOK1
        SUB     ONE
        STA     TOK1
B3_D2
        * Repeat for D2, D3 ...

        JMP     BRET
BRET    NOP

I/O completion polling (CHECK_IO)

IN/OUT start asynchronously; we detect completion with JBUS (“jump if busy”). When JBUS falls through, the device finished. We then move the buffer to the right queue and clear INFLI?/INFLO?.
CHECK_IO STJ    CIRET

        * ----- outputs: when OUT completes, return buffer to FREE -----
C_O1    JBUS    C_O1_BUSY,O1    * jump if O1 still busy
        * Completed
        LDA     INFLO1
        JAZ     C_O2            * no inflight -> nothing to do
        ENTX    A               * X=b
        ENTA    0
        STA     INFLO1
        * STATE[b] := FREE; ENQ FREE
        LDA     =BDST1-1=,X
        ENTA    STATE_FREE
        STA     0,X
        ENTA    =FREEHD=
        JSR     ENQ
        * FREE_COUNT++
        LDA     FREE_COUNT
        ADD     ONE
        STA     FREE_COUNT
        JMP     C_O2
C_O1_BUSY
        NOP
C_O2    * repeat for O2 ...

        * ----- inputs: when IN completes, put buffer GREEN on G[d] -----
C_I1    JBUS    C_I1_BUSY,D1
        LDA     INFLI1
        JAZ     C_I2
        ENTX    A               * X=b
        ENTA    0
        STA     INFLI1
        * STATE[b] := GREEN; DEV[b]:=D1; ENQ to GHD1
        LDA     =BDST1-1=,X
        ENTA    STATE_GREEN
        STA     0,X
        LDA     =BDDEV1-1=,X
        ENTA    D1
        STA     0,X
        ENTA    =GHD1=
        JSR     ENQ
        JMP     C_I2
C_I1_BUSY
        NOP
C_I2    * repeat for D2, D3 ...

        * Optional: immediately run B_POOL to kick off drain/anticipation
        JSR     B_POOL
        JMP     CIRET
CIRET   NOP

FREE_COUNT maintenance (recommended)

Counting FREE precisely makes the reserve rule simple and efficient.
FREE_COUNT  CON  B           * initialized to B at startup

* On taking from FREE (DEQ success): FREE_COUNT--
* On returning to FREE (ENQ):        FREE_COUNT++

* You can integrate these bumps directly around the DEQ/ENQ calls that touch FREE.

Putting it together (call pattern)


The compute routine calls A_IN? to obtain a YELLOW buffer (filled input) or A_OUT? to obtain a YELLOW buffer for output space. It then does work and calls REL_IN or REL_OUT? accordingly.
A_* calls may block, which triggers B_POOL and CHECK_IO until the requested list becomes nonempty.
B_POOL is also called:

right after REL_OUT? (to drain RED → device),
from CHECK_IO when completions happen,
and opportunistically anywhere you’d like to make the system responsive.



Policy knobs (the “anti-flood” part)


Token buckets: TOK[d] minted only when compute actually consumes a GREEN buffer (in A_IN?, after successful dequeue). One token is spent when scheduling an anticipatory IN in B3. Essential reads in B2 don’t spend tokens (that’s the “exception when blocked”). Keep CAP[d] small (1–2).
Reserve: RESV = #outputs is a good default; increase if outputs can bunch up or are slow. The FREE_COUNT > RESV guard must hold before B3 starts more anticipation.
Output priority: Encoded by B1 running before B2/B3. This prevents the pool from filling with GREEN while outputs starve.


Notes for a real MIX assembler/emulator


The snippets use “address arithmetic” placeholders like =BDST1-1=,X. In a real MIXAL, define macros BDNXT(i), BDST(i), BDDEV(i), BDADR(i) that compute addresses from i (e.g., via EQU tables or by laying out BD fields in contiguous arrays).
The IN 0(D) / OUT 0(D) lines indicate “start I/O at address in A to device D”. Adapt to your assembler’s exact operand format; many MIX tools accept IN A,D.
If your emulator blocks on IN/OUT (non-asynchronous), simulate async by splitting start/complete as shown (or just keep the same control logic; JBUS tests become no-ops).

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
18 [30]
Below is a clean, interrupt-driven rewrite of Knuth’s multiple-buffer scheme (Algorithms/Programs A, R, B in §1.4.4) for MIX, using the proposed control-state “interrupt” extension described right after Exercise 18. I first restate the interrupt model very briefly, then give the modified algorithms and a compact MIX sketch, and finally explain why it works and how it maps to the original buffering logic.
Interrupt model (per the exercise)

MIX gains control memory locations −3999..−0001 and two internal states: normal and control. On an interrupt, registers rA, rI1..rI6, rX, and (rJ, OV, CI, “next-inst”) are saved into −0009..−0001, and MIX enters control state at an address determined by the interrupt type. There is also a “clock” at −0010 that ticks each 1000u and, on zero, interrupts to −0011. The instruction INT: in normal state, forces an interrupt to −0012; in control state, it restores registers from −0009..−0001 and returns to normal state. I/O completion interrupts vector to −(0020+unit). (All as specified in the exercise statement.) 

Variables and notation
We keep Knuth’s §1.4.4 notation:

N = total buffers in the circle.
n = number of green buffers (assignable).
NEXTG, NEXTR, CURRENT = ring pointers as in the text.
New: t = 0 if the device is idle, 1 if a transfer is in progress.
Device unit number is U.

(Initial conditions are as in §1.4.4: for input, start with all buffers green; for output, with all buffers red; and NEXTR = NEXTG at start. See the section text around Algorithms A/R/B.) 
The modified algorithms
These are exactly the changes needed to eliminate any polling/JRED and run purely interrupt-driven. The outline matches Knuth’s official answer (“Partial solution”) but with full context and linkage.
A′ — ASSIGN (normal state, called by the compute routine)
Unchanged from Algorithm A in the text:

A1. Wait for n < N.
A2. CURRENT ← NEXTG.
A3. Advance NEXTG (clockwise).

(Identical to §1.4.4A.) 
R′ — RELEASE (normal state, inlined where the compute routine is done with the current buffer)

R1′. Increase n by 1.
R2′. If t = 0 (device idle), force an interrupt with INT so that control jumps immediately to the control routine’s step B3′ (see below).

This is exactly the little “nudge” we need so that when the CPU frees a buffer and the device happens to be idle, the control routine can immediately kick off the next I/O without delay. 
B′ — BUFFER CONTROL (runs in control state; it processes interrupts)
Think of B′ as the interrupt handler framework for buffering; it is entered:

on a software interrupt from R2′ (vector −0012),
or on an I/O completion interrupt (vector −(0020+U)).

It uses t to remember whether the device is currently busy.

B1′. Restart the main program (execute INT) if there’s nothing useful to do right now.
B2′. If n = 0, set t ← 0 and go to B1′ (no buffers to service, stay idle and let the CPU compute).
B3′. Set t ← 1 and initiate I/O on unit U using the buffer pointed to by NEXTR (e.g., IN for input flow, OUT for output flow).
B4′. Restart the main program (INT). An I/O completion will later interrupt and re-enter control state.
B5′. On the I/O completion interrupt, advance NEXTR to the next buffer in the ring.
B6′. Decrease n by 1 (we’ve consumed a green buffer for input, or emptied a red buffer for output), and go to B2′.

This is the full interrupt-driven analogue of the original B; no busy-waiting or JRED appears anywhere. 
Minimal MIX skeleton (how to wire it)
Below is a compact sketch to show where the control-state entry points jump. It’s deliberately schematic (addresses/labels are illustrative), but it shows the exact linkage you can adopt in your own code.
* --------------------------
* Data (normal state)
N       CON   ...          * total buffers
n       CON   0            * current green count
t       CON   0            * 0 = idle, 1 = busy
NEXTG   CON   ...          * ring pointers per §1.4.4
NEXTR   CON   ...
CURRENT CON   ...

* --------------------------
* R′ (normal state, inline macro)
Rprime: INC   n,1          * R1′
        LDA   t
        JAZ   ForceINT     * if t = 0 -> nudge the control routine
        JMP   *+1
ForceINT:
        INT                * software interrupt -> control entry at −0012

* A′ (normal state): identical to Algorithm A in text (A1–A3)
* ... your A code from §1.4.4 ...

* --------------------------
* CONTROL STATE VECTORS
* (hardware jumps here on different interrupts)

ORG    -12                 * −0012: software INT from R2′
INT_SW: JMP   B3prime

ORG    -(20+U)             * I/O completion for device U
INT_IO: JMP   B5prime

* (Clock −0011 not needed here, but could JMP B1′ if you like.)

* --------------------------
* B′ control routine (control state)
B1prime:
        INT                * Return to normal state immediately when idle

B2prime:
        LDA   n
        JAZ   Idle         * if n = 0: idle the device
        JMP   B3prime

Idle:   STA   t            * store 0 (accumulator assumed 0 from JAZ path)
        JMP   B1prime

B3prime:
        ENTA  1
        STA   t            * t ← 1 (device busy)
        * Initiate I/O on buffer at NEXTR:
        *   For input:  IN  BUF(NEXTR)(U)
        *   For output: OUT BUF(NEXTR)(U)
        * (Addressing of BUF(NEXTR) per your ring layout.)
        INT                * B4′: restart main program; completion will re-enter at INT_IO

B5prime:
        * advance NEXTR one buffer clockwise
        * NEXTR ← next(NEXTR)
        DEC   n,1          * B6′: n ← n − 1
        JMP   B2prime
(You’ll splice in the actual IN/OUT origin fields to address the buffer at NEXTR, exactly as you did in your non-interrupt version.)
Why this is correct

No polling: The original B used a “wait until device ready” flavor (conceptually via JBUS/JRED). Here we never poll. The control logic either restarts the main program (B1′/B4′) or is re-entered only when something interesting happens (a release when idle, or I/O completion). This directly matches the exercise’s intent. 
Immediate kick-off when possible: If the CPU frees a buffer while the device is idle (t=0), R2′ forces a software interrupt, which vectors straight to B3′ and starts I/O at once. This eliminates latent delays that might otherwise occur before the next timer/clock tick. 
Invariant preservation: The same invariants from §1.4.4 hold:

n counts available (green) buffers; is incremented only by RELEASE (R1′) and decremented only when a transfer is actually begun (B6′).
t tracks device occupancy; set in B3′ and implicitly cleared by the path B2′ takes when n=0 (and by the fact that once completion fires, the next cycle will either start another I/O or idle again).
NEXTR advances only upon I/O completion (B5′), exactly mirroring the original control logic. 

State transitions match hardware: INT in control state restores registers and resumes normal execution (B1′, B4′), and I/O completion vectors to −(0020+U) (B5′ path). That’s precisely how the exercise defines INT and the I/O interrupts. 

Mapping to Knuth’s official answer
Knuth’s answer gives the same three changes concisely:

“In the algorithms below, t is 0 when idle, 1 when active.”
A′ unchanged.
R′: increment n; if t=0 force an interrupt to B3′.
B′: (i) restart; (ii) if n=0, set t ← 0 and restart; (iii) set t ← 1 and initiate I/O; (iv) restart; (v) on I/O complete, advance NEXTR; (vi) n ← n − 1; loop. 

Notes and practical tips

If you use the “green–yellow–red–purple” combined input/output circle (Fig. 26) elsewhere in §1.4.4, the same interrupt framework applies; you just choose IN vs. OUT in B3′ based on which half (input or output) is being serviced at that moment. 
You do not need the clock interrupt (−0011) for correctness; it can be ignored or used for other housekeeping. The key events are R2′’s software INT and device completion interrupts. 

If you’d like, I can drop this into your specific MIX buffer layout (addresses for BUF1..BUFN, ring macros for NEXTR/NEXTG, etc.) and produce a runnable MIXAL file.

title: 1.4.4 Input and Output
text:
19 [M28]
We process \(n\ge 2\) consecutive blocks from a rotating device. Block \(k\) can begin input only when its start passes the head, namely at times
\[
t_k \equiv (k-1)L \pmod P,\qquad t_1=0,
\]
and it becomes available \(T\) time units later, i.e. it may be assigned at
\[
u_k\;\ge\; t_k+T,
\]
then takes \(C\) time units to compute, so it is released at
\[
v_k = u_k + C .
\]
Processing is sequential so \(u_k \ge v_{k-1}\) for \(k>1\). With only \(N\) buffers we also need \(t_k \ge v_{k-N}\) for \(k>N\).
Question: what is the least \(N\) that lets us achieve the theoretical optimum finishing time
\[
v_n^{\min}=T+C+(n-1)\max(L,C)?
\]
Give a general rule and then work it out for \(L=1,\;P=100,\;T=0.5,\;n=100\) with several \(C\)’s.
Lower bound and target schedule
The lower bound arises because each additional block must wait at least \(L\) time units (until its start again appears) and at least \(C\) time units (to compute). Thus the best possible cadence is by \(\max(L,C)\), with the pipeline start-up cost \(T+C\). We therefore try to hit the bound by choosing
\[
v_k=\begin{cases}
T+C, & k=1,\\[2pt]
v_{k-1}+C,& C\ge L\quad\text{(compute-bound)},\\[2pt]
v_{k-1}+L,& C\le L\quad\text{(I/O-bound)}.
\end{cases}
\]
Case A — I/O-bound (\(C\le L\))
Here we can target \(u_k=t_k+T=(k-1)L+T\) (use the earliest revolution), giving
\[
v_k=T+C+(k-1)L .
\]
To be feasible with \(N\) buffers we must not start input before a buffer frees:
\[
t_k\;=\;(k-1)L\;\ge\;v_{k-N}=T+C+(k-N-1)L
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
(N-1)L\;\ge\;T+C.
\]
Hence the minimum number of buffers is
\[
\boxed{\,N_{\min}=\left\lceil 1+\frac{T+C}{L}\right\rceil\,,\qquad C\le L.}
\]
Case B — Compute-bound (\(C\ge L\))
Now we target \(u_k=v_{k-1}\) so \(v_k=T+kC\). Block \(k\) must have finished input by \(v_{k-1}-T\). Among the allowed input start times for block \(k\),
\[
\mathcal{T}_k=\{(k-1)L+mP \mid m\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge0}\},
\]
let \(t_k\) be the latest \(\le v_{k-1}-T\). The lag we need to “absorb” with buffering is the backward distance
\[
\delta_k \stackrel{\text{def}}{=}\bigl( (v_{k-1}-T) - t_k \bigr)
            = \bigl((k-1)(C-L)\bigr)\bmod P \;\in [0,P).
\]
(That identity uses \(v_{k-1}-T=(k-1)C\).) With \(N\) buffers the available “window” before compute touches the block is
\[
(v_{k-1}-T)-v_{k-N} = (N-1)C - T,
\]
so feasibility for all \(k\) requires
\[
\delta_k \le (N-1)C - T\quad\text{for }k=2,\dots,n.
\]
Let
\[
\Delta_n \;=\;\max_{1\le j\le n-1}\; \bigl(j(C-L)\bmod P\bigr),
\]
then a necessary and sufficient condition to attain the optimum is
\[
(N-1)C - T \;\ge\; \Delta_n .
\]
Therefore the minimum number of buffers is
\[
\boxed{\,N_{\min}=\left\lceil 1+\frac{T+\Delta_n}{C}\right\rceil\,,\qquad C\ge L,}
\]
with \(\displaystyle \Delta_n=\max_{1\le j\le n-1}\!\bigl(j(C-L)-P\lfloor j(C-L)/P\rfloor\bigr)\).
Remarks.

If \((C-L)/P\) is rational \(=p/q\) in lowest terms and \(n-1\ge q\), then \(\Delta_n=P-P/q\). If \(n\) is smaller, compute the max over \(j=1,\dots,n-1\) explicitly.
If \(C=L\), then \(\Delta_n=0\) and the formula gives \(N_{\min}=\lceil1+T/C\rceil\).

Worked examples (\(L=1,\;P=100,\;T=0.5,\;n=100\))
For each \(C\) we evaluate \(\Delta_{100}=\max_{1\le j\le 99}(j(C-1)\bmod 100)\) and then \(N_{\min}\).
